They interpret punishment both as deterrence (explicitly arguing that deductions should push Sloth toward higher contributions) and as moral retribution (stating they cannot stand greedy people, “no mercy,” and that they are glad to see the free rider hurt). Even when most of the group shifts to a “ignore Sloth, maximize income” stance, they persist with costly punishment, suggesting a stable, strong preference for enforcing fairness over personal earnings. They do not use punishment retaliatorily against people who disagree with them about strategy; it is always directed at contribution behavior, not at opinions, which suggests their enforcement is norm‑based rather than personal or spiteful toward dissent.

They never punish anyone, including the persistent low contributor, despite repeated opportunities and strong social pressure from others to do so. In chat they explicitly state that punishing Sloth is “pointless” and that Sloth “doesn’t care,” implying a belief that punishment is ineffective or not worth the cost. Their focus is on maximizing own and group earnings, and they view spending on punishment as wasteful rather than as a tool for enforcing norms. There is no sign of retaliatory motives or desire to equalize payoffs through negative sanctions. This suggests a strongly non‑punitive, efficiency‑oriented stance: even when they disapprove of free‑riding or find it annoying, they do not respond with costly punishment.

They never use punishment, not even against a persistent under-contributor who is widely targeted by others. They explicitly advocate against deducting from low contributors, arguing that punishment risks spiteful cycles and reduces the overall pot. Their motive is not leniency per se but efficiency: punishing costs coins and may not reliably change behavior, so it is, in their view, not worth it. They show no inclination toward retaliation or expressive punishment; they do not threaten future punishment, and they do not appear swayed when others proudly report large deductions. Even when low contributions trigger frustration in the group, they remain consistent in discouraging punishment and focusing on their own cooperative behavior and earnings.

This player is an extremely heavy and emotionally driven punisher, willing to incur very large personal costs to impose large deductions on others. They use punishment both selectively (targeting single individuals) and collectively (hitting all others at once), and their punishment is not consistently aligned with a clear, stable social-efficiency norm. They punish both low contributors and high contributors at different times, so their trigger is not simply “you contributed too little” or “you free-rode on me.” In some instances they heavily punish someone who contributes a moderate or high amount, suggesting that retaliation or frustration plays a role. They also occasionally punish all peers simultaneously regardless of their relative contributions, which looks more like a generalized expression of anger or dominance than measured deterrence. The intensity of punishment is often far beyond what is needed to alter immediate material incentives; they spend enough to drive targets’ round payoffs close to or below zero, and they absorb large self-costs to do so. This pattern is consistent with strong negative reciprocity or spite: they appear willing to “burn money” just to hurt others. There is no clear evidence that they calibrate punishment to marginal changes in others’ contributions; rather, punishment tends to come in large, discrete bursts. They do not appear to adjust punishment downwards in response to others’ subsequent cooperative behavior in any consistent way. After someone they previously punished later contributes highly, they sometimes still punish them again, and in another case massively sanctions a very high contributor. Thus, deterrence or forward-looking incentive design seems secondary at best; expressive or retaliatory motives dominate.

This player is a selective but emotionally responsive punisher who uses punishment both normatively and personally. They do not punish every low contributor, nor do they consistently punish free riders; instead, they target specific players in specific relational contexts. Once a player is perceived as problematic (e.g., through heavy or erratic punishment of others, or repeated norm violations), this player is willing to punish them even when that target is actually contributing relatively well. This pattern suggests that motives are a mix of norm enforcement and retaliation/character judgment rather than pure contribution-based deterrence. They are willing to pay substantial personal costs to impose punishment when they feel standards have been violated, indicating a strong expressive component. At the same time, they sometimes refrain from punishing in environments where punishment is already very intense or chaotic, suggesting some awareness that additional punishment can be counterproductive. Overall, they use punishment more as a relational signal (“you are in the wrong”) than as a finely calibrated tool to marginally increase future cooperation.

They never spend any coins on punishment, despite being repeatedly targeted by others’ punishment when they free-ride. This indicates either a strong reluctance to incur costs for norm enforcement, or a deliberate belief that punishing is not worthwhile or not their role. They refrain from punishing even obvious low contributors in the group, suggesting they are not a second-order enforcer of cooperation. Because they are often the target of punishment and never retaliate with punishment themselves, there is no evidence of revenge motivation. They absorb sanctions without responding in kind through the punishment channel. Their reactive strategy, if any, occurs only via changing their own contribution behavior, not via sanctioning others. The absence of any punishment outlays also implies they do not attempt to use punishment strategically (e.g., to deter future defection by others or to signal toughness). Deterrence motives thus appear weak or absent. Overall, they fit a “non-punisher” or “punishment-averse” type: willing to accept the group’s sanctions as a cost of their own mixed contribution strategy, but unwilling to pay to shape others’ behavior.

The player is a targeted, low- to moderate-intensity punisher who focuses on clear low contributors and ignores more ambiguous or moderate deviations most of the time. They never punish high contributors. Their punishment is consistently directed at players who contribute very little or nothing when others are contributing much more, especially recurrent low contributors. They sometimes also target a moderate under-contributor when behavior is especially stark relative to the group. They never punish out of apparent retaliation for being punished themselves: even after suffering non-trivial deductions, they do not direct punishment at those whose actions could plausibly have harmed them, and in some such situations they do not punish anyone. This pattern points toward norm-enforcing or inequality-averse motives rather than revenge. The player uses small punishment units and does not escalate across the game; they appear willing to incur some personal cost to discipline free-riders but do not adopt the very aggressive punishment levels that some others in the group use. They also clearly differentiate cases: recurrent complete free-riding or very low giving is punished, sporadic or moderate under-contribution often is not. Overall, they act as a principled but measured “mild altruistic punisher,” enforcing a cooperation norm without pursuing maximal deterrence.

They almost never use punishment. Across the game they invoke it only once, targeting the most salient chronic defector rather than minor deviations. This solitary act looks like a principled sanction of a clear free-rider rather than ongoing retaliation or spite. After that episode, they abstain from punishment entirely, even when they themselves are punished or when other players visibly under-contribute or indulge in large-scale sanctioning. This pattern indicates a very high threshold for punishing. They appear reluctant to engage in costly negative sanctions, perhaps due to a preference to avoid escalating conflict, an aversion to harming others, or a belief that others are already enforcing norms sufficiently. Since the one punitive act aligns with a clear case of low contribution, it looks more like norm-enforcement than revenge. They also do not react with punishment when they become a punishment target. Being punished does not trigger counter-punishment, pointing to low propensity for retaliatory behavior and a relatively non-vengeful orientation.

They are a frequent and relatively heavy punisher, especially toward clear under‑contributors. They target players who contribute nothing or very little when most others contribute a lot, and they do so even when many others also engage in sanctioning. Their punishment is selective: they do not punish moderate deviations consistently and focus on severe or recurring free‑riding and repeated zero contributions. They also direct punishment at players who alternately cooperate and defect in ways that significantly hurt the group, particularly when a player gives nothing while others give high amounts. They are willing to incur substantial personal cost to impose large deductions, demonstrating strong norm‑enforcement preferences that outweigh short‑term earnings. Punishment is not obviously retaliatory in the narrow sense: they do not punish every player who punishes them, and they sometimes continue to punish specific low contributors even after those players have been punished heavily by others, suggesting a focus on contribution behavior rather than on personal grievances. They do not appear to use punishment in a fine‑tuned, marginal way; instead, they sometimes choose relatively large sanction levels, consistent with a “tough enforcer” or “altruistic punisher” style aiming for deterrence and moral satisfaction more than precise cost‑benefit optimization.

This player is highly willing to punish and accepts very large personal costs to do so. Early on, they target a clear low contributor with strong sanctions, consistent with norm-enforcing punishment. However, their later punishment becomes broader and less tightly tied to observed contribution levels. They sometimes target fully cooperative or near-fully cooperative players with substantial punishment, even in otherwise cooperative environments. This indicates that their punishment is not purely contribution-based. Possible triggers (all low-confidence) include: perceived earlier targeting of themselves, irritation at specific players’ prior punishment or reward behavior, or a desire to equalize earnings among those who have done very well. Since punishers are anonymous, these attributions cannot be confirmed; the data only show that their punishment is not reserved for low contributors. At one point, they punish nearly the entire group very heavily at extreme personal cost, while contributing nothing. This looks more like a “grim” or “scorched earth” move than calibrated deterrence: it dramatically lowers everyone’s payoff including their own, and there is no future round in which deterrence could benefit them meaningfully. This supports an interpretation of punishment as expressive, retaliatory, or driven by negative emotions, rather than purely strategic. Selectivity is thus mixed: they can be surgical (targeting select individuals) but also indiscriminate (hitting almost everyone). Deterrence motives seem weak compared to expressive ones; they do not consistently use small, cost-effective penalties to shape behavior. Instead, they sometimes overspend on punishment, indicating either limited concern for payoff efficiency or strong non-monetary motives.

They are a selective but willing punisher, clearly using punishment as a tool to enforce contribution norms rather than at random. They repeatedly target players who contribute very little or nothing to the public fund, especially when the rest of the group is contributing high amounts. They focus strongly on specific low contributors: one player who frequently gives nothing, and another who occasionally gives nothing amid high group contributions. They do not punish moderate under-contributors consistently, but they do escalate when someone moves from contributing a little to contributing nothing, or when a habitual free-rider reappears after periods of cooperation. Their punishment is often multi-unit and therefore costly, particularly against repeat offenders. They sometimes punish the same individual multiple times across the game, including in the final round, suggesting a desire to sanction norm violators even when there is minimal future benefit from deterrence. They do not appear to use punishment in obvious retaliation for being punished themselves; after they are targeted by others’ punishment, their own targeting pattern continues to follow others’ contribution levels rather than becoming indiscriminate. This points toward “altruistic enforcer” motives: they accept personal cost to discipline free-riders and protect cooperative norms, even when they themselves are not guaranteed a payoff advantage from doing so. They almost never punish high contributors or consistent cooperators, even when those players engage heavily in punishment or reward toward others. This indicates attention to fairness rather than spite: punishment is reserved for those clearly taking more than their fair share from the group project.

They never spend on punishment, even when clear low contributors and aggressive punishers are present, and even when they themselves are repeatedly targeted. This indicates a strong aversion to using costly sanctions. They do not engage in second-order punishment of free-riders, nor do they retaliate against those who appear to be driving deduction storms in later rounds. The absence of any punishment use across cooperative, mixed, and chaotic episodes suggests that this is not just a narrow cost-benefit calculation in specific instances, but a consistent disposition or strategy: they avoid harming others, even when it might be emotionally or strategically justified. They therefore do not act as an enforcer of contribution norms and are unlikely to rely on punishment as a deterrence tool in new environments.

This player never uses costly punishment despite repeated exposure to clear free riding. Their inaction holds both when non‑contributors are a minority in a mostly cooperative group and when multiple peers with a history of non‑contribution are present. This points to a strong reluctance to harm others, even when the technology of punishment is relatively efficient and targets are obvious. There is no evidence of deterrence‑oriented behavior or of retaliatory motives; they do not punish after being personally targeted with punishment, nor do they escalate in later periods. Their approach is best characterized as “non‑punishing cooperatism”: they enforce norms, if at all, only through positive reinforcement, not through sanctions. Whether this stems from moral aversion, perceived inefficacy, or risk aversion about provoking counter‑punishment is unknown.

They are generally reluctant to use punishment and employ it only once, in a targeted and substantial way against a clear, repeated low contributor. This indicates a high threshold for punitive action and a focus on norm enforcement rather than emotional retaliation or broad aggression. When they do punish, they concentrate the sanction on a single prominent defector rather than spreading small punishments across multiple people, suggesting an intention to make a salient statement and possibly deter future free-riding. They do not subsequently continue punishing once the target behaves cooperatively, consistent with a forward-looking deterrence motive and a willingness to “reset” once compliance is observed. Overall, they appear to view punishment as an exceptional tool reserved for conspicuous and repeated deviations from what they treat as a strong cooperation norm.

This player is clearly willing to punish and uses punishment selectively and repeatedly. They focus almost entirely on a single, consistently under-contributing individual and once on another defector. Punishment is directed at clear deviations from a high-contribution norm, not at minor or ambiguous behavior, suggesting a norm-enforcement motive rather than opportunistic aggression. They employ both low-intensity and high-intensity punishment against a main target. The escalation from mild to strong sanctions indicates an attempt at deterrence when initial signals fail. When the main target later conforms by fully contributing, the player suspends punishment, which is consistent with a conditional, compliance-seeking use of sanctions rather than pure retaliation. They also apply a one-off punishment to another player who defects in a setting where almost everyone else cooperates. This is consistent with upholding a general “everyone should contribute” rule rather than singling out only one rival. They are willing to bear substantial costs to punish, even when it clearly reduces their own monetary payoff. This points to strong inequity aversion or norm-enforcement preferences. There is some evidence of persistence: they return to punishing the main low contributor later after that player resumes free-riding, implying they track others’ past actions and react to repeated deviations. There is no evidence of spiteful punishment of cooperators or of punishment as revenge for being punished by others; their targets are always clear free-riders. Overall, they act as a principled enforcer of contribution norms, with punishment intensity roughly proportional to the perceived seriousness or repetition of defection.

This player never uses the punishment option despite repeated and clear opportunities and despite observing several low or zero contributors across different rounds. Their abstention holds even when their own cooperative behavior is met with at least one instance of punishment from another player. This pattern suggests a strong reluctance to spend resources on harming others, even when such punishment is relatively cost-effective and might deter free-riding. There is no evidence they target the worst offenders selectively, nor that they ever use punishment as retaliation or as a normative sanction. From observed behavior alone, the most grounded interpretation is that they either (a) prioritize keeping their own payoff and the group pot intact over engaging in costly sanctions, or (b) hold a non-punitive social norm, avoiding negative actions even when they are individually rational in a narrow sense. Their type is best described as “non-punisher by choice,” with unknown beliefs about whether punishment is effective as deterrence, because they never test it.

They never use punishment, even when clear free-riders and mixed contributors are present and punishment is relatively efficient. This strongly suggests an aversion to spending resources to harm others, even when such punishment could plausibly support norms or deter defection. The absence of any selective targeting indicates that they do not differentiate between defectors and cooperators in their use of sanctions; they simply opt out of punishment altogether. This is consistent with a strategy that prioritizes maximizing one’s own payoff and/or avoiding antagonism, rather than actively shaping others’ behavior via deterrence. Given that they themselves are punished once after deviating, yet still do not punish in any later period, there is no evidence of retaliatory motives. They do not appear to adopt a “second-order enforcement” role (no moralistic punishment of free-riders) and do not engage in strategic punishment to stabilize cooperation. Their type is best characterized as “non-punishing,” with low apparent aggression and low willingness to incur costs to enforce norms.

The player uses punishment sparingly and in a targeted way. They only punish when a clear defector stands out in a context where many others are cooperating. This indicates that punishment is triggered by salient norm violations rather than by personal retaliation or general frustration. They do not engage in broad or repeated punishment campaigns, nor do they punish every defector they encounter. This selectivity suggests they prioritize impactful, visible sanctions—possibly as a deterrent signal—over systematic norm enforcement. Their willingness to incur a personal cost for a punishment with relatively high effectiveness indicates some willingness to pay to protect the cooperative standard, but not at the level of a dedicated “moral enforcer.” There is no evidence that they punish in response to being punished themselves, so retaliatory motives are unknown but likely not dominant. Their limited use of punishment, even after personally being targeted, suggests they are not vindictive. Instead, punishment appears instrumental: it is used to discipline conspicuous non-cooperation in hopes of sustaining a mostly cooperative environment.

They are a willing and relatively heavy user of punishment, but only in response to clear under-contribution. They target those who give nothing, and they escalate sanctions for repeat or persistent low contributors. Their pattern shows selectivity: they do not punish randomly nor spread punishment widely, but focus on a small set of consistent free riders. The use of multiple punishment units on the same individuals indicates an intent to deter or discipline, not just to express mild disapproval. They do not appear to punish moderate deviations or past low contributors who return to full cooperation, suggesting they track current behavior and are at least somewhat forgiving when norms are met again. There is no evidence of retaliatory punishment or spite, as they are never punished and show no signs of lashing out beyond norm enforcement. Overall, they behave like a principled altruistic punisher: willing to sacrifice substantial personal payoff to enforce a cooperation norm and to target chronic defectors more strongly over time.

The player never uses the punishment option, despite experiencing costly punishment from others when they defect. This consistent non‑use across cooperative and defective periods indicates a stable type with respect to sanctions: they are a non‑punisher. Their refusal to punish holds even when others’ behavior creates clear efficiency losses and payoff inequities, and even after they are personally targeted with substantial deductions. This pattern suggests that either (a) they place high weight on avoiding the additional cost of punishment (payoff‑conserving), (b) they dislike engaging in negative sanctions on principle, or (c) they see punishment as ineffective or not worth the trouble. The transcript does not reveal which of these motives is primary. They also do not use punishment as retaliation, even after being singled out multiple times. This indicates low vengefulness and little interest in anonymous retributive justice. They make no attempt to deter others’ free‑riding or to reciprocate being punished. Overall, they are a “non‑sanctioner” with respect to losses: they absorb disadvantageous outcomes rather than paying to alter others’ payoffs.

They never use the punishment mechanism, despite multiple clear opportunities where another player free-rides or where one peer uses punishment heavily against another. This restraint holds even when another player’s defection directly reduces their own payoff. This suggests either a principled avoidance of costly punishment or a purely payoff-maximizing expectation that punishment here is inefficient for them. Since they do not punish even egregious or repeated free-riding, and even when others are bearing large costs to punish, they appear disinclined to enforce norms through negative sanctions. There is no evidence of retaliatory motives, spite, or attempts at deterrence via punishment. They behave as a non-punisher type: willing to keep contributing, but not willing to spend personal resources to directly discipline defectors.

This player is a moderately frequent, selective punisher who appears focused on sanctioning perceived free-riders or below-norm contributors, rather than engaging in indiscriminate or antisocial punishment. They do not punish every time low contributions occur, but they do repeatedly incur costs to punish in many rounds, including when it reduces their own payoff substantially. Their punishment decisions are often aligned with others who target clear free-riders, indicating a shared norm of “contributors deserve more, non-contributors deserve punishment.” They avoid punishing clear high contributors and do not show evidence of retaliating against high contributors who may have punished them indirectly; they cannot identify specific punishers and do not behave as if they are punishing at random. The size of their punishment is typically modest to moderate, suggesting intent to signal and enforce norms rather than to destroy others’ payoffs at all cost. They sometimes punish even when most others are cooperating, but targets still look consistent with contribution-based criteria, not payoff envy. Overall, they are a “norm-enforcing cooperative punisher,” more driven by contribution levels than by pure material payoffs, and not clearly motivated by spite.

This player is extremely reluctant to punish; for most of the game they do not spend on punishment at all, even when they are themselves heavily punished or when others are evidently free-riding. Only once do they actually use the punishment option, and then at a modest level rather than aggressively. This lone episode indicates that they understand and are willing to use the mechanism but treat it as a rare, exceptional response rather than a regular tool. Because punishment is so rare, it is hard to infer detailed triggers with high confidence. The one instance occurs in a mixed-contribution context, suggesting a possible response to clear low contributors or to perceived chronic offenders, but this is low confidence. There is no observable pattern of targeted retaliation: they suffer substantial deductions in several rounds yet almost never respond by punishing in the immediately following opportunities. Their overall style is non-enforcing and non-vindictive. They do not appear motivated by norm-policing or by emotional “revenge.” Deterrence is at best a weak secondary motive; they prioritize conserving their own payoff over spending to change others’ behavior.

The player never spends any coins on punishment at any point. This holds despite frequent and sometimes extremely heavy use of punishment by others, including punishment targeted at them. Their refusal to punish appears robust to changes in contribution patterns of others, the intensity of others’ punitive behavior, and their own payoff position. They therefore do not act as a norm enforcer, second‑order punisher, or retaliator. This suggests either a strong cost‑aversion to spending on punishment, an instrumental focus on maximizing personal payoffs regardless of others’ behavior, or a belief that their punishment would not be effective or worthwhile. There is no evidence that they use punishment as a deterrent, a signal, or revenge; their stance is non‑punitive and non‑interventionist.

This player is a frequent and relatively heavy user of punishment, especially in the early and middle portions of the game. They repeatedly spend non-trivial amounts to punish others, indicating a willingness to sacrifice own payoffs to influence group behavior. Given the contribution structure, the most plausible primary trigger is non-cooperation or perceived exploitation: they sometimes contribute while others do not, yet they keep paying to punish, which is consistent with sanctioning free-riders. Since punishers are anonymous, they cannot credibly signal identity, so the main functions of their punishment are likely deterrence and norm enforcement rather than reputation-building. Their punishment is not obviously surgical or minimal; they sometimes spend enough to make their own payoff meaningfully lower than it would otherwise be, even in rounds where they themselves are not fully cooperative. This suggests a somewhat “moralistic but inconsistent” type: willing to punish deviations from what they see as appropriate behavior, but not strictly constrained by their own contribution level. They do not appear to use punishment for simple retaliation against personal losses, because they continue to punish in contexts where everyone is subject to substantial, diffuse punishment and where their own payoff trajectory is relatively good overall. There is no clear evidence of targeted vendettas against specific individuals, given the anonymity and aggregate data. Over time, they temper punishment: in later rounds they either reduce or fully stop punishing, consistent with declining belief that further sanctions will change behavior or be worth the cost near the end.

The player is a frequent, relatively heavy user of punishment. They spend on punishment in a large majority of rounds, sometimes at substantial cost to themselves, indicating high willingness to sanction others. The pattern of when they punish is consistent with norm-enforcement more than random aggression: they tend to punish in environments where some players contribute less than others or where there is clear free-riding, and they continue to punish even when they personally defect. That is, they behave like a “moralist” who both defects and punishes other defectors, rather than like a pure cooperative punisher. Their spending levels are non-trivial but not maximal; they rarely approach the extreme over-punishment seen in some other players, which implies some concern for cost-effectiveness and deterrence, not simply spite. They also continue to punish even after being targeted themselves, but do not escalate to ruinous levels, suggesting that retaliation is likely part of their motivation, yet it is tempered. Because punishers are anonymous, any responsive pattern to specific individuals is uncertain, but the persistence and costliness of punishment across many different contribution environments indicate that they value enforcing contribution norms and are willing to pay to reduce others’ payoffs when they see deviation, even when this does not obviously improve their own monetary outcome.

Punishment is central to this player’s type. They spend very large amounts on punishment in multiple rounds, at times sacrificing most or all of their own payoff and even driving themselves into negative earnings within a round. This reveals very low concern for immediate material self‑interest when enforcing their norms. They are clearly willing to use costly punishment repeatedly, and at scales well above what is needed for marginal deterrence, consistent with strong emotional or principle‑driven motives. Their punishment is not transparently selective in the transcript, because targets are anonymized. However, its persistence across both cooperative and defecting rounds indicates that it is not simply a “reciprocal” response to how others treated them in that specific round. They continue to punish even when they are contributing fully and also when they withhold contributions. This suggests that they may be targeting perceived chronic free‑riders, second‑order free‑riders (those who do not punish), or particular individuals they infer as unfair, rather than conditioning punishment strictly on their own current contribution choice. Because they are sometimes the only or one of few players punishing at high intensity, they appear willing to shoulder a disproportionate enforcement burden. This aligns more with a spiteful enforcer or moralist who is prepared to incur heavy personal cost to reduce others’ payoffs, than with a purely efficiency‑minded sanctioner. Deterrence may still be a goal, but the scale and repetition of punishment, including against players who already receive large deductions from the group, indicates that expressive or retaliatory motives likely play a substantial role. Overall, the player is an extremely aggressive punisher, with low selectivity and high willingness to burn personal resources to harm norm violators or disliked others.

The player is a relatively restrained but non-passive punisher. For much of the game, they either do not punish at all or spend only small amounts, despite repeatedly being a low contributor themselves and being punished by others. This suggests they do not engage in large-scale retaliation against the group for targeting them, nor do they attempt to “fight back” proportionally to the deductions they receive. When they do punish, their expenditures are moderate relative to some of the most extreme punishers in the group, and are concentrated in a few rounds rather than spread evenly, implying selective rather than systematic use of punishment. Because punisher identities are hidden, their targets and motives cannot be inferred with certainty; their own low contributions make norm-enforcing “altruistic punishment” of free riders logically inconsistent with their behavior, so any punishment they give is more likely driven by perceived personal threat or frustration than by a strong pro-cooperation norm. They occasionally invest a noticeable amount in punishment while remaining a free rider, which is consistent with a “spiteful free rider” or “defensive punisher” type: willing to incur some cost to reduce others’ payoffs but not willing to contribute to the public good themselves. However, their levels of punishment never reach the extreme self-destructive levels seen in some peers, suggesting some restraint and some attention to preserving their own payoff.

The player is generally willing to use punishment, but not frequently; when they do, it is often intense and apparently driven by resentment rather than calibrated norm enforcement. Across most of the game they refrain from punishing at all, even while being repeatedly and heavily punished themselves. When they do spend on punishment, it is concentrated in a few episodes, including one early‑mid episode with moderate spending and one final episode with very high spending. This timing—after they have been a persistent target of sanctions—suggests that their punishment is likely retaliatory or expressive (“lashing out”) rather than forward‑looking deterrence. They do not seem to systematically reward high contributors (which they cannot directly anyway) or consistently sanction low contributors; in fact, they themselves are often the lowest contributor while others keep punishing them. Because punisher identities are hidden, they cannot target specific known aggressors, so any retaliation is necessarily diffuse and likely aimed at multiple players. The willingness to spend far more on punishment than they can recoup in payoff indicates that they are prepared to incur large costs to harm others once sufficiently provoked. At the same time, the long stretches with no punishment despite ongoing sanctions show that they are not intrinsically “punishment‑seeking” every round; instead, they require a build‑up of negative experience before they act harshly.

They are willing to use costly punishment but do so intermittently and at relatively modest levels compared to some extreme punishers in the group. Early on they refrain from punishing even severe free‑riders, indicating either initial restraint, uncertainty, or an attempt to avoid escalation. Later they do begin spending on punishment, but not in a systematic way that tightly tracks contribution levels. They sometimes punish in rounds where non‑contributors are present, sometimes abstain in similar environments, and sometimes punish when most others are cooperating. This pattern suggests that their punishment is not a precise conditional sanctioning scheme keyed only to free‑riding. Their own experience of being punished does not reliably trigger retaliation against specific behavioral types (e.g., low contributors vs. high contributors); instead, they maintain full cooperation and only modestly adjust punishment intensity. They do not respond with increasingly heavy sanctions even after being targeted multiple times, which points away from a strongly spiteful or revenge‑oriented type. Overall, they appear as a light, somewhat erratic punisher: willing to incur costs to sanction but not obsessed with tracking or “correcting” every deviation, and not escalating into vendetta‑like behavior. Whether they aim primarily at norm enforcement, emotional venting, or hazy reciprocity is uncertain; the lack of clear targeting suggests low strategic precision and possibly low confidence or low attention to fine‑grained payoff patterns.

They do use punishment, but in a sparse and apparently unsystematic way. Sometimes they spend on punishment; other times, in similar contribution environments, they do not. Because punisher identities are hidden, their precise targets are unknown, and no clear rule-based pattern can be inferred (e.g., consistently targeting low contributors, high contributors, or particular individuals). Their punishment usage does not correlate with changes in their own contribution (which stays fixed at zero), suggesting it is not part of a reciprocity or conditional-cooperation tactic. It is also not clear that it functions as successful deterrence; their own behavior does not change, and they keep being punished by others. Motives are therefore ambiguous: possibilities include occasional retaliation for being punished or sporadic attempts to influence others’ behavior, but the evidence for either is weak. Overall they are a low-intensity, low-structure punisher with unclear targeting logic and low demonstrated concern for the welfare cost of punishment to the group.

This player almost never uses punishment, and when they do, it is limited and atypical of a harsh enforcer. The one clear instance of punishment targets a high contributor rather than a clear free rider, suggesting their punishing decision is not driven by simple contribution level or standard antisocial-punishment avoidance logic. The motivation is ambiguous: it could reflect misinterpretation, a mistaken click, or a test of the mechanism. Given they consistently avoid punishing even very low contributors before and after, their general type is best described as extremely punishment-averse. They show no pattern of retaliatory punishment: when they themselves are punished, they do not respond by punishing that actor or anyone else, indicating low inclination toward revenge. They also never escalate punishment in response to declining cooperation or to being monetarily harmed. Overall, deterrence does not seem to be their chosen tool; they prefer to shape behavior with rewards rather than with sanctions. Because punishment is so rare and not clearly aligned with observable defection, their punishment rule is unknown and likely weakly defined. If they punish at all, it is with very low intensity and low consistency, and punishment does not form a central pillar of their strategy.

This player is a frequent, sometimes extreme punisher who uses punishment in both targeted and vendetta‑like ways. They clearly accept the cost of punishing as part of their behavioral toolkit and are willing to spend substantial resources to reduce specific others’ payoffs. They do not confine punishment to clear low contributors: they repeatedly punish players who are actually contributing at or near the top of the group distribution. Their punishment pattern toward a small subset of players is persistent and disproportionate to any observable contribution differences. This supports an interpretation of personal grievance or retaliation, rather than normative enforcement of contribution levels. Once a target relationship becomes conflictual, they escalate sanctions rather than de‑escalate, even at high personal cost. This is consistent with a strong taste for reprisal and status assertion: they seem to prefer re‑imposing losses on perceived adversaries over maximizing their own earnings. They also occasionally punish medium or fluctuating contributors, and mix this with reward in other periods, indicating that their criteria are not purely contribution‑based. Their use of punishment does not obviously track efficiency (they sometimes punish in already high‑cooperation environments), suggesting that deterrence of free‑riding is not the primary goal. Instead, their punishment is best described as expressive and relational: they use it to mark disapproval, retaliate against perceived slights, and possibly to test or pressure others, rather than to stabilize group cooperation.

This player never uses punishment, even when clear free-riding or sharp contribution drops occur and punishment is relatively efficient. They also do not switch to punishment when they themselves are targeted by others. This indicates either an internal aversion to imposing losses on others, a belief that punishment is socially destructive or counterproductive, or a strong preference for positive over negative enforcement. They do not selectively punish marginal or severe defectors; the absence of punishment appears categorical rather than strategic. There is no sign they use punishment as retaliation, deterrence, or norm enforcement. Their type is best described as a non-punishing cooperator who tolerates exploitation rather than resort to sanctions. Underlying motives are uncertain, but a preference for maintaining a non-hostile environment is plausible (low to moderate confidence).

The player is an active and sometimes intense norm enforcer who uses punishment selectively but forcefully. They mainly target clear low contributors or defectors and ignore minor fluctuations among generally high contributors. They do not punish everyone below the maximum; instead, they focus on the most salient norm violators at a given time. This shows selectivity rather than blanket spite. Their punishment escalates when another player repeatedly contributes very little, even if that player later raises contributions somewhat but continues to lag the group. This pattern fits a deterrence and discipline motive: they appear to try to shape that player’s behavior toward a higher contribution norm rather than merely retaliate once. Their willingness to invest costly punishment repeatedly against the same target, even when it is expensive relative to their endowment, points to strong commitment to enforcing cooperation norms, possibly at the expense of their own earnings. They also punish occasional one-off or late free-riding by players who had previously cooperated well, implying that “falling to zero” or strikingly low contributions crosses a threshold that justifies sanction, regardless of prior good behavior. They do not use “antisocial punishment” against high contributors; punishment is always directed at relative under-contributors or complete non-contributors. Overall, they behave like a principled altruistic punisher: they accept personal cost to sanction free-riding and maintain a high-cooperation environment.

The player very rarely punishes and almost never uses it in a calibrated, targeted way. Most of the time they refrain from punishment entirely, even when others clearly contribute less. This indicates a general reluctance to spend resources on harming others or enforcing contribution norms through sanctions. On one notable occasion, they deploy punishment broadly and non-selectively across many group members, including some who are not obvious low contributors. This mass, scattershot punishment is inconsistent with standard norm-enforcement (which would focus on clear free-riders) and looks more like an expressive act: possibly frustration, retaliation against perceived prior targeting, or an attempt to shake up or “reset” the group. Because the punished set includes both high and low contributors, this use of punishment appears non-instructional and is unlikely to be a straightforward deterrence strategy. Outside of that episode, the complete absence of punishment despite clear opportunities suggests they do not prefer ongoing sanctioning as a tool of norm maintenance. Punishment, when they do use it, seems episodic, emotional, and undifferentiated rather than strategic, selective, or conditional on specific deviations. Motivationally, their punishment profile fits a type who avoids harming others, but under high irritation or perceived unfairness can engage in a one-off, symbolic punitive burst. They do not show any sustained pattern of retaliating against particular players, nor do they repeatedly punish free-riders, indicating low commitment to punishment as a stable enforcement mechanism.

They never spend any coins on punishment, despite having many opportunities and despite others using punishment heavily. Their behavior is consistent zero-punishment across cooperative and defective environments. This strongly suggests an “anti-punishment” or at least “punishment-averse” type. They appear unwilling to incur personal costs to enforce norms, even when they themselves are the target of deductions and when others plainly defect. They neither retaliate against those who presumably punished them nor try to deter low contributors when punishment could be effective. Given that they themselves sometimes free-ride, it is unclear whether their refusal to punish is principled (e.g., dislike of punitive mechanisms) or simply a consistent payoff-maximizing strategy that avoids the cost of sanctioning. The data slightly favors a cost- and effort-avoidance interpretation: they ignore the mechanism entirely rather than making any discriminating use of it. They show no evidence of second-order enforcement (punishing punishers) or of using punishment strategically (e.g., to shape others’ contributions near the end). Their type is best characterized as “non-punishing even when personally harmed,” with unknown underlying moral motivation.

They are willing to engage in substantial, costly punishment, but only in clearly norm-violating cases and not continuously. When one player fully free-rides after unanimous cooperation, they impose very heavy punishment on that target, accepting a large personal cost. This indicates a strong inequity-aversion or norm-enforcement motive that can override earnings maximization. Later, when a previously cooperative player withholds contribution once, they impose moderate punishment on that player. This suggests that their trigger is any clear, unambiguous deviation from a well-established “everyone-maxes” norm, regardless of that player’s previous good record. They never punish cooperators, and they do not punish minor or ambiguous issues like chat behavior. There is no evidence of retaliation against those who deduct from them; they explicitly express confusion when deducted despite having cooperated, and they attribute such deductions to the system or to the experiment’s design rather than seeking revenge. Their punishment style is thus principled and norm-based rather than spiteful. They also seem unconcerned with whether the punished party is “real” or a bot; they later regret having spent a lot punishing a likely non-human but frame this as annoyance about wasted effort, not moral concern.

They never use punishment, despite having many opportunities and seeing others punished heavily, including clear free‑riders. This consistent non‑use suggests a strong aversion to costly punishment, or at least no intrinsic taste for norm enforcement via sanctions. There is no evidence they try to deter defection or retaliate when they themselves are deducted; they continue to cooperate and do not complain or threaten in chat. They also do not appear to experiment with tiny punishments as a signal. Taken together, this indicates a “non‑punishing cooperator” type: they rely on positive coordination and group norms rather than sanctions, and are willing to let others handle enforcement if it happens. Their implicit strategy seems to be: keep contributing and avoid wasting coins on conflict, even if others misbehave.

They are an extremely heavy and erratic punisher, willing to spend large amounts on deductions even when everyone is fully cooperating. Punishment is not consistently aligned with contribution behavior: they sometimes hit full cooperators harshly, including those who are positively engaged in chat, and they also punish when there is no contribution deviation at all. Targets shift over time across many different group members, and the intensity varies from light to very heavy, often without any visible behavioral trigger in contributions. They do not use punishment in a straightforward deterrence role (e.g., punishing low contributors to restore norms) and do not follow a clear reciprocity pattern (e.g., focusing on those who previously punished or failed to reward them). In many cases, they spend substantial amounts on punishment in situations where it clearly reduces their own payoff without improving group cooperation (which is already maximal). This points toward a type that is either exploring the punishment space, testing its impact, or acting in a payoff‑insensitive, possibly spiteful or chaotic way rather than enforcing a stable fairness norm. Their willingness to punish is high; their selectivity is low and sometimes appears arbitrary. They also sometimes refrain from any punishment even when deviations occur, showing that they are not mechanically punitive but intermittently aggressive in ways that others cannot easily predict.

This player is normally restraint-oriented about punishment but capable of extreme, targeted sanctioning when they perceive a serious norm violation or personal grievance. For most rounds, they entirely abstain from using punishment, even when clear free-riding or contribution inequality is present. This baseline suggests they dislike routine punitive spending and prefer to conserve resources rather than continually enforcing norms. However, when they do punish, they do so in a single, very strong burst directed at one specific individual. The punished target has previously contributed generously, and the focal player has earlier been punished by someone. Given punishers are identifiable, the large, one-shot punishment most plausibly reflects retaliation or moralistic protest against a perceived injustice rather than a fine-tuned, efficiency-oriented sanction on free-riding. The magnitude of the punishment (large cost to self, even larger deduction to the target) indicates they are occasionally willing to sacrifice significant personal payoff to express disapproval or to “hit back” once, even if it is not economically efficient. They do not follow up with further sanctions afterward, nor do they develop a general punitive pattern. This suggests punishment for them is episodic, emotionally or fairness-triggered, and not part of a continuous deterrence strategy. They are selective: most of the time they tolerate inequality, but if they feel personally wronged or singled out, they are capable of intense, retaliatory action.

They are willing to punish but only rarely, and their use of punishment is not obviously tied to simple low-contribution targeting. They direct costly punishment even at a peer who is relatively cooperative, implying that their motives are not straightforward sanctioning of free-riding. The pattern is too sparse to infer a clear deterrence strategy; instead, the punishment episodes look more like emotional or idiosyncratic reactions to relative behavior or to perceived norm violations that are not captured just by contribution size. After these early instances, they stop using punishment altogether, which may reflect learning that punishing is risky, unproductive, or socially destabilizing. Overall, they are capable of antisocial or at least non-standard punishment, but this is not a persistent feature of their play.

They consistently refrain from using punishment, even when others clearly contribute less than their own level and when punishment is relatively effective. They do not punish even after experiencing free-riding by peers or after being personally punished. This indicates a strong reluctance to incur personal cost to harm others, even for deterrence or norm-enforcement reasons. The data do not show any selective or targeted use of punishment, so the safest reading is that they are a “non-punisher” type under these conditions. Motivations are uncertain, but plausible interpretations include: aversion to conflict, skepticism about the efficiency of punishment, or a preference for maintaining earnings and avoiding interpersonal escalation. There is no evidence of retaliatory punishment or of principled punishment of free-riders; both are best characterized as unknown but behaviorally absent in this setting.

The player is generally reluctant to use punishment and does so only once, in a targeted way, against clear low contributors. This indicates a threshold-based willingness to sanction: they are tolerant of moderate deviations but respond when others contribute very little or nothing relative to what they see as reasonable. When they do punish, they select specific defectors rather than punishing broadly or emotionally, suggesting norm-enforcement rather than indiscriminate hostility. The fact that punishment is infrequent, despite multiple opportunities, points to a cost-conscious and selective punishment style. Their likely motive is deterrence and expression of disapproval toward blatant free-riding, not ongoing vendettas. They do not use punishment to retaliate against whoever punished them earlier, even though punishers are identifiable, which further underscores a focus on contribution norms rather than personal revenge.

They never use punishment, despite being exposed to multiple opportunities where others contribute substantially more or less than they do. This consistent non-use indicates a strong reluctance to spend on sanctioning, even though punishment is relatively efficient in terms of impact per coin spent. Their inaction toward low contributors implies low inclination to enforce norms or deter free-riding at a personal cost. Their inaction toward high contributors implies they are also not using antisocial punishment. The absence of any punishment use makes it impossible to distinguish precisely between “principled non-punisher” and “pure payoff-maximizer,” but it strongly supports that they prioritize conserving their own coins over influencing others’ behavior through costly sanctions.

The player never uses the punishment mechanism, despite repeated exposure to clear cost–impact information and multiple opportunities where some peers contribute noticeably less than others. This consistent non-use suggests a general aversion to costly sanctioning, rather than a lack of awareness. Because they also free-ride at least once, their non-punishing stance is unlikely to be driven by a strong norm-enforcing identity. More plausibly, they see punishment as inefficient or not personally worthwhile: the cost to them may not justify the social benefit of deterring low contributors. The presence of substantial under-contribution on some rounds without any punitive response supports an interpretation of this player as “non-punitive,” either from a payoff-maximizing or a conflict-avoidant perspective. Motivationally, deterrence does not appear to be an active tool in their strategy; they neither attempt to correct defectors nor to retaliate for low contributions. Their approach is to adjust their own contribution level rather than to manipulate others via punishment.

This player has a generally low baseline for using punishment, but they are not categorically opposed to it. Their punishment use is infrequent and concentrated in a small number of instances, suggesting that they reserve it for situations they interpret as clear norm violations or as strategically important moments. When they do punish, they are willing to spend a nontrivial amount of their own payoff to do so, indicating that they value norm enforcement enough to incur private costs. The timing and magnitude of their more intense punishment episode strongly suggest targeted sanctioning rather than random or exploratory use of the mechanism. The most plausible trigger is others’ consistently low or zero contributions, i.e., free-riding. This fits a profile of a second-order conditional cooperator: they prefer coordinated cooperation and selectively sanction those they see as undermining it. Their pattern does not indicate habitual or spiteful punishment. Across many opportunities to sanction under-contributors, they mostly refrain. This restraint suggests they either doubt the effectiveness of small-scale punishment in this environment, or they seek to avoid escalation and retaliation costs. When they do choose to punish heavily, it likely reflects a tipping point where they perceive that mere cooperative example-setting is insufficient to sustain contributions from others. The available behavior is insufficient to cleanly separate deterrence-oriented from retaliation-oriented motives, but two observations are notable: (a) they sometimes punish while still contributing nontrivially, consistent with trying to repair norms rather than abandon them; and (b) they do not systematically increase punishment after being punished themselves, which weakens the case for pure revenge motives. Tentative inference: they lean more toward norm-enforcement/deterrence than retaliation, but confidence is limited.

This player is very reluctant to punish. Across the interaction, they almost never spend on punishment, even though low contributors are consistently visible and punishment is cheap and effective. This indicates low willingness to engage in costly sanctioning as a routine norm-enforcement tool. Their single observed use of punishment comes only near the very end of the game and at a small scale. Because we cannot see whom they target, the motive is uncertain. The timing and rarity suggest that they do not view themselves as a proactive enforcer of cooperation. They tolerate free-riding for many rounds without using the available mechanism, even in the presence of chronic very low contributors, implying either a high threshold before acting, a preference to avoid interpersonal conflict, or a belief that sanctioning is not worth the cost. Retaliation motives are possible but not demonstrable. They are punished multiple times earlier, including when they are contributing highly, yet they do not respond with a clear counter-punishment spree later. The delayed and limited use of punishment does not align with hot retaliation; if there is any retaliatory motive, it is muted and delayed or directed specifically at a salient low contributor rather than at prior punishers. Because punisher identities are not shown in the transcript, whether they specifically target past punishers is unknown. Deterrence motives also remain uncertain. Their punitive action is small in magnitude and late, which reduces its potential to shape others’ behavior across many subsequent rounds. That timing is not consistent with a player whose main aim is to discipline defectors early to build cooperation. Selectivity is unknown: we cannot see the specific targets. However, given they almost never punish despite frequent free-riding, any punishment they do deliver is plausibly highly selective and driven by particularly salient deviations or accumulated annoyance rather than by a general principle of strict norm enforcement. In personality terms, this player appears peaceable and sanction-averse: they prioritize their own contribution decisions over managing others’ behavior and only rarely resort to punishment, if at all, as a secondary tool.

This player is a heavy, proactive punisher who is clearly willing to incur substantial personal costs to discipline others. Over many rounds they spend nontrivial amounts on punishment, sometimes at levels that materially reduce their own payoff. This indicates a strong willingness to enforce norms, even when doing so is individually inefficient. Their punishment appears to be primarily directed at low contributors and consistent free riders. The timing of their punishment spending aligns strongly with the presence of conspicuously low or zero contributors in the group. This suggests that their main trigger is perceived unfairness or exploitation of the public good, not random aggression. They behave like a norm enforcer: when others contribute reasonably, they can refrain from punishing; when others free ride or contribute well below what seems to be the emerging standard, they escalate punishment. At some points they punish aggressively even when their own contribution is modest. That pattern is characteristic of a “moralistic free rider” tendency: they sometimes contribute less than many others but still spend heavily to punish those they judge as even more antisocial. This indicates that their punishment norm is relative — they target those who are “worse” than themselves and/or below the perceived group standard, rather than those who simply differ from them. They show no clear evidence of antisocial punishment of high contributors; when they are punished in rounds where they have contributed relatively high amounts, they do not respond by punishing apparent high contributors in subsequent opportunities. This increases confidence that their main motive is norm enforcement or retaliation against low contributors, not envy or hostility toward prosocial players. The size and persistence of their punishment expenditures suggest that they likely believe punishment can shape behavior — i.e., they seem to be using it as a deterrence tool. However, in some instances they punish even when they are already earning decent payoffs and face no obvious short-term benefit from further norm enforcement, which implies that emotional or principled motives (e.g., anger at unfairness, desire for justice) also play a role. Their selectivity is moderate: they are not punishing every small deviation, but they are willing to intervene repeatedly when the same style of behavior (very low or zero contributions) recurs. They act like a “highly engaged monitor” rather than a minimalist or random punisher.

This player is an “active but selective punisher” who uses costly punishment as a norm-enforcement tool rather than as a constant retaliatory weapon. They do not punish every round and do not systematically target everyone who contributes less; instead, they intervene in specific phases when undercontribution is salient and cooperative norms appear threatened. Their punishment spending is non-trivial at several points, showing a willingness to incur personal costs to sanction others. The likely trigger is visible, repeated low contributions by specific players relative to a generally cooperative group. The size and recurrence of punishment episodes suggest they attempt to correct free-riders or chronic low contributors rather than to fine-tune small deviations. The pattern of when they punish versus when they abstain suggests that their primary motive is deterrence and norm stabilization, not blind revenge. There are instances where they endure substantial punishment from others yet do not immediately respond in kind or escalate punishment expenditure. This indicates that they are not primarily focused on punishing those who harmed them personally, but more on punishing behavior they classify as socially harmful (e.g., zero or very low contributions). They also at times stop punishing after periods of heavier use, even though undercontribution and punishment aimed at them persist. This suggests they learn that further punishment may not be cost-effective or may fuel conflict without improving contributions. They then switch to conserving resources or to signaling via contribution changes instead. Overall, their punishment style is consistent with a “moralist” or “norm enforcer” who is willing to bear costs to discipline defectors early and mid-game, but who becomes more pragmatic and less interventionist when results are discouraging or the cost of mutual sanctioning becomes too high.

This player is a consistent and active punisher of low contributors and free‑riders, using punishment as a norm‑enforcing tool rather than as a rare last resort. They spend on punishment in many rounds, even when doing so is personally costly and when they cannot verify its effect on others’ final payoffs. The fact that they continue to punish despite these costs indicates that enforcing fairness and deterring exploitation is intrinsically important to them, beyond immediate material gain. Their punishment behavior appears selective rather than indiscriminate: they tolerate medium contributions and focus their sanctions on those who contribute very little or nothing. This indicates a threshold‑based fairness rule: players who clearly undercut the group norm are targeted, while moderate deviations are more likely to be ignored. Their punishment seems primarily deterrent and norm‑policing, aiming to signal that very low contributions are unacceptable in this environment. They show willingness to incur punishment costs repeatedly across the game, not only early on, which suggests they do not quickly “give up” on norm enforcement even when free‑riding persists. They appear less motivated by emotional retaliation against particular individuals and more by role‑based enforcement of contribution norms; however, precise motives (anger vs. principled fairness) are unknown.

This player is an unusually heavy and persistent user of punishment, willing to spend substantial resources to sanction others. Punishment is not sporadic; it is a key part of their strategy. They rarely leave the punishment stage unused, and their total spending on sanctions is high relative to the cost-benefit of punishment in this environment. Their targeting rule is not fully observable, but several tendencies are apparent: - They reliably punish very low contributors, indicating an enforcement motive against clear free-riding. This aligns with a norm-enforcing or “altruistic punisher” orientation: they are willing to pay to reduce the payoff of those who contribute little compared to others. - They are not purely efficiency-focused: punishment continues even in conditions where average contributions are already moderate and where extra sanctions are unlikely to significantly improve public good provision. This implies that restoring or enforcing a fairness norm matters to them at least as much as maximizing group payoffs. - Their own contribution level does not have to be high for them to punish others. They sometimes undercontribute relative to highly cooperative peers yet still pay to punish lower contributors. That is consistent with a “moralistic free-rider” pattern: they tolerate contributing less than many, but they strongly resent those below what they see as a minimum acceptable standard. - The intensity of punishment is non-trivial: they are willing to impose multiple units on some targets rather than merely symbolically sanctioning. This suggests they expect deterrence to require meaningful cost to the target, or they derive psychological satisfaction from imposing a sizeable penalty on norm violators. They also sometimes punish robust contributors or at least do not clearly exempt medium contributors, which hints that the targeting rule may include personal factors (such as retaliation) or misperceptions, not just a clean ranking by contribution. However, because identities of their exact targets are not shown, any claim about systematic antisocial punishment remains low confidence. Net, they function as a “strong normative enforcer” who is prepared to sacrifice a notable share of their own earnings to punish others, even though their own contributions are not consistently exemplary.

The player never uses the punishment option, despite being repeatedly targeted by others and despite clear opportunities to punish lower contributors or retaliate against likely punishers. This stable non-use implies one or more of the following: a strong reluctance to incur personal costs for sanctioning; a purely instrumental, narrow payoff focus that considers punishment a dominated strategy; or indifference to norm enforcement and group welfare. There is no evidence of selective or conditional punishment; they do not respond with targeted sanctions against those whose contributions are relatively low, nor do they appear to use punishment strategically to shape others’ behavior. The absence of any retaliation toward probable punishers suggests that vengefulness is low or that they simply do not see value in spending coins on social or emotional goals. They also do not experiment with punishment even once, which points to a consistent, stable stance rather than indecision. Whether they understand the potential deterrent power of punishment is unknown; what is clear is that they do not attempt to use the mechanism for either deterrence or revenge.

This player is a *frequent and persistent punisher of low contributors*. They spend on punishment in most rounds, often at a nontrivial personal cost. Because punishing is always costly and there is no evidence they reduce punishment in response to being targeted themselves, their behavior fits a *norm-enforcing* rather than purely self-interested profile. Their punishment appears to be *selective* rather than random: opportunities for punishment co-occur with visible heterogeneity in others’ contributions, and they regularly choose to incur punishment costs even when group contribution levels are moderate or relatively high. This is consistent with targeting perceived free-riders or below-norm contributors. The data do not specify which peers they choose, so the exact cutoff or rule (e.g., punishing near-zero vs moderately low contributors) is unknown. They do not reliably escalate punishment in response to being punished, as there are rounds with no punishment spending despite them being targeted by others. This supports the interpretation that their primary motive is *deterrence and norm enforcement* rather than retaliation. They seem willing to keep paying for punishment over time, indicating a stable preference for sanctioning low cooperation even when it reduces their personal payoff and does not clearly improve aggregate cooperation in a way visible from the transcript. Overall, they behave like a *strong altruistic punisher*: they are willing to sacrifice their own earnings to discipline others, with low sensitivity to whether that sacrifice is immediately rewarded or reciprocated.

This player is a selective, norm‑enforcing punisher rather than systematically vengeful or systematically passive. They incur punishment costs on only a minority of opportunities, indicating that they do not treat punishment as routine or automatic. When they do punish, they are willing to spend more than the minimum, showing that they are prepared to invest in enforcement when they deem it important. Because target information is not visible in the transcript, the trigger pattern must be inferred indirectly and is thus uncertain. However, the timing of their punishment behavior coincides with contexts where some group members are contributing substantially less than others, while the focal player is themselves contributing at or above the group’s typical level. This pattern is consistent with punishment aimed at low contributors rather than random or spiteful punishment, but this inference has low confidence since target identities are unknown. They do not punish in every environment with free‑riding, and they sometimes refrain from punishing even when they themselves are being deducted. This suggests they differentiate between situations where punishment seems “worth it” and those where it does not, rather than applying a simple tit‑for‑tat rule. The proportional size of their punishment expenditures also indicates they may attempt to send stronger signals in some cases (investing multiple punishment units) while not escalating indefinitely. There is no observable evidence that they use punishment solely to retaliate against those who punished them, because their punishment activity does not consistently follow or coincide with their own receipt of deductions. Thus, a plausible interpretation is that they see punishment as a tool for enforcing contribution norms and deterring free‑riding, not primarily as a tool for personal revenge. That said, absent visibility of whom they punished, some portion of their punishment could be retaliatory; this is unknown. Overall, their punishment style fits a “reluctant enforcer” type: willing to spend resources to uphold perceived fairness and discourage low contributions, but not eager to use punishment constantly, and not evidently locked in a vendetta pattern.

The player never uses punishment, despite being punished at least once themselves. This persistent non‑use suggests a strong reluctance to spend resources on negative sanctions, even when they have clear targets (e.g., visible low contributors). Their behavior is consistent with either a principled aversion to costly punishment, a belief that punishment is ineffective, or a strict focus on personal payoff that treats punishment as an unnecessary expense. Because they do not punish even in cases where others defect noticeably or where they are themselves sanctioned, retaliation motives appear weak or absent. Selectivity patterns cannot be analyzed because there is no variation—the player simply does not punish. They therefore are unlikely to function as a norm enforcer or deterrent provider in new environments; they instead rely on adjusting their own contributions rather than trying to shape others’ behavior through sanctions.

They are definitely willing to punish and can become quite active with sanctions. Punishment is selective rather than constant: they mostly target clear low contributors, especially when those low contributions occur in a setting where others (including themselves) are contributing substantially. This is consistent with an inequity-averse or norm-enforcing motive—punishing those who appear to free-ride on a cooperative group. At times, they punish a small subset of low contributors; at other times, they spread punishment across multiple peers who all contribute relatively little compared with the group norm. This shift suggests that as the game progresses and they encounter repeated low contributions, their tolerance decreases and they are more willing to sanction several individuals at once. They do not use punishment to reward high contributors by contrast, nor do they seem to punish purely randomly. However, there is at least one instance where they punish someone whose contribution is not the lowest in the group; this may indicate that they track past behavior or cumulative impressions rather than only the current round’s contribution, or that they hold a stronger standard for certain players. Because past-round memory is not directly visible in the transcript, the exact rationale for those borderline punishments is uncertain. They do not appear to use punishment as retaliation for having been punished themselves; they punish regardless of whether they suffered deductions in the current round. This further supports a norm-enforcing rather than strictly retaliatory motive. They also accept the private cost of punishing even in later rounds, indicating that upholding contribution norms or expressing disapproval is sufficiently valuable to them to sacrifice immediate payoff.

They are almost entirely non-punitive, using the punishment option only once across the whole interaction. When they do punish, they target a specific under-contributing peer rather than multiple players, indicating selectivity. The target at that moment is contributing noticeably less than most others, and the group still shows substantial contributions, so the punishment is plausibly norm-enforcing rather than retaliatory: they appear to sanction someone viewed as not pulling their weight while the group is trying to maintain cooperation. They do not display spite or broad punishment campaigns; they never punish those who occasionally give low but are not uniquely deviant. They also do not use punishment to discipline extremely low contributors during clear breakdowns of cooperation, suggesting they see little point in spending on deterrence when the public good has largely collapsed. Overall, they are a reluctant, weak norm enforcer: willing to spend a small amount to signal disapproval of a salient free rider when cooperation still seems salvageable, but otherwise preferring to adjust their own contribution rather than invest in costly sanctions.

They almost never use punishment and do so only once, and only mildly, despite repeated opportunities and repeated exposure to clearly low contributors. This indicates a strong reluctance to spend resources on harming others. When they do punish, it coincides with a situation where group cooperation is weakened by one or more very low contributions, suggesting that their rare use of punishment is norm-enforcing rather than spiteful. There is no evidence of retaliatory punishment or of targeting high contributors. Their default stance appears to be “cooperate rather than punish.” They tolerate some level of free-riding without enforcement, relying more on continued high contributions and occasional own-contribution adjustments than on costly sanctions. This aligns with a personality that is conflict-averse or at least punishment-averse, prioritizing efficiency and group welfare over retribution, and willing to absorb some unfairness instead of engaging in frequent deterrence.

They never use punishment, despite facing several opportunities where others contribute less than them and despite once being punished themselves. This robust non-use suggests a stable reluctance to incur personal cost to sanction others, even when it might support higher future cooperation or serve as retaliation. Their refusal to punish after being targeted indicates they are not motivated by revenge. They also refrain from sanctioning low contributors in cooperative phases, which points away from strong norm-enforcement motives. They appear to adopt a “live and let live” stance: they accept others’ deviations and adjust their own contribution behavior (sometimes defecting themselves) rather than using institutional punishment to correct them. This aligns with a payoff-conscious but non-punitive type: they are willing to opportunistically free-ride at times instead of paying to discipline free-riders. At the same time, they still often cooperate at high levels without enforcing that norm on others. This combination suggests either a dislike of punitive actions, a belief that punishment is inefficient or ineffective, or low confidence that their individual sanctions would change group behavior. The precise underlying belief is unknown, but their revealed preference is a consistently non-punishing, non-retaliatory strategy.

The player never uses costly punishment despite repeated opportunities and clear instances where others contribute less.
