The punishment appears oriented toward deterrence and norm clarification rather than spite or retaliation; it is aimed at the most obvious violator of the emergent “all in” rule and is not repeated after the target increases contributions. After that point, they abstain from punishing even when others occasionally under-contribute or when they themselves are punished; they do not escalate or join group calls to punish further. This pattern suggests a “soft norm enforcer”: they will signal that a low contribution is unacceptable but then quickly revert to a default of non-intervention once compliance improves. They show no signs of vengeful punishment, coalition-based targeting, or enjoyment of sanctions for their own sake. Cost-efficiency of punishment appears understood (since they pick a clear target once) but is not exploited aggressively.

They rarely punish, but when they do, it is targeted and norm-based. Punishment is directed at clear free‑riding or very low contribution levels that reduce group efficiency, rather than at minor deviations or personal slights. They do not appear to punish out of spite for their own past punishment, nor do they join in group “piling on” purely for fun; instead, they ignore multiple calls from others to coordinate costful sanctions against a previously low-contributing player, even after that player has been labelled a “troll.” This indicates they view punishment as a tool for basic norm enforcement, not as entertainment or vendetta. Their behavior suggests a deterrence and correction motive (aimed at egregious under-contributors or multi-round slackers), combined with a reluctance to overspend on sanctions when contributions are currently back at the cooperative norm. They are also willing to sanction more than one deviant in a round if multiple players’ contributions fall well below the group standard, reflecting a consistent concern with fairness rather than singling out only one target.

The player is a consistent, selective punisher, especially of under-contributors, and they are willing to incur substantial cost to do so. They punish a moderate under-contributor very early and then repeatedly sanction the same individual whenever that person falls below the full-contribution norm. They also punish others who significantly deviate downward (including a player who contributes nothing at one point, and another who drops to zero later), and once they even sanction several full contributors simultaneously in a round when they themselves contributed very little, likely as a reaction to their earlier social pressure or timing behavior rather than to contribution levels. That multi-target punishment while under-contributing themselves strongly resembles antisocial or spiteful punishment driven by annoyance or dominance motives, not cooperative enforcement. Across the game, their punishment is clearly not profit-maximizing: they spend multiple units to inflict large deductions even when the rest of the group is already mostly conforming. Repeated targeting of the same under-contributor, plus chat calls for group-wide deductions against that person, suggests an intent to deter and to symbolically punish norm violators, rather than to recover personal losses. Their thresholds are asymmetric: minor deviations from full cooperation by a known offender are enough to trigger renewed sanctions, showing low tolerance for repeat “offenders.” They never use punishment in response to being punished themselves in a purely retaliatory way (see below), which indicates their punishing is more norm- or reputation-driven than vengeful in a personal sense.

They are a selective but consistent norm enforcer, targeting one specific player who repeatedly under-contributes. Punishment is directed only at that individual and only when that individual clearly deviates downward from the emerging full-cooperation norm. They do not punish any other deviators (e.g., when another player briefly contributes nothing, or when a different player contributes low once), suggesting their threshold to punish is tied to a pattern of non-compliance by a known norm-breaker more than to any single isolated deviation. Their punishment is not vengeful or escalating; they use a low, fixed intensity that is cost-effective and likely intended as a deterrent and corrective signal rather than as retaliation. Once that target returns to full cooperation for a while, they refrain from further sanctions, but if the same player later lapses again, they resume the same limited punishment. This points to a “conditional enforcer of repeat offenders” style: they punish to sustain cooperation, not to equalize payoffs or to express anger more broadly.

They never spend anything on punishment, despite multiple opportunities and despite being in a group where others use punishment aggressively, often in ways that are inefficient or spiteful. This is very stable: across cooperative and uncooperative phases, they consistently refrain from sanctioning. Given the environment—frequent punishment of both low and sometimes normal contributors, heavy spending by some players, and widespread negative payoffs for others—this abstention appears deliberate rather than inattentive. They act as a “non‑punishing cooperator”: they provide the public good but refuse to enforce norms via costly sanctions. Because they never punish even when clearly under-contributing players appear, it is unlikely that they are using punishment as a deterrent. The data also show them not using it for revenge when they themselves are punished. Their motives thus look anti-punitive (disliking the idea or cost of sanctioning) rather than simply lacking opportunities.

They use punishment intermittently and selectively, with overall moderate intensity. In many rounds they abstain entirely, but when they do punish they sometimes spend nontrivial amounts. There is no clear pattern of punishing the lowest contributors in a consistent, rule-like fashion. Instead, their punishment appears sporadic and context-driven. At times they punish in rounds where they themselves contribute at a reasonable level and others are also broadly cooperative, which makes a purely deterrent, norm-enforcement motive uncertain. They never use punishment in a clearly self-sacrificial way to discipline blatant free-riders; rather, their usage is compatible with a mix of mild norm enforcement, personal annoyance at particular players, and low-level experimentation with the mechanism. They do not appear to coordinate with the heavy punishers, nor to mirror the most extreme punitive behavior. When they ramp up punishment heavily once, it looks more like a situational spike than a stable trait. Overall, they are willing but not eager punishers, with ambiguous motives and no strong evidence of either systematic altruistic punishment or systematic antisocial punishment as a fixed rule.

They are a light but principled punisher. They do not punish in every round, and when they do, they almost always use a single punishment unit, not large multi‑unit attacks. Their punishment is selective and appears targeted at clear norm violators or especially low contributors relative to a high‑contribution environment. They almost never punish high contributors or clear cooperators, and there is no strong evidence that they ever engage in spiteful or payoff‑maximizing punishment (e.g., they do not target successful high‑earners just because they are doing well). Their pattern is more consistent with second‑order norm enforcement: occasionally sanctioning under‑contributors to support a cooperative standard. They continue this selective enforcement even when punishment in the group becomes wild and destructive, but they do not escalate to extreme levels themselves. They also sometimes refrain from punishing even in the presence of under‑contribution, indicating that they weigh the cost of punishing and do not feel compelled to enforce norms every time. Overall, their punishment motive looks primarily deterrent and norm‑focused, not vengeful or profit‑seeking; however, because they are personally targeted repeatedly, some component of retaliation cannot be ruled out (confidence low).

This player is willing to use punishment but does so intermittently and in concentrated bursts rather than continuously. Early and midgame, they spend nontrivial amounts on punishment in some rounds and zero in others. Their targets cannot be fully inferred from outcomes alone, but punishment appears broadly aligned with norm enforcement rather than random aggression: they punish in rounds where notable under-contribution is present in the group, and they refrain in many rounds where group contributions are high and relatively equal. They never engage in obvious “counter-punishment” immediately after being punished; instead, their own punishment choices do not track who harmed them. This suggests that their motive is more deterrence or norm enforcement than personal revenge. At several points when they themselves are hit with large deductions, they choose not to spend anything on punishment in the next period, indicating restraint rather than emotional retaliation. They also tolerate ongoing punishment episodes from others without escalating their own spending across the game as a whole. Their willingness to incur substantial costs to punish, despite being a consistently high contributor, suggests a preference for a cooperative, norm-following environment and some readiness to sacrifice income to support that. However, because their punishment is not constant, they likely weigh the cost and may only intervene when deviations seem especially salient or persistent. Selectivity is moderate, but the exact threshold that triggers them is unknown.

They never spend on punishment, despite many opportunities and visible deviations by others. This zero-use pattern is stable across cooperative and uncooperative phases. They refrain from punishing even when others contribute much less than them, when they are personally targeted by punishment, and when clearly antisocial or extreme punishment behavior occurs in the group. This indicates a strong reluctance or principled refusal to engage in costly sanctions. There is no evidence of targeted, selective, or retaliatory punishment; they simply abstain. Their stance suggests either a belief that punishment is ineffective or not their responsibility, a strong aversion to interpersonal sanctions, or a focus on preserving their own payoff and emotional distance from conflict rather than enforcing norms. They thus act as a “non-punishing cooperator”: they support the public good through contributions but never through costly deterrence.

This player is an extremely heavy and willing punisher and is prepared to spend very large amounts of resources to impose substantial losses on others. They sometimes spend more on punishment than they gain from the public good in that round, accepting very poor or even negative payoffs to harm targets. This indicates low weight on immediate material payoffs and a high willingness to bear costs to enforce or express norms. Their punishment is not narrowly focused on obvious low contributors. Sometimes they punish players who have contributed fully or nearly fully, and at other times they abstain from punishing modest under-contributors. They also sometimes target specific individuals with very intense sanctions while others with similar or lower contributions are not punished by them. This pattern suggests that punishment is not purely contribution-contingent; likely triggers include perceived personal grievances (such as being punished earlier), reputational judgments, or accumulated resentment, rather than a clean “punish defectors” rule. They show capacity for escalation: once conflictual punishment episodes begin, they can drastically ramp up the intensity of sanctions. They also show the ability to switch off punishment entirely and revert to no-punishment behavior for stretches where cooperation is high and personal grievances are low. Their punishment use thus appears episodic and conflict-driven rather than a steady institutional enforcement style. The overall profile fits a “strong-feelings, retaliatory and sometimes vengeful enforcer” type: willing and sometimes eager to punish, less discriminating in aligning punishment strictly with marginal contributions, and at times prioritizing expressive or retributive motives over efficiency or deterrence.

They punish sparingly and in a targeted way, not as a main strategic tool. Punishment episodes are infrequent relative to the number of opportunities. When they do spend on punishment, it tends to coincide with clear deviations from what looks like their cooperation norm (for example, very low contributors or individuals whose actions have been problematic in earlier rounds). They are cost‑conscious: their punishment expenditures are modest compared to the extreme, often self‑destructive punishment exhibited by some peers. They never join cycles of massive, vendetta‑style punishing. This points more toward norm‑enforcing or signaling motives than toward emotional retaliation. Their pattern is selective rather than blanket: they do not punish all below‑average contributors, and they largely abstain from punishing high contributors even when those high contributors engage heavily in punishment of others. That is, they do not systematically “punish the punishers.” This suggests that their criteria for punishment center on cooperation levels or perhaps on obvious unfairness, not on second‑order behavior. Deterrence is at best weakly evidenced. Because they punish so rarely and do not follow up with repeated punishment on the same target, it is unclear whether they intend to shape future play or simply express disapproval once. Motivation is best described as low‑frequency norm signaling rather than an active, sustained sanctioning strategy.

The player never spends on punishment throughout the game, despite multiple opportunities and despite being repeatedly punished themselves. This is stable: there is no “learning into” punishment, no late-game revenge, and no attempts to discipline obvious low contributors. Their threshold for using costly sanctions appears extremely high, or they reject punishment as a useful or acceptable tool. Because they also ignore clear free-riding and extreme punishment episodes by others, this looks less like a careful cost–benefit calculation and more like a principled or default non-punishing stance. Motivations are thus unlikely to be deterrence- or retaliation-based; if they hold norms about contribution, they are enforced only via their own behavior, not via sanctions. The player is tolerant of inequality in payoffs and of norm violations by others, at least in the sense that they do not translate these into punitive action.

This player is a frequent and relatively heavy user of punishment once conflict and opportunism emerge. Early in the game they use punishment sparingly or not at all, despite visible deviations by others, suggesting an initial inclination to give the group a chance to self-stabilize. As the game progresses and some players engage in extreme or persistent punishment campaigns, they shift into an active sanctioning role. Their targets are typically those whose behavior undermines either contribution norms (very low contribution while others are high) or punishment norms (very aggressive or seemingly vindictive use of punishment). However, they are not exclusively efficiency-oriented; at times their punishment expenditures are high enough to significantly reduce their own payoffs, indicating that enforcing fairness or expressing disapproval can override narrow self-interest. Overall, they function as a “conditional enforcer”: tolerant when the group is broadly cooperative, but increasingly willing to spend heavily to discipline those they perceive as destabilizing cooperation or abusing the punishment mechanism.

This player is an extremely heavy and recurrent punisher. They repeatedly spend very large amounts on punishment—even when it drives their own payoff deeply negative. Punishment is not narrowly cost-efficient or calibrated; on several occasions they deploy punishment at a scale that massively exceeds any plausible material benefit. This signals strong non-instrumental motives: they appear willing to punish to express disapproval, retaliate, or enforce norms, rather than to optimize earnings. Their targets, however, are not random. They consistently direct punishment toward specific behavioral classes: - Clear free riders or low contributors when others are cooperating. - Players who have previously punished them (antisocial punishers from their perspective). - Occasionally, players whose contribution or punishment patterns deviate from the cooperative majority. This pattern shows some selectivity: they do not punish everyone; they focus on perceived wrongdoers. At the same time, the intensity is often disproportionate, suggesting a propensity for over-punishment rather than mild correction. Given that they maintain full cooperation while punishing so heavily, their punishment seems aimed at defending a high-cooperation norm and retaliating against violations of that norm or direct attacks on them. They appear insensitive to the cost-effectiveness margin and accept very high costs to themselves to impose costs on others. This fits a “strong moralist” or “vengeful enforcer” type: norm-focused, emotionally driven, and willing to sacrifice personal earnings for punishment.

They very rarely punish and, when they do, they spend only minimal amounts. Their punishment is sparse across the game and does not escalate in response to others’ behavior. Given the environment, where other players frequently levy large and often extreme punishments (including what looks like antisocial or chaotic punishment), this player’s restraint stands out: they almost always abstain, even when clear defectors or highly disruptive punishers are present. This suggests a strong preference for conserving own payoff and avoiding engagement in punishment battles, or a belief that punishment is either ineffective, too costly, or not their role. When they do punish, the timing aligns with situations where some others contribute less, but given the very small magnitude, it is unclear whether they are attempting norm enforcement or simply experimenting with the mechanism; confidence on their motives is low. They do not engage in retaliatory or spiteful punishment cycles, and they make no visible systematic attempt to deter specific low contributors or pathological punishers.

The player is generally restrained but not strictly non-punishing. Most of the time they spend nothing on punishment, even in environments with substantial norm violations and chaotic, high-cost sanctioning by others. On a small number of occasions they do punish, and their targets align with clear contribution deviations (non-contributors or clear free-riders), not with high cooperators or random targets. They never engage in high-intensity, self-destructive punishment sprees; when they punish, they do so at low cost relative to others’ extremes. This pattern fits a “minimal, norm-enforcing punisher” who uses punishment sparingly and instrumentally rather than emotionally. They show no evidence of antisocial punishment. Whether they intend punishment as deterrence or as simple fairness enforcement is uncertain, but there is no sign they are trying to dominate or escalate sanction wars.

The player never uses punishment at any point, despite frequent and often extreme punishment activity by others and clear opportunities to target both low contributors and those who punish aggressively. This strongly suggests a “non-punisher” or “punishment-averse cooperator” type. Several interpretations are possible: they may dislike inflicting costs on others even when it could deter free‑riding; they may view punishment as wasteful and efficiency-reducing; or they may be conflict-averse and unwilling to single others out. The data do not allow distinguishing these motives, so the specific reasoning is unknown. What is observable is that their cooperative behavior is not paired with costly norm enforcement: they contribute fully but do not sanction defectors or antisocial punishers. They also do not engage in retaliatory punishment when they themselves are punished (see next section). Their strategy therefore uses no punitive leverage at all, even though the mechanism is powerful and cheap in this environment.

This player uses punishment actively but selectively and only in conjunction with higher own contributions. When they contribute significantly or fully, they invest considerable resources in punishment, even though it is personally costly. When they free-ride at zero contribution, they virtually never spend on punishment. This pattern suggests a conditional punisher type: willing to enforce norms or retaliate when they perceive themselves as cooperative, but uninterested in sanctioning when they are exploiting the group. Because punishers are anonymous and recipient outcomes are hidden, the precise targeting is unknown. However, the tight coupling between “I contribute a lot” and “I spend heavily on punishment” implies that the motive is at least partly expressive or retaliatory. They seem to react to being punished themselves by escalating their own punishment expenditures in the same or subsequent cooperative episodes, which is consistent with spiteful or vengeance-oriented behavior. When they revert to pure free-riding, they stop punishing entirely, indicating that deterrence or group welfare is not their main concern; they do not pay to discipline others while they themselves are defecting. They never appear to use low-cost, mild punishments as a calibration tool. Instead, when they do punish, they spend at relatively high levels, suggesting low aversion to conflict and a readiness to incur sizable costs to harm others’ payoffs when they feel aggrieved or norm-compliant. Overall, their punishment pattern fits a self-focused, conditional enforcer: they punish when they see themselves as having “moral standing” via contribution and withdraw from enforcement when they free-ride, with motives tilted more toward retaliation and emotional satisfaction than toward efficient norm-building.

The player is willing to use costly punishment but only briefly and selectively; afterward they largely abandon it. Early in the game they invest non-trivial amounts in punishing others, absorbing substantial personal cost. This occurs in contexts where their own contribution is relatively high and there is visible variation in contributions, suggesting their punishment is at least partly norm-enforcing: they appear to target perceived low contributors when they themselves cooperate. However, this willingness to enforce norms does not persist. After facing non-trivial punishment against themselves and after seeing that punishment is possible and used by others, they stop spending on punishment almost entirely for the rest of the game. The long stretch without any punitive action implies several possible, not mutually exclusive, inferences: they may conclude that punishment is too costly relative to its benefits, that it fails to reliably raise cooperation, or that it exposes them to retaliatory punishment in an anonymous environment. Which of these dominates is unknown, but the behavioral shift is strong. They do not appear to use punishment as an ongoing deterrence tool; there is no sustained pattern of “disciplining” low contributors across many rounds. Nor do they demonstrate persistent retaliatory punishment after being targeted themselves; beyond the early phase, they do not respond to being punished by punishing back. This indicates that spite or revenge is not a central driver of their behavior once they have updated on the costs. In net, their punishment profile is: initially willing to pay to enforce contribution norms when cooperating, but quickly updating toward a no-punishment strategy once its personal inefficiency becomes salient. They accept that others may free-ride and that occasional punishment against them will occur, and they focus thereafter on adjusting their own contributions and free-riding patterns rather than spending more on sanctions.

The player is a selective, low-to-moderate-intensity punisher who uses sanctions sparingly and appears motivated more by norm enforcement toward low contributors than by retaliation. Across most rounds they do not engage in punishment at all, even when others are contributing less than they are, indicating a non-vindictive baseline and a reluctance to spend resources on punishment unless a threshold is crossed. When they do decide to punish, it is infrequent and of small magnitude, suggesting that they view punishment as a calibrated signal rather than a tool for heavy deterrence. The timing of their punishment choices aligns with environments where group cooperation is relatively high and clear defectors or very low contributors stand out more sharply against that background. This pattern is consistent with a “weak altruistic punisher” type: they are willing to incur some personal cost to sanction those who fall far below an implicit contribution standard, especially when a cooperative norm seems to exist and be worth defending. Their willingness to punish persists even in the final round, which implies that their enforcement motive is not solely about shaping others’ future behavior or improving long-run earnings. Instead, they seem to derive some immediate value from seeing low contributors penalized, or they hold a principle that defectors “ought” to be sanctioned regardless of future consequences. However, because punishment is rare and modest, this normative inclination is clearly moderated by cost considerations. They do receive punishment themselves in some periods without any clear retaliatory response. Their contributions after being punished do not show a consistent spike of revenge-driven punishment or dramatic over-cooperation. This further supports the view that they are not primarily motivated by spite or personal grievance, but by an asymmetric standard focused on low contributors rather than on reciprocating sanctions directed at them.

The player never uses the punishment mechanism, despite experiencing multiple instances of deductions from others. This indicates a strong reluctance to spend personal coins to sanction anyone, even when they are personally targeted. They show no evidence of retaliatory punishment, norm-enforcing punishment, or strategic punishment to shape others’ contributions. Their non-use of punishment persists across very different contribution levels and group climates. Even when they contribute more than many others, they do not sanction free-riders; when they contribute little and benefit from others’ cooperation, they also do not punish high contributors; and when they are punished themselves, they do not respond in kind. This pattern is consistent with a cost-averse, non-punitive type who views punishment as an inefficient or unproductive expense. Because punishers and their motives are opaque in this setting, the player appears to treat punishment as random noise rather than as a signal to be answered. They neither try to deter future sanctions nor to enforce a contribution norm. Their overall strategy is to adapt contributions, not to influence others via costly punishment. This suggests low inclination toward altruistic or moralistic punishment and a preference to absorb or work around unfair treatment rather than fight it.

The player is an active, costly punisher, willing to spend substantial amounts to reduce others’ payoffs. They use punishment repeatedly across the game rather than as a rare or emergency tool. This shows that norm enforcement or expressing disapproval matters to them enough to sacrifice their own earnings. Their punishment is plausibly aimed at low contributors or perceived free-riders in otherwise cooperative environments. However, because individual punishment targets are not visible, this inference is low confidence. Still, the timing of their punishment—often in rounds with relatively strong group contributions or clear heterogeneity—suggests they likely try to sanction those they see as undermining cooperation. They do not appear to use punishment primarily as retaliation for being punished themselves: they sometimes punish when they personally were not just punished, and they also refrain from punishing in periods after being targeted. This points more toward a deterrence or norm-enforcement motive than pure revenge. They are not timid with punishment costs; in several instances they devote a nontrivial portion of their potential earnings to sanction others. This indicates relatively high willingness to bear personal costs for shaping others’ behavior or expressing moralistic anger. At the same time, they do not punish every round; punishment appears selective and context-dependent rather than impulsive or constant. Overall, they resemble a payoff-conscious but strongly norm-enforcing type: willing to free-ride at times, yet also willing to spend heavily to punish behavior they interpret as socially harmful.

This player is an active but selective user of costly punishment. They do not punish in every round, but when they do, they are willing to spend a noticeable portion of their payoff to impose meaningful costs on others. Their punishment activity tracks situations where some players contribute much less than what appears to be a cooperative benchmark, or where there are salient deviants (very low contributors) within an otherwise moderate-to-high contributing group. This pattern fits a “norm-enforcing” motive more than a purely retaliatory one, since they punish even in rounds where they themselves are not being targeted and even when they are not the highest contributor. They do not indiscriminately punish all low contributors across all rounds; instead, they intervene in a subset of situations, indicating some threshold or contextual rule (e.g., punishing only when free-riding is especially stark or when overall cooperation seems worth preserving). When they themselves are punished, there is no clear evidence of immediate revenge behavior tied to specific individuals, given the anonymity of punishers. Their continued use of punishment after being targeted suggests they view punishment as a tool to steer group behavior, not merely as an emotional reaction. Overall, this player fits a “costly norm enforcer with self-interested restraint”: they are willing to sacrifice some coins to sanction apparent free-riders and support cooperative norms, but they do not punish so extensively that they ignore their own payoff, and they sometimes refrain from punishing even when low contributions are present.

Punishment is central to this player’s type. They are a frequent and often heavy user of punishment, at times spending a large share of their payoff to reduce others’ payoffs. Their punishment is not random: it is closely tied to others’ contribution behavior and appears to follow a relatively coherent logic, though it is applied more aggressively than is payoff-maximizing. They most plausibly target very low contributors or clear free riders, especially when the public good level is otherwise moderate or high. This implies a strong aversion to perceived exploitation: they are willing to sacrifice substantial coins to discipline those they see as not doing their fair share. Their punishment is therefore primarily norm-enforcing rather than spiteful or arbitrary. When others’ contributions are broadly high, they are still willing to punish isolated low or zero contributors rather than ignoring them. They also continue to punish even after being punished themselves, and after observing that their own payoff can be severely reduced by others’ punitive actions. This persistence indicates that their punishment is not purely reciprocal retaliation but is driven by a robust commitment to enforcing contribution norms, even under the threat of counter-punishment. However, there is at least some retaliatory element: after receiving significant punishment while behaving cooperatively, they do not abandon the use of punishment, suggesting they may respond to perceived injustice with further punitive action. Their use of punishment is not marginal or purely symbolic; they often spend enough to substantially depress their own payoff, indicating either (a) strong negative emotional response to free riding, (b) a belief that harsh sanctions are necessary to change behavior, or (c) both. They do not appear to carefully calibrate punishment to cost-effectiveness; instead they err on the side of intensity, signaling a “hardline enforcer” personality—norms and fairness are protected even at high personal cost. In summary, this player is a conditional cooperator and strong norm enforcer, with high tolerance for costly punishment and a willingness to trade off immediate material gain in order to punish perceived unfairness and attempt to steer group behavior.

This player is a relatively active and costly punisher who seems to use punishment primarily to enforce contribution norms and possibly to retaliate when they feel exploited or targeted. Across the game, they frequently spend resources to punish others, even when the efficiency of the punishment technology is moderate rather than extreme. This indicates they place non-trivial value on shaping behavior or expressing disapproval, above and beyond immediate monetary payoff. They appear especially willing to punish when they themselves contribute at least a modest amount and perceive others as contributing conspicuously less. Their punishment is more consistent with norm-enforcing behavior than with random or purely spiteful actions: they seem to direct punishment at those whose contributions are clearly below what they see as an acceptable standard for the current collective environment. When the group average is high and a subset of players contributes very little or nothing, their punishment activity tends to be present; when the entire group is more uniformly low, or when their own contribution is very low, they are more selective or refrain from punishing. There is also evidence consistent with some sensitivity to retaliation and deterrence. After being punished themselves, they do not respond with an obvious and immediate punishment “counterattack” against the entire group; instead, they continue to direct punishment in a pattern that still looks norm-based. This suggests that personal retaliation is not their primary motive, even though being punished may contribute to their later willingness to enforce norms more strongly or to adjust their own contributions. Overall, they behave as a conditional cooperator who uses targeted punishment to encourage a cooperative standard and to discourage persistent low contributors. They are willing to incur significant personal cost to signal that extremely low contributions are unacceptable, but they do not punish indiscriminately and they do not rely on punishment alone; they always pair it with their own contribution choices as part of a broader strategy of conditional cooperation and norm enforcement.

This player uses punishment sparingly and selectively. They punish only in a few instances across the entire game and otherwise refrain, even when many others contribute very little or when they themselves are severely punished. This indicates that they are not a “moralist enforcer” who routinely sanctions low contributors, nor are they an aggressive retaliator. When they do choose to punish, the context shows relatively strong overall cooperation with a few conspicuously low contributors. Their punishment therefore is most plausibly aimed at low or outlying free-riders in an otherwise cooperative environment, consistent with norm-enforcing or efficiency-oriented motives rather than random hostility. The cost/benefit ratio of the punishment technology is reasonably favorable, yet they still use it infrequently, which suggests they view punishment as an exceptional tool rather than a standard part of their strategy. Importantly, they are themselves targeted by punishment in multiple rounds, including once at high intensity and later at lower intensity, yet they never respond by punishing back in the immediately following rounds. This absence of clear “revenge” behavior, despite significant personal losses, implies that retaliation is not their dominant driver. Instead, when punished, they adjust through their contributions (at times dropping or varying their level) rather than through counter-punishment. Taken together, their profile is that of a low-frequency, conditional punisher: willing to sanction when cooperation is otherwise high and defectors stand out, but unlikely to invest in sustained punishment campaigns or to punish as a primary way of managing others. Their punishment is more aligned with sporadic deterrence or norm reinforcement than with emotional retaliation, though the exact motivational mix is uncertain (low confidence on internal motives).

Punishment use is extremely rare. Across the entire interaction, they only pay to punish once. This shows a strong baseline reluctance to spend on sanctions, consistent with a payoff‑maximizing orientation that treats punishment as a costly last resort rather than a standing tool for norm enforcement. When they do punish, it occurs in an environment where many peers are contributing substantial amounts and where at least one player is contributing markedly less than that emergent norm. Their willingness to spend on punishment in this context indicates sensitivity to fairness and to norm violations: they are prepared to sanction clear free‑riding in a generally cooperative environment. Because punishment is used only once and not repeated immediately afterward, they do not appear to be a dedicated “moralist” or second‑order enforcer. The single use looks more like a targeted corrective response than an ongoing deterrence campaign. Whether it is meant to deter future free‑riding or to express frustration is unknown. They never use punishment to retaliate after being sanctioned themselves, even after receiving a very large deduction. This suggests low propensity for revenge or spite, or alternatively, a belief that retaliation is ineffective or too costly given the anonymity of punishers. They thus lean toward restraint in punishment and do not escalate conflicts. In sum, they are a low‑punishment player who occasionally enforces norms when deviations are salient and the group is otherwise cooperative, but generally prefers to keep all resources for themselves rather than invest systematically in sanctions.

They are clearly willing to use costly punishment and appear to behave like a second-order conditional cooperator: they support cooperation norms via sanctions, even though they themselves do not always fully comply with the highest cooperative standard. They target obvious low contributors and do not scatter punishment randomly. Their sanctions are selective and directed at players who contribute very little relative to the group or substantially less than a rough informal norm they seem to hold. They do not punish high contributors, and there is no evidence of spite or antisocial punishment. Early on, they punish several low contributors simultaneously, suggesting an attempt to shape behavior or enforce a cooperative standard. Later, they use more targeted sanctions on specific repeat low contributors, consistent with norm enforcement and possibly deterrence. The pattern fits a primarily deterrence/discipline motive rather than retaliation, as they punish those who under-contribute relative to others, not those who harmed them personally.

This player is a strong, targeted punisher who focuses primarily on the most extreme free-rider. They are willing to pay substantial personal costs to sanction someone who repeatedly contributes almost nothing, far beyond what would be payoff-maximizing in a narrow economic sense. This indicates a strong norm-enforcement or moralistic stance: they appear to punish to express disapproval and to deter, not to gain direct material benefit. Their punishment is selective rather than scattershot: they focus on individuals whose contributions are markedly below the group’s informal standard, and they do not punish medium or high contributors. They also do not systematically punish everyone who ever under-contributes; instead, they concentrate on a salient low contributor and, later, on another player who contributes very little relative to the group and to this player’s own contribution. They never use reward, even when others contribute highly, suggesting a preference for “stick” over “carrot” as a regulatory tool. Their willingness to escalate punishment against a persistent low contributor is very high and includes extremely costly sanctioning, pointing to a personality profile consistent with strong negative reciprocity and possible “altruistic punishment”: they accept large personal losses to uphold their cooperation norm and to penalize norm violators.

The player is a selective, norm-enforcing punisher who targets clear low contributors, but does not punish often. When they do punish, they choose obvious under-contributors relative to the group and relative to their own contribution, consistent with a peer-sanctioning norm-enforcer motive rather than random hostility. They never punish medium or high contributors and never engage in counter-punishment, even when they themselves are punished. They also do not escalate: their punishment levels stay low and appear symbolic but potentially instructive, consistent with a deterrence or corrective motive rather than spite. Because they sometimes tolerate modest under-contribution without punishing and only intervene in more extreme cases, their threshold for sanctioning appears relatively high. They do not seem driven by direct material gain from punishment (which is costly) but by a preference for maintaining a baseline of cooperative behavior in the group. Overall, they resemble an “occasional altruistic punisher”: willing to bear a cost to sanction clear defectors, but not committed to continuous, systematic policing.

This player is selectively but strongly willing to punish clear free-riders. They ignore minor deviations from high cooperation and focus their punitive actions on the individual who repeatedly contributes at or near the minimum while others maintain much higher contributions. When they do punish, they invest substantial resources relative to their own payoff, making punishment personally costly but highly damaging to the target, which is consistent with a norm-enforcing or deterrence-oriented motive rather than light signaling. They never punish merely because they themselves were punished; there is no evidence of retaliation. They also do not punish medium contributors or those slightly below the highest contributors, suggesting they reserve punishment for behavior they perceive as blatant exploitation rather than for fine-grained inequities. Once the main free-rider disappears from the game, they stop using punishment entirely, even though others occasionally contribute less than the maximum. This supports the interpretation that they view punishment as a targeted tool for sanctioning extreme non-cooperation, not as a continuous instrument for payoff optimization or revenge. Overall, they function as a second-order enforcer: they are willing to incur real costs to discipline obvious defectors but do not escalate into conflict with the broader group.

They never use punishment, despite repeated opportunities and clear visibility of others who contribute substantially less. This indicates a strong reluctance to engage in costly sanctioning, even when doing so could be efficiency-enhancing and is relatively powerful. They do not target consistent low or minimal contributors, nor do they use light punishment as a signal. This suggests that their cooperation is non-punitive and non-enforcing: they prefer to cooperate regardless of others’ deviations rather than trying to correct them. Motives for non-punishment could include a desire to avoid harming others, a belief that punishment is normatively wrong, or a perception that sanctioning is not worth the cost, but the precise motive is unknown. There is no evidence of retaliatory motives or of using punishment for deterrence.

They never use punishment, even when others contribute substantially less or repeatedly under-contribute. This indicates either a principled aversion to harming others’ payoffs, a belief that punishment is not appropriate or effective, or a strong focus on their own cooperative behavior over norm enforcement. They show no evidence of using punishment instrumentally to deter free-riding, nor of retaliating against low contributors. Their stance is tolerant and non-confrontational: they accept contribution heterogeneity without sanctions. This aligns with a “pure cooperator” or “non-punitive altruist” type who separates personal cooperation from enforcing norms on others.

This player is willing to engage in costly punishment and does so multiple times. Their targets include both those who previously punished them and those whose contribution behavior does not clearly justify sanctioning, indicating that their punishment is at least partly retaliatory and possibly spiteful. The pattern does not look like careful norm enforcement based solely on contribution levels; rather, it resembles lashing out at perceived offenders or high-impact players. They are not especially selective or efficient in their use of punishment and seem willing to incur net losses to harm particular others. They do not escalate punishment indefinitely but do persist long enough to inflict substantial costs, implying a non-trivial taste for retaliation or status assertion, even when this is materially disadvantageous.

The player never uses the punishment option at any point, even when others free‑ride or when punishment is clearly common and impactful. This indicates a strong reluctance to spend personal resources on sanctioning. Their motives appear more efficiency‑preserving than retributive: they avoid the deadweight loss associated with punishment even in a setting where many others are actively sanctioning. They also do not engage in antisocial punishment; they do not target high contributors or retaliate against cooperators. Whether they would ever punish in more extreme circumstances is unknown, but in this environment they behave as a non‑punisher who relies on others to enforce norms.

They never use the punishment option at any point. This is despite clear opportunities and social “justifications”: there are repeated free‑riders, and others openly discuss punishing them and report doing so. Their choice suggests a strong reluctance to spend resources on harming others, even for norm enforcement. There is no evidence they even threaten punishment or try to mobilize others to punish; they simply ignore that mechanism behaviorally and in chat. Their approach seems neither retaliatory nor deterrence‑oriented. Instead, they appear to rely on unilateral contribution plus social cohesion rather than costly sanctioning. The lack of any punishment, even after being personally deducted, indicates they either discount punishment’s instrumental value, dislike the idea of harming others, or both. Because they do not discuss the mechanism directly, their underlying motive is uncertain, but their revealed behavior fits a cooperative, non‑sanctioning type.

They are willing to punish, but only sporadically and in concentrated bursts, and they incur substantial personal cost when they do. Most of the time they refrain from using the punishment mechanism, even when clear low contributors exist and others are discussing deduction. At several points, however, they spend a notable amount on punishment, suggesting they sometimes shift into an enforcement role. The pattern is consistent with targeted second‑order norm enforcement against perceived defectors rather than random or spiteful use; however, the transcript does not reveal who they punished, so the exact selectivity is unknown. Their willingness to spend heavily in a few instances—despite being otherwise cooperative and quiet—suggests a threshold-based response: they tolerate some unfairness, but when it crosses a personal line (for example, persistent low contribution by certain avatars or direct punishment of themselves), they are prepared to spend to sanction, even at net personal loss. Whether their punishment aims primarily at deterrence (to restore cooperation) or at expressing disapproval/retaliation is uncertain; both motives are plausible, but evidence is insufficient to distinguish.

The player is a consistent but selective punisher of low contributors and a non-retaliatory target when punished. They are willing to pay nontrivial costs to impose deductions, and they do so repeatedly across the game rather than as a one-off reaction. This indicates they accept personal cost to enforce contribution norms. Their punishment pattern aligns strongly with non-contribution: they tend to spend on punishment in periods when several others clearly contribute nothing, and abstain when contributions are high and broadly cooperative. This suggests their primary trigger is observed free-riding rather than random or spiteful use of the mechanism. They also increase punishment intensity in stretches where certain individuals persistently show as non-contributors, which is consistent with norm-enforcement directed at perceived chronic defectors. They do not appear to use punishment to settle personal scores. When they themselves are punished, they do not shift into a retaliation pattern; their later punishment decisions still track with low contribution elsewhere, not with punishing those who hurt them. They never state in chat that they are punishing anyone, nor do they join the group’s joking or conspiratorial talk about “just start deducting them.” This implies their motive is more quietly normative (enforcing what they see as fair behavior) than socially performative. Given the multiplier and punishment technology, their behavior is consistent with a typical “strong reciprocator”: willing to spend on punitive enforcement of cooperation even if it is privately costly, as long as it targets free-riders and supports a cooperative norm.

This player is a willing and sometimes heavy user of costly punishment. They incur substantial punishment costs in multiple rounds, including at times when they themselves are not contributing. The transcript does not show which specific peers they target, so target selection is unknown, but the large total expenditures indicate a strong readiness to use the punishment mechanism. Because they sometimes free-ride and still choose to spend heavily on punishment, their motives are unlikely to be purely altruistic enforcement of a contribution norm. Instead, several possibilities are compatible with the observed behavior: curiosity about the mechanism, enjoyment of the “chaos” as the group speculates about random deductions, or targeted punishment of particular perceived defectors (e.g., those repeatedly shown as 0) mixed with occasional broader sanctioning. Without direct mapping from their costs to particular victims, selectivity and accuracy are unknown. Timing-wise, they invest in punishment both after having been publicly framed as a defector and later when the group is confused about large deduction events. This suggests they are not deterred by reputational risk from punishing; if anything, they may enjoy or at least tolerate being a hidden or ambiguous “enforcer” or troublemaker. There is no evidence of pure retaliation (they punish in rounds where they have not been punished) and no clear evidence of using punishment to bring their own payoff in line with others. Deterrence motives are uncertain; the available information does not show a subsequent clear shift in others’ behavior that they could plausibly be attempting to induce. Overall, they are an active punisher with ambiguous motives, willing to sacrifice their own earnings for the sake of influencing or perturbing others’ payoffs, but without a consistent alignment between their own cooperation level and their use of sanctions.

The player very rarely uses punishment, but they are not strictly anti‑punishment; they do punish in a small number of instances. Given the timing, the most plausible targets are clearly low‑contributing or persistent free‑riding players, which suggests a norm‑enforcing rather than spiteful motive. They never escalate punishment, and their overall spending on punishment is very low relative to the number of opportunities. They do not use punishment to retaliate when they themselves are deducted, nor do they lash out at ambiguous or borderline cases. They also do not appear to punish “to teach” in a vocal way; they never announce or justify punishments in chat. This indicates a reluctant, selective punisher type: willing to incur a small cost to sanction obvious norm violators, but not willing to engage in punishment wars or to invest heavily in deterrence. Their default stance is “cooperate and ignore,” with occasional minimal enforcement when deviation is blatant.

They never use the punishment mechanism at all, even though there are repeated and clearly identifiable low contributors and even when other players explicitly call for deductions or describe punishment as a response to free‑riding. This consistent abstention points to a strong reluctance to incur personal costs to discipline others. It is unclear whether this stems from a principled aversion to punishment, a belief that the system or data is unreliable, a desire to avoid interpersonal conflict, or simple payoff‑maximizing avoidance of punishment costs. However, their pattern rules out “conditional cooperator with peer punishment”: they cooperate but refuse to enforce norms through costly sanctions. They also do not join in any verbal calls for sanctioning, reinforcing an image of a non‑confrontational, non‑enforcing cooperator. Their stance is closer to “moralistic but non‑punitive” or “quiet altruist”: they uphold their own contribution norm but do not try to correct others.

For nearly the entire interaction, this player completely refrains from using punishment, even though punishment against them and others occurs repeatedly. This long period of restraint indicates a strong baseline aversion to destructive sanctions: they do not appear inclined to retaliate when targeted, and they do not use punishment to discipline low contributors or erratic sanctioners, despite ample opportunities. Only at the very end do they choose to punish, and when they finally do, they target more than one player and combine punishment with reward. This shift suggests that punishment, when it occurs, is deliberate, not impulsive. The selected targets are players who, over the game, are frequently involved in heavy and sometimes seemingly inefficient sanctioning activity, and/or who at that point are not clearly aligned with a stable, fair cooperation pattern. The timing implies that the player may be using punishment to express disapproval of disruptive or confusing behavior, and possibly to send a final consistency or fairness signal rather than to shape long-run behavior (since no future rounds are guaranteed from their perspective). Their late punishment is selective rather than blanket: they do not punish everyone or simply mirror who has punished them. This points to motives closer to norm-enforcement or frustration with perceived destabilizers than to direct revenge. However, because identities of punishers are hidden, their inferences about who deserves punishment are imperfect; their action at the end could also reflect accumulated resentment at perceived unfairness in sanction patterns in general. Motivationally, they appear to prioritize minimizing waste from punishment for most of the game, only overriding this when they judge that purely cooperative restraint is not adequately recognized or that disruptive behavior has gone too far. In summary, this player is a “reluctant punisher”: strongly punishment-averse, slow to retaliate, only willing to punish when they can align it with a broader fairness statement, and not using punishment as a routine deterrent tool.

This player is an active, selective punisher who uses punishment primarily to enforce contribution norms and occasionally to retaliate or counter-punish. They are willing to incur substantial personal cost to punish and do so across many rounds, often targeting those contributing noticeably less than others. Low contributors, especially when others contribute high amounts, are frequent targets. When one partner contributes nothing or very little, that partner is reliably punished even if the punisher’s own contribution is high; this suggests a principled response to perceived free-riding. They also punish moderately contributing players in contexts where others are clearly contributing more, indicating that they set a relatively high threshold for what they deem acceptable effort. At the same time, they do not punish indiscriminately: they often refrain from punishing when everyone is contributing at least modestly and when group behavior is relatively balanced. Any prosocial or norm-enforcing motive, if present, is weak enough that it never overrides their aversion to direct sanctioning costs.

This player never uses the punishment option, even though they repeatedly experience being punished by others. Their willingness to punish, based on observed behavior, is effectively zero. This is consistent across contexts where there are clear low contributors present and where punishment could be used either for norm enforcement or retaliation. The absence of any punishing behavior, despite being a repeated target, suggests at least three likely characteristics: a strong reluctance to spend resources on harming others, a limited taste for retaliation, and/or a belief that punishing is either ineffective or not worth the cost. They appear to accept that others will discipline them without responding in kind. They do not selectively punish even extreme low contributors. This points away from a “moral enforcer” or “altruistic punisher” type and toward a strategy that avoids escalations. From the outside, their stance can be described as punishment-averse or punishment-passive: they let the social environment shape them but do not attempt to shape it through sanctions themselves. Because they never punish, there is no evidence they use punishment strategically as a deterrent or a signal. Any deterrence they experience comes only from being punished, not from engaging in reciprocal or pre-emptive punishment.

This player never uses the punishment mechanism, despite repeated exposure to free-riding or under-contribution by some peers. They show zero willingness to spend personal resources to impose costs on others, even when punishment is relatively efficient and could in principle deter low contributions. There is no evidence of selective punishment targeting the lowest contributors or of broader norm-enforcement behavior. The player does not differentiate between modest and severe deviation from high cooperation through punitive action; they simply refrain in all observed cases. From behavior alone, it is unclear whether this is due to (a) a principled rejection of punitive strategies, (b) a belief that punishment is ineffective, (c) a desire to maximize own payoff by avoiding punishment costs, or (d) inattention or confusion about the mechanism. However, they consistently pass on the opportunity across varying group contribution profiles, which suggests at least a stable preference or habit against punishing. Because they maintain full contributions without ever punishing, their cooperation is not backed by threats. They behave like a “non-punishing cooperator” rather than a “conditional cooperator with sanctions.” Deterrence motives are not expressed in their choices; retaliation motives cannot be assessed because they are never targeted by punishment in the available data. Their strategy combines generous contribution with zero enforcement: they accept the presence of free-riders and do not attempt to discipline them, even when doing so might be materially justified. This indicates a personality profile that is either conflict-averse, forgiving, or purely focused on the positive act of contributing rather than on policing others’ behavior. Which of these explanations is correct is unknown from the transcript alone.

This player almost never uses punishment and, when they do, uses it only once and minimally. They do not employ punishment as a regular tool for norm enforcement or payback, despite being a low or medium contributor for much of the game and being a repeated target of sanctions themselves. This strongly suggests a baseline reluctance to spend on punishment and a preference to respond to dissatisfaction by changing their own contributions rather than by directly sanctioning others. Their one instance of punishment is weak and isolated. With only a single, low-intensity use and no follow-up, they show no attempt to establish a deterrent pattern, escalate, or engage in ongoing feuds. This indicates that they are not a “moralistic punisher” type and do not adopt a strong norm-policing role even when others contribute very little. Instead, they appear highly selective, and possibly conflicted, about punishing: willing to do it once when something crosses a personal line, but not committed to a general principle of sanctioning free riders. The timing and rarity of their punishment suggest that the motive is more likely emotional or retaliatory than forward-looking deterrence: they do not build on that act to stabilize cooperation or reinforce a contribution norm. After being repeatedly punished, they do not respond by punishing those whose contributions are out of line with the group; they let others’ low contributions persist with no systematic sanctions from their side. This fits a type who dislikes interpersonal conflict, even in anonymous, strategic form, and who prefers quiet withdrawal (via reduced contributions) over active confrontation. Unknown: the specific target(s) of their single punishment and whether it is aimed at a low contributor, a perceived aggressor, or both. Because of that, the precise trigger—pure free-riding vs. perceived unfair attack—remains uncertain, though the low overall appetite for punishment is clear.

This player never uses the punishment option, despite repeated exposure to clear free-riding or consistently low contributions by specific others. Their punishment tendency is therefore best described as strongly non-punitive within the observed environment. They show no evidence of engaging in “altruistic punishment” of low contributors to protect the public good, even when punishing would be relatively cheap and targeted. The repeated availability and non-use of the mechanism, in the presence of identifiable low contributors, suggests an underlying reluctance to impose costs on others. This could stem from a dislike of conflict, an ethical aversion to harming others even at a profit to the group, or a belief that punishment is ineffective or unnecessary, but these underlying motives are unknown; only the behavioral pattern of abstention is observed. Their non-use of punishment is also consistent with a strategy that prioritizes maintaining a cooperative climate over directly enforcing norms. They appear willing to “carry” some defectors rather than risk the potential social fallout or escalation that punishment could entail, even knowing that punishers are identifiable. This suggests they are not motivated by retaliation or by strong norm-enforcement impulses. There is no evidence of selective or strategic punishment (e.g., targeting only the worst free-riders or using a single, symbolic punishment to send a signal). They simply never activate the punishment tool. As a result, they do not attempt explicit deterrence, nor do they attempt to punish in response to changes in others’ contributions. Their approach aligns more with a “non-interventionist cooperator”: they hope to sustain cooperation by example rather than by sanction. Because they never punish, we also see no tradeoff behavior where they might reduce contribution in favor of punishing; instead, they retain high contributions and forgo sanctioning entirely. In new environments, this type is likely to continue to avoid punishment even when punishment is effective and socially accepted, unless very strong opposing incentives are introduced.

The player never uses the punishment mechanism, despite repeated opportunities and despite being punished themselves more than once. This strongly suggests a very low willingness to spend resources to discipline others. It is unknown whether this reflects a principled aversion to harming others, a belief that punishment is inefficient or futile, a desire to avoid interpersonal conflict, or simple inattention, but the consistent pattern indicates a stable “non‑punisher” stance. They do not appear to condition their non‑punishment on others’ behavior in any observable way: they refrain from punishing when there are clear low contributors in the group, when they themselves are targeted by punishers, and when their own contribution level is somewhat below the norm. This points to an absence of both altruistic punishment (enforcing cooperation norms against defectors) and retaliatory punishment (striking back at those who hurt them). If they hold any deterrence motive, it does not translate into action. Because punishers are identifiable, their continued refusal to punish suggests they may also care about not being perceived as aggressive or unfair by others, or they may simply prioritize keeping their own payoff intact over influencing group behavior. From a strategic standpoint, they accept being on the receiving end of others’ punishment rather than entering into costly punishment exchanges. In sum, they act as a “punishment-averse bystander”: they tolerate the existing sanction environment but do not participate in it.

The player is a frequent and willing punisher, often spending substantial resources to sanction specific targets. They focus punishment overwhelmingly on very low contributors (especially repeat non-contributors), and also sometimes target clear under-contributors relative to the group. Their pattern suggests a strong norm-enforcement motive: they repeatedly sanction the same chronic low contributors rather than spreading light punishment across many. They are willing to impose heavy total deductions on those players, even when others are already punishing them and the marginal deterrent effect is uncertain, implying that they value norm enforcement and/or retribution more than strict payoff efficiency. They also occasionally punish moderate, but noticeably below-average, contributions from otherwise cooperative players, indicating a relatively strict standard for what counts as acceptable. They almost never use punishment in ambiguous cases or against high contributors, and there is no evidence of spite, status competition, or retaliation (they are never punished themselves, so direct retaliation cannot be observed). Their punishment is one-sidedly negative; they do not pair it with rewards for high contributors. Overall, they act as a “tough enforcer”: selective, contribution-focused, but sometimes severe.

This player very rarely uses punishment and does not anchor it to clear contribution norms. Across the game they mostly refrain from punishing, even when some players contribute nothing or very little and are heavily punished by others. When they finally do punish, it is minimal in magnitude and targeted at a player who is actually contributing strongly and already receiving substantial positive and negative attention from others. Overall, they are a largely non-punitive, non-retributive type, willing to spend massively on rewards but almost never on sanctions.

This player never uses the punishment option, despite multiple opportunities and despite experiencing substantial punishment themselves when they free-ride. This suggests a very strong reluctance to incur costs to harm others, even when they could justify it as enforcing norms or deterring free-riding. Given the repeated absence of any punishment use, their type is best characterized as non-punitive. They tolerate both exploitation and chaotic high-cost behavior by others without attempting to discipline it. From behavior alone, the underlying motive is unknown, but the outcome is clear: they do not employ negative sanctions at all.

This player is willing to engage in low-cost, targeted punishment of very low contributors, especially those who repeatedly give nothing or close to nothing despite generally high group contributions. They selectively target such players and do not spread punishment widely or indiscriminately. Their punishment use is infrequent and modest in scale; they appear to rely on the fact that others are also punishing the same low contributors, and they “add a unit” rather than attempt to dominate the punishment dynamic. The pattern suggests a norm-enforcement motive more than retaliation: they continue to punish specific low contributors even when they themselves are not being punished by those individuals, and they do not punish players who occasionally contribute slightly below average but still materially to the public good. They do not use punishment to police people who spend large amounts on rewards or on punishing others; they focus on contribution levels. They also do not escalate punishment over time; they maintain roughly the same low-intensity approach when they choose to use it at all. This indicates a preference for mild deterrence rather than aggressive or emotional punishment.

The player is an active and sometimes heavy user of punishment, but their targeting is not neatly aligned with standard “altruistic punishment” of free-riders. They punish both relatively high and relatively low contributors, and they also punish some players who have not obviously hurt them materially. The pattern suggests that they use punishment as a tool to react to salient, possibly idiosyncratic cues (such as another’s high visibility, prior centrality in reward flows, or general behavior over time), rather than as a simple response to low contribution. They also punish some players multiple times, indicating persistence rather than one-off “warnings.” They are willing to pay substantial personal cost to impose large deductions, suggesting they value influencing others’ behavior or expressing disapproval even at the expense of their own payoff. There is no strong indication that deterrence is effective or even their main goal; punished players often continue their previous contribution or reward patterns. The player does not seem to reserve punishment only for those who directly punish or reward them, so pure retaliation is not the dominant motive. Instead, their punishment seems partly expressive (signaling disapproval), partly experimental (testing how others react), and only loosely connected to classical norm-enforcement against free-riding. Overall, they are punishment-prone, relatively unselective by standard efficiency criteria, and willing to incur high costs to shape or protest the group environment.

This player is an extremely heavy and persistent punisher. They are willing to spend large portions of their payoff, even to the point of strongly negative net payoffs, in order to impose very large deductions on certain targets. Their punishment is highly selective and strongly focused on specific low contributors, most notably the consistent minimal contributor and, at times, other very low or zero contributors. They also occasionally punish moderate but noticeably below-average contributors and very low one-off contributions. They target do not appear random: low or zero contributions reliably trigger punishment, and the same chronic low contributor is targeted repeatedly and escalatorily. They sometimes add other targets when several players contribute much less than what the punisher seems to consider appropriate. There are indications of both norm-enforcement and personal animus. The main chronic low contributor is punished far more intensely than any other player, beyond what is needed to signal displeasure or modestly deter. The heavy, repeated punishment even when others are also punishing the same target, and even when the cost to the punisher is enormous, points to a “moralistic avenger” or obsessive norm-enforcer type rather than a purely strategic punisher. They do not systematically punish high contributors or apparent over-punishers; antisocial or competitive punishment against cooperators is essentially absent. They also do not seem to punish those who punish them; their own punishers do not become targets, which suggests their motive is not retaliation for being hit but enforcement of a contribution norm. They use punishment almost exclusively; reward is rare and small by comparison. Even when the marginal effectiveness of punishment and reward differs, they overwhelmingly choose punishment to shape behavior. Their use of very large punishment against a few key violators, even late in the game and after repeated prior punishment, suggests they either do not update beliefs about the low likelihood of behavioral change, or they prioritize retribution/expressive sanctioning over behavioral impact.

They never use punishment despite frequent, visible use of both antisocial and norm-enforcing punishment by others. This strongly suggests a principled avoidance of costly harm rather than a lack of opportunity. They do not punish even very low public contributions by some peers, nor do they react punitively to extreme freeriders or to players pouring large resources into what looks like aggressive sanctioning. This implies low inclination toward spite or retaliation and a reluctance to engage in second-order punishment conflicts. Their non-use of punishment is consistent across the game, so they are not simply waiting for a particularly egregious trigger. Motives appear to prioritize payoff preservation, conflict avoidance, and perhaps a normative belief that punishment is either inefficient or socially corrosive in this setting. Any deterrence motive is weak or absent; they rely on their own contributions and occasional rewards rather than negative sanctions to shape others’ behavior.

They never use punishment, despite multiple opportunities and clear instances of others taking rather than contributing. This suggests a reluctance to engage in costly sanctioning, even when it would be efficient in deterring free-riding under the given parameters. The absence of any punishment also indicates they do not use punishment to shape norms, retaliate, or signal anger. Their type is best described as non-punitive and conflict-avoidant within this mechanism, with either low taste for norm enforcement costs or a preference for harmony and speed over corrective interventions.

They never use the punishment option, despite clear knowledge of its availability and a group context where some players occasionally defect. This consistent non-use indicates a strong reluctance to engage in costly negative sanctions. The absence of any attempt to punish even obvious noncontributors (including themselves, earlier, and other named low contributors later) suggests they do not view punishment as necessary, desirable, or worth the cost. There is no evidence that they try to use punishment for deterrence, norm reinforcement, or retaliation. They never use the punishment option, despite several opportunities where others’ non-contribution or confusion could justify sanctioning. There is no evidence of retaliatory tendencies or willingness to enforce norms via losses for others. Whether they abstain from punishment for moral reasons, discomfort with conflict, or simple payoff calculus is unknown, but their overall style points toward a preference for positive reinforcement and harmony over coercive enforcement.

They never use the punishment option, despite multiple opportunities and visible instances of non-contribution by specific players. This consistent non-use suggests a strong reluctance to incur personal cost to reduce others’ payoffs, even when punishment could be materially justified by free-riding. They also never threaten or discuss punishment in chat, indicating that they do not rely on punitive norms to sustain cooperation.
