The exact motive is uncertain, but they clearly do not act as a norm enforcer via punishment.

They never use punishment at any point, despite having multiple free-riding episodes by at least two players and a highly effective punishment technology. There is no evidence that they even experiment with a minimal sanction. This suggests a strong reluctance to spend resources on negative sanctions, regardless of group-level efficiency or availability of targets. The transcript does not show them verbally supporting or opposing punishment, so the underlying motive is unknown. Possible interpretations (low confidence) include dislike of conflict, preference for “hands-off” participation, or a view that as long as payoffs remain good and group cooperation is high, punishment is unnecessary. They do not use punishment instrumentally to deter or correct defectors, nor for retaliation, and they do not appear to treat punishment as part of their strategy set at all.

They are willing to use costly punishment and do so multiple times. Their punishment appears primarily norm-enforcing and deterrent rather than retaliatory. They punish in periods when some players clearly free-ride or contribute less than the emerging group standard, while they themselves maintain full contributions. They sometimes verbally reference or imply who is not contributing, then incur punishment costs in those same general contexts, matching a pattern of targeting defectors to sustain cooperation. There is no evidence they punish high contributors, nor that they retaliate against whoever punished them when they themselves receive a deduction; their contribution stays high and their tone toward the group remains positive. They also use mild social sanctions (calling attention to under-contributors) alongside formal punishment, suggesting a multi-channel enforcement style. Overall, they act as a conditional punisher: non-contribution or repeated deviation from the cooperative norm reliably triggers their willingness to pay for sanctions, with the apparent aim of protecting the efficient, high-cooperation outcome rather than maximizing their own short-term payoff.

They never use the punishment mechanism at any point, and they are never punished. This indicates a clear reluctance to spend resources on sanctions, even when free-riding occurs and others are explicitly calling for punishment. Their non-use of punishment is consistent both when cooperation is high and when there are visible defectors. They also never discuss punishment in chat, neither endorsing nor criticizing it. This behavior suggests they are either (a) efficiency-focused and reluctant to burn coins on costly punishment, (b) conflict-averse and uninterested in playing an enforcement role, or (c) indifferent to norm enforcement as long as their own payoff remains high. Their type is best characterized as a cooperative non-punisher: they contribute heavily but rely on others, if anyone, to enforce norms, and do not engage in retaliation or deterrence themselves. Their beliefs about the effectiveness or morality of punishment remain unknown, but operationally they act as a consistent abstainer from costly sanctions.

They never use the punishment mechanism, despite having multiple opportunities and experiencing deductions themselves after they free-ride. They appear to understand that punishment is occurring and why, but they do not retaliate or join in sanctioning others. Even when they are directly targeted with deductions after defection, they do not respond with counter-punishment, suggesting low inclination toward revenge and a willingness to accept punishment as a consequence of deviating from group norms. They rely on social talk and internal adjustment more than on institutional sanctions to support cooperation. Overall, they act as a cooperative but non-enforcing participant: willing to benefit from the presence of punishment in the environment, but not personally inclined to wield it.

They are willing but selective and somewhat opaque in their use of punishment. Their punishment is not publicly coordinated; they never explicitly announce who they plan to punish or threaten punishment in advance. The most plausible trigger is perceived unfairness or norm violation: they punish during phases when some players are not contributing while the group is trying to maintain an “all in” standard, and they verbally support fairness and “being fair” in chat. They also sometimes talk as though others are “taking” their coins, which suggests they interpret punishment against them as theft-like, but there is no evidence they retaliate specifically; they do not accuse or target particular avatars in chat. Instead, they likely use punishment as quiet second-order enforcement of contribution norms rather than as overt revenge. Their threshold for punishing seems higher than that of more punitive players; most of the time they tolerate minor deviations and focus on contributing rather than repeatedly sanctioning.

This player is a willing and strategically minded punisher who uses punishment as a tool both for enforcing contribution norms and for reacting to perceived unjust treatment. They are prepared to pay a significant cost to punish, and they do so in discrete, purposeful bursts rather than continuously. They are not hyper-punitive: many opportunities are left unused even when others contribute less, indicating they do not punish every deviation from equal contributions. They target both apparent free-riders and at least one counterpart who has previously punished them, which suggests a mix of norm-enforcement and retaliation motives. They are willing to punish multiple players simultaneously when both appear to fall short of their cooperation standard, and they also at times punish a single counterpart selectively. The timing and intensity of punishment changes after they themselves are punished: they escalate from initially not using the mechanism to later imposing substantial deductions, consistent with a retaliatory or defensive dimension. They appear to accept the cost-inefficiency of punishment, using it even when it clearly lowers their own immediate payoff. This supports an interpretation that they value enforcing fairness or expressing disapproval enough to sacrifice material earnings. However, they do not turn to constant vendetta; there are stretches without punishment after prior punishment use, suggesting that if others’ behavior stabilizes within an acceptable range, they suspend further sanctions rather than punish for its own sake.

This player is willing to use costly punishment, but only at the start and then not at all later. The early use of punishment coincides with clear deviations from full cooperation by others, consistent with norm-enforcing behavior rather than random aggression. Once group cooperation stabilizes at the full-contribution level, the player ceases to punish entirely, suggesting that they primarily punish observable free-riding and do not engage in gratuitous or retaliatory punishment. They do not escalate punishment after being personally punished; instead, they continue contributing and stop spending on punishment, implying low emphasis on revenge and higher emphasis on maintaining cooperation at minimal personal cost. Their early punishment pattern is selective and directed toward under-contributors (inferred from the structure of outcomes), consistent with a “pro-social punisher” type that initially tries to discipline defectors, then withdraws from punishing once group behavior improves, possibly to conserve resources. Their sensitivity to receiving antisocial punishment is limited in behavioral response; they do not respond in kind, which suggests either a forgiving stance or a decision to prioritize earnings and stability over retribution. The exact beliefs they form about who punished them are unknown, as punishers are anonymous.

This player is clearly willing to punish and accepts substantial personal cost to do so. They selectively target clear free-riding or under-contribution, focusing punishment on those who contribute nothing when others (including themselves) contribute fully. They do not punish when all group members cooperate at the high level, even if they themselves have recently been punished by someone in the group; this strongly suggests that their primary motive is norm enforcement and deterrence of free riding, not broad retaliation or spite. When there is a single low contributor in an otherwise cooperative group, they consistently direct punishment toward that individual, and they cease punishing them once that player shifts to full cooperation. This pattern indicates conditional, behavior-based targeting rather than fixed animosity. They are willing to spend enough in some episodes to impose severe deductions on free riders, accepting an inefficient personal cost in order to send a strong signal and reduce or eliminate freeriding in subsequent rounds. They do not show evidence of “anti-social punishment”: they never punish high contributors, and they do not punish merely because someone punished them earlier. The balance of evidence points to a “strong altruistic punisher” type: willing to pay to enforce contribution norms, but restrained when the norm is actually being met.

The player never uses the punishment mechanism, even when they are exploited by free-riders and directly harmed by targeted deductions. They do not punish low contributors, nor do they punish those who punish them or other cooperators. This indicates a strong reluctance to incur personal costs for sanctioning, regardless of whether the motive would be norm-enforcement or retaliation. The data do not reveal whether this stems from a general aversion to punishment, a belief that punishment is ineffective, or an effort to avoid escalating conflict; the underlying motive is unknown. However, behaviorally they function as a non-punishing cooperator: they prefer to maintain contributions rather than enforce norms or seek revenge via costly sanctions.

This player never uses the punishment option, despite multiple opportunities and clear instances where others defect or punish them. This consistent non-use suggests a strong reluctance to spend personal resources on sanctioning, even when it might be normatively or strategically justifiable. They appear to adopt a “no-punishment” stance rather than selectively punishing free-riders or retaliating against aggressors. From behavior alone, it is unclear whether this is driven by a principled aversion to harming others, a belief that punishment is inefficient or ineffective, or simple payoff-maximization. However, they remain cooperative even without punishing, which weakens a pure selfish-payoff explanation and suggests either moral discomfort with punishment or skepticism about its social value. They do not engage in second-order punishment against players who punish them or others, and they also refrain from punishing clear free-riders. Their conduct is consistent with a type who prefers to regulate cooperation through their own contributions and strategic adjustment, not through costly sanctions.

The player is willing to pay substantial personal costs to punish behavior they perceive as unfair or uncooperative, even when the target’s action benefits the group. Their punishment decisions are selective and asymmetric: they target those whose behavior deviates from what they appear to regard as the appropriate contribution level, regardless of whether that contribution is above or below the group average. This pattern suggests a strong, but somewhat idiosyncratic, norm-enforcement motive that is not purely about raising public contributions in a simple conditional-cooperator way. At times, they punish free riders who contribute nothing while others contribute, which is consistent with typical altruistic punishment aimed at deterring exploitation. At other times, they punish a player who contributes less than peers within a generally cooperative context, which could reflect intolerance of deviation, perceived opportunism, or a focus on relative fairness rather than absolute contribution. Their punishment is not used for retaliation, as they are never punished themselves; it instead appears primarily forward‑looking and normative, intended to shape or discipline others’ behavior. Once broad cooperation is in place and the end of the game is reached, they stop punishing, indicating that they treat punishment as an investment in future rounds rather than as a standing moral obligation.

They never use the punishment option, even when they are a clear free-rider relative to others, and even when others have punished them previously. This indicates a strong reluctance to spend resources on norm enforcement or retaliation. They do not attempt to discipline defectors when someone else free-rides, and they also abstain from “antisocial” punishment of cooperators. The absence of punishment holds across situations where punishment could increase relative payoffs or serve as revenge. Their behavior is most consistent with a “non-punisher” type: they prioritize keeping all their own tokens and accept others’ behavior—cooperative or not—without imposing costly sanctions. Whether this stems from fairness beliefs (e.g., viewing punishment as inappropriate) or a purely payoff-maximizing aversion to costly punishment is unknown.

They are generally reluctant to use punishment and almost never act as a normative enforcer. For most of the game they do not spend on punishment at all, even when someone else deviates from the group norm or when they themselves are the clear free-rider. They eventually do spend on punishment briefly, but this does not follow a clear pattern of enforcing cooperation or punishing the worst offender; instead, it coincides with a period when they themselves are not contributing. This timing makes it ambiguous whether their punishment is retaliatory, experimental, or driven by some idiosyncratic reaction rather than clean moralistic enforcement. They do not escalate punishment after being targeted earlier, suggesting a low propensity for spiteful revenge. They also do not try to deter low contributors systematically, even when it would be profitable and socially supported. Overall, they appear punishment-averse and inconsistent: they mostly avoid the cost, and when they do punish, the target choice and motive remain unclear (low confidence).

The player is generally reluctant to punish but not completely averse to it. They almost never spend on punishment despite having multiple opportunities. On one occasion, when a group member free-rides while others continue to cooperate, they do pay a small cost to punish. This suggests a weak but present willingness to enforce norms when cooperation is violated in a clear and salient way. Their punishment use is selective, low-intensity, and seemingly aimed at norm reinforcement rather than aggression or retaliation; there is no pattern of repeated or escalating sanctions. When they themselves later receive a small punishment despite consistent cooperation, they do not respond with counter-punishment or visible anger. This supports an interpretation of them as a “soft enforcer”: willing to send a signal when necessary but preferring to conserve resources and rely on social coordination and forgiveness once behavior improves.

They never use the punishment option, even when they face clear opportunities to punish clear free‑riding or unexplained sanctioning against themselves and others. Their non‑use of punishment persists across contexts: when everyone cooperates, when a single player contributes zero, and when someone punishes them despite their full cooperation. This suggests either a principled aversion to costly punishment, a belief that punishment is unnecessary given the group’s high cooperation, or a preference to avoid interpersonal conflict even when anonymous. There is no evidence of targeted or retaliatory motives; they do not respond punitively when they are personally harmed nor when fairness is violated. They also do not appear to use punishment strategically as a deterrent, even though the technology is relatively efficient in terms of impact per unit cost. The safest characterization is “non‑punishing, non‑retaliatory cooperator” who treats punishment as outside their behavioral repertoire, either for moral, pragmatic, or conflict‑avoidant reasons (exact motive unknown).

The player never spends any resources on punishment, even when others clearly defect or when targetable free-riding or confusing behavior occurs. This indicates a strong reluctance to engage in costly sanctioning. Their type fits a “non-punishing cooperator”: they contribute fully but leave norm enforcement to others, if it occurs at all. It is unclear whether they refrain because they dislike harming others, because they prioritize their own earnings, or because they believe the cooperative norm is already stable without their intervention. They also do not engage in retaliatory punishment; when others’ choices or punishment decisions reduce their payoff, they still neither punish nor adjust their contribution, which suggests they do not use punishment instrumentally for deterrence or revenge.

They are a frequent, targeted punisher and never use reward. Their punishment is strongly tied to contribution behavior: they almost exclusively punish players who contribute nothing or less than others, including when those low contributors earn high payoffs. They also sometimes sanction a player who has been repeatedly free-riding or mixing in defection even when that player is not uniquely worst in the current period, suggesting a memory-based, history-sensitive rule rather than a purely myopic response. They are selective: they do not punish when everyone contributes fully, and they do not appear to use “antisocial” punishment against high contributors or unconditional cooperators. Punishment is directed at those who fall below what appears to be their normative standard of full contribution, especially when that behavior is repeated. When more than one player defects, they often punish multiple defectors, indicating willingness to bear significant cost to enforce what they see as fair behavior. They accept substantial personal cost to punish, even in rounds where they themselves could have improved payoffs by abstaining from sanctioning. This pattern is consistent with strong norm-enforcement or inequality-aversion motives: they seem to value reducing the payoff advantage of free-riders and signalling disapproval over strictly maximizing their own earnings. The pattern of punishment against some players (notably one persistent low contributor) escalates over time, including using multiple units in a single round. This suggests a deterrence or “teaching” motive: they appear to try to force chronic defectors into compliance. However, they do not systematically retaliate against those who punish them; their sanctions continue to track contribution behavior rather than who targeted them earlier, implying punishment is primarily norm-enforcing rather than revenge-based.

They never spend any resources on punishment, despite extensive opportunities and a clear pattern of others using punishment heavily. This strongly suggests a firm aversion to paying for norm enforcement, regardless of whether someone defects or cooperates. They do not punish even after being repeatedly sanctioned for free-riding, so there is no evidence of retaliatory punishment. It also implies that they are comfortable letting others bear the cost of norm enforcement and are willing to operate in an environment of asymmetric punishment, where they can be sanctioned but will not respond in kind.

They never use the punishment option, even though the punishment technology is relatively efficient and even though they can clearly identify who they could punish. They are generally reluctant to use punishment and do so only in a very focused, norm‑enforcing way. They are very willing to use costly punishment and do so at substantial intensity, but in a highly targeted, norm-enforcing way. Punishment is central to their identity in this game. They are a very heavy, proactive punisher with a clear focus on persistent, extreme free-riding. Overall, they embody a “strong altruistic punisher” type who willingly sacrifices substantial personal income to uphold contribution norms and to express disapproval of greedy or unresponsive behavior.

They are quite willing to punish, especially in the early and mid game, and they focus their punishment almost exclusively on the chronic non-contributor. They are highly willing to use costly punishment and do so early, repeatedly, and at nontrivial cost to themselves. They consistently abstain from using punishment, even when there are clear, repeatedly identified free-riders and when a large portion of the group explicitly coordinates to “nuke” the main defector. The player is an active, norm-enforcing punisher who is clearly willing to incur personal cost to sanction others. This player never uses the punishment option despite repeated and substantial free-riding and despite the presence of a highly effective punishment technology. Punishment is central to this player’s social strategy. This player never uses punishment despite multiple opportunities, including occasions when some peers contribute very little relative to others. The player is clearly willing to punish and uses it selectively to enforce contribution norms rather than randomly or vindictively. This suggests they prioritize preserving their own payoff and/or avoiding direct harm to others over enforcing contribution norms through sanctions. Their motive appears neither retaliatory nor deterrent; they simply abstain. Whether they morally disapprove of punishment, do not notice or understand the mechanism, or strategically free-ride on others’ punishing efforts is unknown, but the consistency of non-use points toward a type that does not self-appoint as norm enforcer.

They are willing to punish and do so selectively and purposefully, but infrequently. When they punish, the target is a clear free-rider relative to the group norm, and their action aligns with the group’s emerging norm that non-contributors should be punished. They never punish cooperators or ambiguous cases, and they never use punishment to respond to personal attacks or retaliation (they are never attacked themselves). Their punishment intensity is moderate: they do not spend extreme amounts of coins, but they invest enough to impose a noticeable deduction. This fits a norm-enforcing motive: they sacrifice some payoff to discipline obvious defectors, not to maximize personal earnings. They abstain from punishment in many rounds where others are already punishing, which may indicate reliance on others to enforce norms when the job is “covered,” or a desire to minimize personal punishment cost. They do not escalate over time; their pattern is sporadic and task-focused. Their final punishment episode continues to target a fresh free-rider, consistent with a sustained principle rather than early-round experimentation. There is no evidence they use punishment for revenge or status; they never appear to punish someone who had previously punished them, and there is no mis-targeting. Overall, they are a restrained norm enforcer: willing to sanction non-cooperation, but not eager or vindictive.

They never use the punishment option, even when others free-ride systematically or when there is a clear group push to punish non-contributors. They refrain from punishing both clear defectors and ambiguous cases, and they do not punish in retaliation when they themselves are targeted. This suggests a strong reluctance to incur costs to harm others, regardless of efficiency arguments or group norms advocating “deduct from non-contributors.” Their behavior fits a non-punitive cooperator: they support the public good but avoid enforcing norms through sanctions. Motive-wise, this could stem from a preference against harming others, from a desire to conserve their payoff, or from skepticism about the value of punishment; the exact reason is unknown. They also do not appear to engage in antisocial punishment.

The player uses costly punishment and is willing to sacrifice some payoff to reduce others’ earnings. Given that they themselves are a high contributor, the most plausible trigger is others’ relatively low contributions, pointing toward a norm-enforcing or inequality-averse motive rather than retaliation against personal harm. The use of punishment in a one-shot, anonymous setting suggests they are prepared to engage in second-order cooperation: paying to discipline perceived free riders even without future personal benefit. However, the exact targeting pattern (who is punished, how strongly, and whether proportional to deviation) is unknown from the available data, so the selectivity and precision of their punitive behavior remain uncertain.

The player chooses not to use costly punishment, despite clear evidence that some others contribute very little or nothing. This suggests a reluctance to engage in costly sanctioning, at least in a single-round context with no future interaction. Their non-use of punishment could indicate a preference for non-intervention, indifference to norm enforcement, a belief that punishment is not worth the cost, or simple inattention to the punishment option; which of these applies is unknown. There is no sign of retaliatory motives, norm-enforcing behavior, or targeted deterrence. Their selectivity in who to punish, if they were to punish in richer settings, is unknown.

This player chooses not to use the punishment mechanism, even when clear free riding and very low contributions are observable. They absorb inequality in contributions without engaging in costly sanctions. This suggests a reluctance to spend personal resources to discipline defectors, at least under these conditions. Their non-use of punishment is not targeted or selective; it is a complete abstention from sanctioning in the observed context. The motive behind this is uncertain: it could reflect a general preference against harming others, skepticism about the efficiency of costly punishment, indifference to norm enforcement, or a belief that, given the one-shot structure and anonymity of punishers, sanctions are unlikely to produce meaningful behavior change. They also do not appear to punish out of anger or desire for retaliation, despite the presence of clear free riders. Overall, they behave as a non-punishing cooperator: willing to contribute fully, but unwilling to incur additional cost to reduce others’ payoffs. Whether they would punish in multi-round contexts to deter future free riding is unknown and would depend on learning and expectations not observable here.

The player opts not to use the punishment mechanism at all. This occurs despite the presence of clearly identifiable very low and zero contributors in the group. Given that punishment is both possible and relatively effective, their inaction suggests a reluctance to impose costs on others, even when some behave in a way that many would classify as exploitative. They neither engage in targeted sanctioning nor low-level “symbolic” punishment. This is consistent with a non-punitive, tolerance-oriented cooperative type who prefers to bear the cost of others’ free-riding rather than initiate hostility or reduce anyone’s payoff. There is no evidence of retaliatory motives, norm-enforcement behavior, or attempts to deter future defection, though in a single-round game future deterrence is moot. Whether they would punish in a repeated setting for long-run norm maintenance is unknown, but based on current behavior their baseline tendency in new environments is likely to be: contribute generously and refrain from costly punishment, even in the face of clear under-contribution.

The player does not use the punishment mechanism, despite the presence of several clear low contributors. They incur punishment from someone else but do not retaliate within the available decision structure. This non-use of punishment could indicate one or more of the following, though exact motives are uncertain: a reluctance to spend personal resources on harming others, a preference for unilateral cooperation over enforcement, a tolerance for free-riding, or simple inattention or indifference to the punishment stage. They do not selectively target anyone, so their threshold for punitive action and their views on proportionality or norm enforcement are unknown. Their behavior is consistent with a type who cooperates but does not enforce cooperation through costly sanctions, even when directly harmed. Their primary mode of social influence appears to be cooperative contribution rather than punishment.

They choose not to use costly punishment, despite multiple very low contributors being present. This indicates either reluctance to engage in costly sanctioning, a preference to avoid conflict, or a cost–benefit calculation that sanctions are not worthwhile in a one-shot environment. Their baseline appears to be “non-punishing cooperator”: they cooperate themselves but do not enforce norms through monetary penalties. There is no evidence of retaliatory motives, as they are not targeted by punishment. Selectivity in punishment targets, sensitivity to degrees of free-riding, and use of punishment for deterrence vs expression of anger remain unknown.

The player chooses not to use costly punishment despite substantial variation in others’ cooperation levels. This indicates a reluctance to spend personal resources to sanction free-riding, at least under uncertainty about others’ identities and future interactions. Their non-use of punishment appears consistent with a focus on positive coordination and efficiency rather than on retribution or norm enforcement via costs. They might view punishment as either inefficient, unnecessary, or ineffective in this anonymous, one-shot setting; however, their explicit beliefs about the mechanism are unknown. The player shows no sign of retaliatory motives or anger-driven sanctioning; they accept the outcome of heterogeneous contributions without attempting to correct it through deductions. Their default type in punishment-enabled environments is therefore a high cooperator who prefers persuasion and principled example-setting over costly punitive action.

This player is an active and often heavy punisher, but their punishment is strongly anti-social and focused on exploiting the punishment mechanism rather than enforcing cooperation. They frequently spend non-trivial amounts on punishment. The pattern of who gets punished (inferred from others’ repeated heavy deductions) indicates that they systematically target the main low contributor in the group. That target is often already punished harshly by several others, yet this player continues to add further punishment even when it severely reduces the target’s payoff to very low or negative levels. This suggests a taste for aggressive sanctioning of that one defector rather than a calibrated, efficiency-oriented approach. However, the key strategic feature is that they themselves never contribute and still punish a lower contributor. When the group is mostly cooperative and one player is contributing less, they join or amplify the punishment against that player while remaining a free-rider. This is classic “second-order free-riding on punishment norms”: they gain from cooperative norms maintained by others’ contributions and also participate in punishing an identified scapegoat, while not upholding the cooperation standard they enforce on others. Their punishment is not used to deter peers from free-riding in a symmetrical way: they do not start contributing once others are generous, nor do they seem to punish other non-contributors systematically unless those non-contributors become salient targets. The intensity ramps up and down over time, suggesting some adaptation to the prevailing punishment climate (e.g., sometimes joining large coalitions of punishers, sometimes backing off when they are heavily punished themselves), but they do not restructure their basic strategy. They are also willing to incur substantial costs from punishment cycles. At several points they both punish and get punished heavily, drastically reducing their payoffs, yet they do not respond by contributing more or ceasing to punish altogether. This indicates low sensitivity to the financial inefficiency of punishment wars; they appear comfortable participating in costly punishment dynamics as long as they can continue to exploit contribution behavior. Overall, their punishment style is opportunistic and norm-exploitative rather than norm-protective: they leverage punishment to attack an under-contributing peer while never meeting that standard themselves.

This player is willing to punish, but in a restrained and highly targeted way. They almost exclusively direct punishment toward a chronic low contributor, consistent with standard “altruistic punishment” of a free rider rather than random or spiteful behavior. They do not escalate to extreme, payoff-destroying levels of punishment when others do; instead, they apply small to moderate amounts at a few points, suggesting a focus on signaling norms or contributing to group-enforced discipline rather than trying to crush the target. They do not punish high contributors, even when those players engage in large punishment campaigns of their own; this indicates that contribution level, not punishment behavior or relative payoff, is their primary trigger. There is no sign of them retaliating against others for punishing them when they defect, even though they themselves get punished as a free rider at times. This lack of apparent revenge suggests low emphasis on personal vindication and higher emphasis on contribution-based fairness. Overall, their punishment use fits a norm-enforcing, deterrence-oriented type with moderate cost tolerance and some willingness to personally bear costs to sanction a visible defector, but without extreme zeal.

This player never uses the punishment mechanism. Despite having many opportunities and seeing active use of punishment by others—often directed at them for free-riding—they consistently choose to spend nothing on sanctions. They do not engage in second-order punishment (punishing defectors) or counter-punishment (retaliating against those who reduce their payoff). This holds even when they are heavily and repeatedly targeted. From behavior alone, it is unknown whether this reflects principled rejection of costly punishment, indifference to norm enforcement, or a purely self-interested calculation that punishment is not worth the cost. Given that they frequently defect in cooperative environments and still never punish, they likely do not see themselves as a norm enforcer or protector of fairness. If they reason strategically, they may view punishment as a dominated action: it reduces their own payoff without reliably changing others’ behavior in a way that benefits them personally. In new PGG settings, they are best modeled as a non-punisher: they do not use sanctions for deterrence, reciprocity, or emotional payback, even under persistent provocation.

This player is a frequent and sometimes heavy user of punishment, willing to sacrifice substantial payoff to deter or sanction behavior they disapprove of. They clearly accept the personal cost of punishing and show no consistent trend toward abandoning punishment over time; instead, they intermittently commit sizable amounts of their available payoff to it. Across the game, their pattern is consistent with a norm-enforcement role rather than a passive cooperator. They appear to target two main categories of behavior: 1. Clear free-riding on the public good, especially by players who repeatedly contribute nothing even when most others contribute fully. 2. Less cooperative actions within a largely cooperative environment, where a minority of low contributors stand out against a high-contribution norm. Their willingness to spend nontrivial amounts on punishment, including in situations where cooperation is already high and group earnings are strong, suggests that they value norm enforcement for its own sake and may derive psychological benefit from seeing defectors lose, over and above monetary payoff maximization. They sometimes invest heavily enough that the punishment is not obviously payoff-maximizing for them personally in that round, which is consistent with strong altruistic punishment. They do not engage in blanket or indiscriminate punishment. There are many rounds in which they refrain from punishing even though the option is available. This implies some selectivity: they likely punish when others’ choices cross a certain internal threshold of perceived unfairness or exploitation, but not automatically whenever someone contributes less than them. Their punishment is not purely retaliatory in the sense of reacting to punishment directed at them: they continue punishing in rounds where they are not themselves targeted, and they sometimes do not punish even after being punished. This indicates that their primary motive is enforcement of contribution norms rather than direct revenge. They also do not appear to punish high contributors or generally cooperative players, so there is no strong evidence of antisocial punishment from them. When all or nearly all others cooperate at high levels, they either punish selectively (targeting the lone defector) or abstain from punishment entirely. Overall, they fit the profile of a principled altruistic punisher: they are willing to incur significant costs to reduce the payoffs of those they see as undermining collective cooperation.

This player is an unusually heavy and escalating punisher, clearly willing to sacrifice substantial own payoff to sanction others. They do not punish in every round, but when they do, they sometimes spend very large amounts, up to the maximum feasible, resulting in extremely severe deductions for at least one target. The pattern strongly suggests that low contributors are their main trigger: the most extreme punishment episodes align with situations where a specific group member persistently contributes nothing while others, including the focal player, contribute fully. The player appears willing to keep investing in punishment across multiple later periods even though it is personally costly and they have already tried it before. This suggests a norm‑enforcing or retributive motive rather than a narrow cost–benefit calculation. At the same time, they do not punish constantly or indiscriminately. There are many periods with clear free‑riding where they refrain from punishing, indicating some selectivity: they may prioritize particularly egregious or repeated offenders, or react strongly after tolerance has been exhausted. They continue to punish even after they themselves become a target of punishment, but there is no clear signal they are retaliating against whoever punished them, since punishers are anonymous and their focus remains aligned with known defectors rather than with changing patterns of who receives punishment. Overall, they behave like a principled but sometimes extreme altruistic punisher: they are prepared to incur large personal costs to reduce defectors’ payoffs, with uncertain attention to whether this is actually effective as deterrence.

They never use the punishment option despite clear opportunities and social license to do so. Even when others defect moderately or when they themselves are targeted by punishment, they refrain from retaliating or from trying to “enforce” cooperation on others. This consistent non-use suggests an aversion to spending resources on sanctions and/or a preference for maintaining a friendly, non-confrontational climate. They seem to rely on talk, explanation, and humor rather than punitive action to manage tensions. There is no evidence they use punishment for deterrence, norm enforcement, or revenge; punishment abstention appears stable throughout. Their behavior fits a “non-punishing cooperator” type who contributes generously but does not take on the enforcer role.

They never use the available punishment mechanism at any point, despite multiple opportunities and several clear instances of under-contribution by others. They also do not publicly encourage others to punish, even when another player suggests coordinated deductions against a repeat low contributor. This indicates a strong reluctance to engage in costly sanctioning, even when such punishment might be normatively justifiable or potentially beneficial for group cooperation. Their consistent abstention from punishment, together with sustained high contributions, fits a “non-punishing cooperator” type: they are willing to shoulder the cost of cooperation but not willing to incur additional costs to discipline defectors. The pattern suggests they prioritize harmony, earnings efficiency for themselves, or emotional comfort over norm enforcement. They may view punishment as unnecessary as long as overall cooperation remains high, or as socially undesirable regardless of instrumentality. They also appear insensitive to the deterrence role of punishment. Even when a specific player repeatedly contributes zero while others maintain high contributions, this player neither punishes nor visibly adjusts their contribution in a targeted way. This indicates that they rely more on a stable cooperative heuristic and social conversation than on formal sanctioning institutions to sustain cooperation. In a different PGG with punishment, they are likely to almost never punish, reserving it, if at all, for very extreme or persistent exploitation; based on current evidence, however, such thresholds remain unknown.

This player is a selective but willing punisher of clear free-riding. They almost never use the punishment mechanism, but they do pay to punish in three distinct situations, each aligned with conspicuously low contributions: once against a complete non-contributor who had previously been part of the cooperative norm, once against a player who gives nothing after being identified in chat as repeatedly “bailing,” and once with a stronger punishment against a repeat zero-contributor after prior warning talk from others. The punishments are targeted and proportionate: they never punish medium contributors or minor deviations, and they do not use punishment in response to boredom, jokes, or experimentation when others quickly return to full cooperation. The pattern indicates a deterrence motive tied to norm enforcement rather than retaliation or spite, and a readiness to incur a modest personal cost to protect high collective payoffs.

They never use the punishment option despite having a clear opportunity and at least one peer contributing less than others in each round. This suggests a reluctance to incur personal cost to sanction others, even when there is a target whose behavior could plausibly justify punishment under common fairness norms. The absence of punishment may indicate a non-confrontational, low-aggression style, a belief that mild deviation does not warrant sanctions, or a preference to adjust their own contribution rather than police others. They also do not engage in retaliatory punishment even after being punished themselves, implying that vengeance is not a driving motive, at least in this short horizon. Because the game is very short and information about others’ payoffs is limited, it is unclear whether they would ever adopt more active deterrence strategies in longer settings.

They are strongly willing to use costly punishment and do so even when the marginal benefit to their own payoff is zero. They selectively target lower contributors rather than punishing broadly, indicating they use punishment as norm enforcement rather than indiscriminate aggression. The threshold for punishment is “contributes less than my own standard,” with harsher punishment directed at the lowest contributors and repeated punishment toward a consistent low contributor. This suggests a clear internal rule: full or near-full contributors are acceptable; noticeably lower contributors deserve sanctions. The pattern indicates a deterrence or norm-policing motive, not retaliation, because they are never punished by others yet still punish. They also never escalate into antisocial punishment against high contributors. The willingness to sacrifice a meaningful portion of earnings to sanction others points to a strong preference for contribution norm enforcement over short-term material gain, and a temperament that tolerates or endorses confrontation to correct behavior.

They never use punishment, even when there are clear lower contributors relative to themselves. This suggests a strong reluctance or disinterest in costly sanctions. They do not appear motivated by deterrence, norm enforcement, or retaliation within the observed context. Their threshold for punishing free-riding is either very high or they generally reject punishment as a tool, possibly viewing it as inefficient or contrary to their cooperative norm. They also do not use punishment strategically to influence future behavior, at least over the short horizon observed. Whether this is driven by moral aversion, cost–benefit reasoning, or indifference is unknown.

They never use punishment, even though the punishment technology is very powerful relative to its cost and they repeatedly observe clear low contributors. There is no evidence of them using sanctioning either as deterrence (to shape future behavior) or as retaliation (to punish past behavior). This consistent abstention suggests several possible features of their type: - They likely dislike burning their own coins for normative enforcement, even when it would be instrumentally effective. They behave as if the personal cost (or perhaps the perceived social cost) of punishing outweighs any expected benefit. - They do not appear to be a “moralist” or strong norm enforcer: they tolerate free‑riding and contribution inequality without intervening. - They also do not experiment with punishment, which implies they either do not believe sanctions are necessary, do not expect them to be effective in this environment, or simply have a strong aversion to engaging in punitive actions at all. Because they are never punished themselves, we cannot infer how they would react to being targeted. We also cannot tell whether their avoidance of punishment is driven more by empathy/reluctance to harm others or by instrumental calculation about payoffs; both remain plausible. What is clear is that they adopt a purely cooperative strategy without any enforcement component and appear content to let others behave as they choose.

The player never uses punishment, despite having multiple salient opportunities: they observe clear low contributors and at times suffer substantial punishment themselves, yet they do not retaliate or try to enforce the norm on others. This persistent non-use across both cooperative and less cooperative phases indicates a stable tendency rather than oversight. Their refusal to punish holds even when punishment is economically potent (each unit is highly damaging relative to its cost) and when they might plausibly benefit from deterring free-riders or retaliating for sanctions against them. This suggests either a strong aversion to spending own resources on harming others, a belief that others will handle norm enforcement, or low interest in social control relative to own immediate earnings. There is no evidence they selectively punish only extreme free-riders or only retaliate; instead, punishment is entirely absent. From this, one can infer they are not a second-order enforcer type. They rely on others to bear punishment costs and adapt their own contribution in response, rather than engaging in deterrence themselves. Their motivation appears more self-regarding than retributive: they accept being punished and adjust contributions at times, but do not invest in changing others’ behavior through costly sanctions.

This player uses punishment selectively and normatively, targeting clear low contributors rather than using punishment broadly. They never punish high or average contributors, avoiding antisocial punishment. Early on, they punish a player who contributes nothing and later repeatedly punish another player who consistently contributes far below the group. Their targets are the clearest free-riders or near free-riders in the contribution profile. The player rarely uses punishment overall, suggesting they treat it as a costly tool for enforcing minimum cooperation standards rather than as a routine strategy. When they do punish, they are willing to spend enough to deliver substantial impact on a target, especially when a specific player repeatedly under-contributes. This pattern supports a deterrence motive: they appear to want to raise that player’s contributions or signal that very low contributions are unacceptable. They do not use punishment for retaliation against those who are merely slightly below average, nor do they use it for revenge against anyone identifiable as having punished them. Their punishment behavior seems driven by contribution norms, not by direct interpersonal conflict. They also never combine punishment with reward, choosing only the negative sanction channel; this may indicate a belief that punishment is more efficient or more necessary than positive reinforcement in shaping group behavior, given the payoff structure (punishment being much more impactful per unit).

This player almost never uses punishment despite having access to a strong punishment technology. For nearly the entire interaction, they tolerate free-riding and low contributions without spending personal coins to sanction others. This restraint holds even when there are repeated near-zero contributors, suggesting a reluctance to incur personal cost for norm enforcement and/or a belief that punishment is not worth it in this setting. They eventually punish exactly once, targeting a player who consistently contributes almost nothing. The choice of target is aligned with standard fairness norms: they punish the clearest, persistent free-rider rather than medium contributors or high contributors. This selectivity indicates that, when they do punish, their motive is likely norm enforcement rather than spite or random aggression. The timing—waiting until very late to punish—suggests a threshold-based trigger: they endure ongoing free-riding for a long time and only resort to punishment when patience is exhausted or when they perceive that gentle conditional cooperation alone is not changing behavior. Because they do not punish mid-level cooperators and never use “counter-punishment,” there is no evidence of reactive retaliation. Their punishment appears one-sided (downward) and principle-based: targeted at the worst offender, minimal in intensity, and not part of a broader punitive strategy. Overall, they are a reluctant, last-resort punisher who prioritizes conserving resources over actively deterring others.

The player is a selective, norm-enforcing punisher with a narrow target set and a focus on deterring low contributions rather than retaliating for personal harm. They rarely use punishment, but when they do, they direct it toward conspicuous under-contributors relative to the surrounding group, especially when those under-contributions are stark against a highly cooperative backdrop. This indicates a simple norm: when the group is broadly contributing at moderate to high levels, very low contributors deserve sanction. Their punishment is individually targeted rather than spread; they typically hit one or a small number of the worst deviators rather than all imperfect players. They do not punish average contributors, and there is no evidence that they punish in response to being personally punished (they never punish in a direct “revenge” pattern following deductions to themselves). This absence of tit-for-tat retaliation, combined with the fact that they sometimes punish while themselves contributing only moderately (not maximally), suggests a “second-order free-rider punisher” style: they are willing to enforce cooperation norms on others even when they are not at the cooperative ceiling themselves. They do not appear to use punishment instrumentally to raise their own future payoffs in a sophisticated way; there is no clear subsequent coordinated response by the target players that benefits them specifically. Instead, the pattern fits a basic fairness or norm-enforcement motive: visible low contributions in an otherwise cooperative context trigger sanctions, regardless of whether punishing is immediately profitable. They are not a heavy or frequent punisher, so the psychological cost or perceived monetary cost of punishing is nontrivial, but in salient norm-violation situations they are willing to pay it. Their punishing is forward-looking rather than expressive retaliation: being punished themselves does not make them more punitive in later rounds, and there is no sign that they try to identify and punish their own punishers. Low-confidence inference: they may implicitly believe that visible norm-enforcement by some players is needed to sustain cooperation, but they do not assume it is their constant responsibility to do so.

This player is a rare, targeted punisher who uses punishment in a norm-enforcing, non-retaliatory manner. They almost never spend on punishment, despite being punished often; when they do punish, they direct it at a specific player who contributes markedly less than most others in that moment. This indicates that their trigger for punishment is visible under-contribution relative to the group, not personal grievance or vendetta. They do not respond to being punished by counter-punishing those who likely sanctioned them, even when they suffer large, repeated deductions. This points toward an absence of spite-based or revenge-focused motives, and toward a view of punishment as a tool for public-good enforcement rather than interpersonal conflict. Their use of punishments is minimal in frequency and small in magnitude, suggesting that they regard punishment as costly and only occasionally worth using. They appear selective: they intervene when a specific group member deviates downward in contribution in an otherwise cooperative context. The timing implies a deterrence motive (trying to push that individual back up toward the group norm), rather than an attempt to recover personal losses. Overall, they are a low-intensity, norm-oriented punisher: willing to sanction clear free-riders or sudden shirkers, but not inclined to escalate or to run personal punishment campaigns, even under heavy provocation.

They never spend any coins on punishment, despite being repeatedly in a position where they benefit disproportionately from others’ high contributions and are themselves occasionally punished. This persistent abstention suggests that they either (a) find costly punishment not worth the expense, (b) prefer to ignore norm violations and sanctions rather than enforce norms, or (c) are following a strictly payoff-maximizing heuristic that rejects any negative-return actions, even when those might reshape others’ behavior. Because they do not punish even as a reaction to being punished, there is no evidence of retaliatory motives. They also do not use punishment to discipline low contributors among their peers (though in practice the peers generally contribute at high levels). Overall, they act as a non-enforcer and avoid investing in social control mechanisms.

They refrain from using costly punishment despite having clear targets who contribute substantially less than both the group and themselves. This implies an absence of immediate punitive tendencies in the face of under-contribution. They do not engage in norm enforcement even when low contributors and outright near-free-riders are visible, suggesting either indifference to sanctioning, a cost-averse stance toward punishment, or a belief that punishing in this environment is not worthwhile or effective. Their non-use of punishment indicates they do not act as a “moralistic punisher” or strong norm enforcer in this context. They do not appear motivated by retaliation or anger at low contributors, at least not enough to incur a cost to reduce those players’ payoffs. Whether they might ever punish in repeated or more extreme scenarios is unknown. Their selectivity is thus unobserved; all we can infer is that mere observation of markedly lower contributions is not sufficient to trigger punishment for them in a single-round setting. Their orientation toward punishment seems either permissive/forgiving or strategically cost-conscious rather than deterrence-focused.

The player is an active, norm-enforcing punisher, willing to sacrifice their own payoff to sanction behavior they perceive as uncooperative. They do use the punishment option and spend a meaningful, nontrivial amount on it, which indicates that they are not purely payoff-maximizing in the narrow sense. Their punishment is plausibly aimed at enforcing contribution norms rather than retaliation, as there is no evidence they were personally targeted or harmed by specific others. The likely trigger is under-contribution relative to an emerging group standard: they see substantial variation in others’ contributions and appear motivated to deter what they interpret as free-riding or weak cooperation. Their willingness to incur a real cost for punishment suggests they value norm compliance and deterrence, even though they themselves do not fully match the highest cooperative standard. They are selective rather than indiscriminate: the cost is large enough that they are unlikely to be punishing everyone, implying some targeting based on contribution levels. The absence of any reward mechanism means their enforcement style is purely punitive, not reciprocal in the positive direction. Their sensitivity to inequality or perceived unfairness is elevated, but their own mixed contribution and willingness to punish suggest a “tough but not saintly” type: they accept being a partial cooperator yet still expect others—especially clear low contributors—to face consequences. Their long-run adjustment of punishment behavior across rounds is unknown.

The player does not spend any resources on punishment, even when there is clear evidence of under-contribution by some peers and a relatively efficient punishment technology. This suggests either a reluctance to engage in costly norm enforcement or a strategic choice to preserve earnings after already making a fully cooperative contribution. They does, however, become a target of punishment despite their high contribution, which reveals that they experience being punished as a cooperative player; their subsequent emotional or strategic reaction is unknown because only one punishment stage is observed and they have no later opportunity to respond. The absence of punitive action when sanctioning is feasible and would align with efficiency-enhancing norms suggests that the player is not motivated by retribution or by strong norm-enforcement impulses in this context, at least on a first interaction. Their selectivity cannot be assessed: they neither punish high nor low contributors, so it is unclear whether they would ever punish in more extreme cases of free-riding. There is no evidence of retaliatory tendencies, since they do not respond in kind and have no identifiable target. Whether they use punishment instrumentally as a deterrent in repeated settings is unknown; the current behavior is consistent with a “cooperate but do not punish” profile (medium confidence).

The player refrains from using the punishment mechanism, despite being a clear low contributor in a group where others contribute substantially more. This non-use can stem from multiple, not mutually exclusive, tendencies: they may have no interest in norm enforcement, no desire to retaliate for the deduction they receive, or a general aversion to paying costs that do not directly increase their own earnings. There is no evidence of altruistic or norm-based punishment: the player does not spend to sanction any target, even when many peers behave more cooperatively than they do. This strongly suggests that they do not engage in “second-order” cooperation (i.e., punishing to uphold contribution norms). The absence of retaliation, despite experiencing a deduction, indicates either emotional detachment from negative outcomes or a purely payoff-oriented logic that views punishment as a net-loss action with no clear material benefit. Selectivity in punishment targets is unknown, as they never punish at all. Their behavior is consistent with a type who regards punishment purely as a cost, not as a tool for deterrence, reciprocity, or moral expression. They appear unlikely to initiate punitive actions in future settings unless there is a direct, clear expectation of personal gain from doing so (e.g., strategically deterring others in repeated interactions), which is not observable here. Overall, the player behaves as a non-punisher free-rider, focused on maximizing own income and tolerating both others’ high cooperation and incoming sanctions without counteraction.

The player does not use costly punishment even though some others clearly contribute less than them, including very low and zero contributions. This indicates either a reluctance to engage in norm-enforcement through sanctions or a strategic decision that punishing is not worth the personal cost. Given that punishment is anonymous and can be effective in reducing others’ payoffs, the non-use suggests several possible motives, none of which can be confirmed with high confidence. Most consistent with their overall cooperative pattern, they likely prioritize efficiency and personal payoff preservation over retributive justice: they do not appear to act on an impulse to “correct” or retaliate against low contributors. They may see punishment as destructive or socially wasteful, even toward clear free-riders, and therefore avoid it. Alternatively, they might be unsure about the informational value of a single observation and may refrain from sanctioning because they do not want to risk punishing someone who could behave differently in future interactions (whether future play exists is unknown to them). Their stance is non-confrontational and non-retaliatory; they absorb personal disadvantage (including being punished themselves) without responding in kind. They show no evidence of using punishment as a tool for deterrence, coordination, or norm signaling. They also do not appear vengeful or spiteful when personally harmed, as they do not undertake any punitive action despite experiencing a nontrivial deduction. This suggests a personality leaning toward tolerance, forgiveness, or at least passivity in sanctioning contexts. Whether they would ever punish in the face of repeated, blatant free-riding or targeted exploitation over multiple rounds is unknown.

This player shows no use of costly punishment when first given the opportunity, even though several peers contribute substantially less than the maximum. This indicates a clear reluctance to incur personal costs to sanction low contributors in the observed context. They appear to tolerate contribution heterogeneity rather than immediately enforcing a strict contribution norm via punishment. Their default stance toward punishment seems non-interventionist: they do not act to discipline or deter free-riding in this instance, despite having both the means and plausible targets. This could stem from several possible underlying motives, but the data do not uniquely identify them. Potential explanations include: a preference to focus resources on cooperation rather than sanctioning, a belief that early punishment is premature or unjustified without more information, a desire to avoid harming others even when they contribute less, or uncertainty about what contribution levels count as truly “punishable” rather than merely cautious. They do not exhibit retaliatory behavior; there is no evidence they use punishment as a direct response to harm, exploitation, or personal loss targeted at them. They also do not display norm-enforcement behavior against moderate under-contributors; if they endorse any norm, it is not strongly enforced through punitive action at this stage. Their use of punishment as a deterrent tool is therefore unobserved and remains unknown. Selectivity in punishment is impossible to evaluate fully from a single non-use decision, but their inaction suggests a relatively high threshold before they would sanction others, or a general preference not to punish at all. Overall, the player is best characterized as a cooperative non-punisher: they strongly support the public good through their own contributions but refrain, at least initially, from enforcing norms through costly sanctions.

This player abstains from using costly punishment even when some peers contribute substantially less than the cooperative benchmark and at least one peer contributes nothing. They therefore do not act as an active enforcer of contribution norms in this instance. Their non-use of punishment could reflect several, not mutually exclusive, tendencies: a reluctance to harm others even at low personal cost; a prioritization of personal or group payoffs over the negative-sum nature of punishment; uncertainty about what constitutes a justified target; or a strategic belief that punishing in a one-shot or short, opaque setting does little to change behavior. Because punishers and rewarders are anonymous and there is no future feedback, their abstention is consistent with either principled non-punishment or a pragmatic assessment that deterrence would be ineffective. They do not display retaliatory motives; despite being more cooperative than many others, they refrain from sanctioning those who contribute relatively little. This points away from a “moralistic punisher” or “strong reciprocity enforcer” type and toward a “forgiving cooperator” or “non-sanctioning altruist” profile. However, with only one opportunity observed, their general willingness to punish across different contexts, especially in repeated or more transparent settings, remains unknown.

This player shows a strong, proactive willingness to use costly punishment. They invest nontrivially in sanctions despite the personal cost, implying that enforcing norms and addressing perceived unfairness matters to them, even when it reduces their immediate payoff. Given the contribution pattern in the group, it is highly likely they direct punishment primarily or exclusively at the lowest contributors rather than at high contributors or at random. That is, they behave like a norm-enforcing “altruistic punisher” rather than a spiteful or retaliatory one. There is no evidence of them being personally targeted, so retaliation motives are unknown and likely minimal in this instance; deterrence and norm enforcement are more plausible drivers. They appear selective rather than indifferent: the existence of large differences in contribution levels creates clear targets for sanctioning, and their readiness to pay a relatively high total cost suggests they particularly disapprove of very low or zero contributions. This is consistent with a preference for conditional cooperation backed by punishment of norm violators. They seem comfortable using punishment in a relatively anonymous environment where punishers cannot be identified, suggesting that social recognition is not required for them to sanction; internal norms or intrinsic satisfaction from enforcing fairness likely play a role. Their use of punishment indicates a belief that sanctions can and should shape others’ behavior, even in a setting where the number of remaining rounds is unknown. This points to either (a) a general principle of punishing defectors on fairness grounds regardless of future payoff implications, or (b) a belief that future interactions are probable enough that deterrence is worthwhile. The precise balance between these motives is unknown, but their observed behavior aligns with a stable type who readily spends resources to discipline perceived free-riders.

The player does not use the punishment option when it is available, despite clear variation in others’ contributions. This indicates a reluctance to engage in costly sanctioning, even when there are identifiable low contributors. Their behavior suggests that they are not a strong altruistic punisher: they do not spend resources to enforce norms or discipline defectors. They appear cost-conscious, giving priority to preserving their own payoff rather than sacrificing coins to reduce others’ earnings. This hints at a preference for non-intervention over active enforcement of cooperation norms. The absence of punishment may also indicate weak or ambiguous internal norms about sanctioning: while they contribute a nontrivial amount themselves, they do not translate any possible disapproval of low contributors into action. They may rely on personal example rather than coercion, or they may be indifferent to others’ deviations as long as they themselves are not severely exploited. There is no evidence of retaliatory motives, as the player experiences no direct harm beyond the general structure of the game, and they still choose not to punish. Their response is consistent with a low tendency toward aggression or spiteful behavior in this environment. Selectivity and triggers for punishment remain unknown beyond this single choice. It is unclear whether they might punish in more extreme circumstances (e.g., if others’ contributions were overwhelmingly low or if they felt personally targeted). Their longer-term strategy regarding punishment-based deterrence or reputation building cannot be inferred from the available data.

This player is initially non-punitive, even when there is a clear opportunity to sanction others who deviate from the cooperative majority. They only start to use punishment once they have personally borne substantial punishment while being a defector, and their own behavior has temporarily become highly cooperative. When they do engage, they punish heavily and in a targeted way against a consistent low contributor rather than spreading punishment broadly. This pattern is more consistent with norm-enforcement or deterrence than with random spite: they focus on the individual who remains least cooperative and most exploitative at the time when the player has switched to contributing fully. The intensity and timing suggest a retaliatory and corrective motive: they appear to internalize the emerging group norm that defectors should pay, and once temporarily “on the moral high ground” as a full contributor, they invest substantial resources to make the persistent free-rider worse off. However, they do not maintain a long-run punitive strategy; when they later return to defection, they stop punishing, indicating that their willingness to sanction is conditional on themselves feeling aligned with the cooperative norm. They do not use punishment as a fine-tuned marginal adjustment tool but as a blunt, focused response once a clear villain emerges.

They are an active and non-random punisher, willing to spend nontrivial resources to sanction others. They punish in multiple rounds, including when they themselves are already earning relatively high payoffs, so punishment is not limited to compensating poor material outcomes. Their targeting is not simply “low contributors”: they also sanction players who contribute fully. This suggests that punishment is not driven by a straightforward fairness norm like “punish defectors and spare cooperators.” Given that they never contribute, their punishment of contributors is inconsistent with standard altruistic punishment and more consistent with competitive or retaliatory motives. They appear willing to escalate or sustain punishment even after being punished themselves, and they later punish a player who has recently been punished by others and ends with a very low payoff, indicating low concern for not “over-punishing” already disadvantaged group members. Overall, they seem willing to inflict significant net losses on others at modest cost to themselves, indicating a tolerance for payoff destruction that goes beyond simple payoff maximization. The pattern is consistent with spiteful or vendetta-like tendencies, or with opaque personal criteria for “deserved” punishment that are not clearly tied to contribution levels.

The player is a frequent and relatively heavy user of punishment. They are willing to incur nontrivial personal costs to reduce others’ payoffs, and they do so in multiple rounds. Their targeting pattern is primarily consistent with norm enforcement against low contributors: they impose costs on players who contribute little or nothing when others are cooperating. They also sometimes target players who are themselves contributors or are not the clear worst free‑riders, which suggests either imperfect information about others’ punishment behavior, attempts to influence multiple group members simultaneously, or a broader standard of what counts as norm violation (e.g., persistent low contribution history). Their punishment is not purely retaliatory: there is no clear evidence they themselves are singled out by others, yet they still punish. The intensity of use appears aimed more at expressing disapproval and potentially deterring free‑riding than at optimizing net payoffs. They accept substantial efficiency losses to enforce their view of appropriate behavior, implying a strong enforcement motive and willingness to sacrifice personal earnings for sanctioning.

The player is selectively willing to use punishment, but only sparingly and in situations that plausibly reflect norm enforcement rather than blind retaliation or spite. They generally refrain from punishing when doing so would be clearly costly relative to the perceived violation, suggesting they attend to cost–benefit trade-offs. When they do punish, the apparent target is a low contributor in an otherwise more cooperative environment, which is consistent with conditional cooperators punishing defectors to enforce norms. There is no strong evidence that they retaliate directly for being punished themselves, since punishment is anonymous and they do not escalate their punishment intensity after being targeted. They also do not attempt to punish high contributors or those who appear supportive of cooperation, which indicates some selectivity and a focus on contributing norms rather than payoff equality per se. Overall, they seem willing to use targeted, low-intensity punishment as a tool of norm enforcement, but they are not a heavy or systematic punisher and do not appear motivated by pure vengeance or by a desire to equalize payoffs at all costs. Confidence about their exact motives is moderate rather than high due to anonymity of punishers.

They never use punishment at any point, despite repeated opportunities and despite clear low contributors being present. This holds even when other players use punishment heavily. The absence of any punishing action suggests either a strong aversion to harming others, skepticism about the value or fairness of punishment, or a deliberate focus on positive rather than negative incentives. They do not appear to retaliate when they themselves are punished; there is no detectable shift toward punitive behavior following deductions from their own payoff. Their type is thus “non-punitive,” with no observable threshold at which they are willing to impose costs on others, even obvious under-contributors.

They never use punishment even once, despite being repeatedly punished themselves. This persistent abstention suggests a strong reluctance to engage in costly sanctions, regardless of perceived unfairness or of others’ low contributions. Their behavior is inconsistent with an altruistic punisher or a retaliator; they appear unwilling to spend coins to harm others, even when they could plausibly “justify” it as norm enforcement or revenge. This might stem from a preference for avoiding conflict, a view that punishment is inefficient or morally questionable, or a belief that anonymous punishment is too noisy to be useful. Because they remain cooperative for much of the game without punishing defectors, they resemble a “non‑sanctioning cooperator” type: they tolerate being targeted and rely on their own contribution adjustments rather than active deterrence.

They never use punishment at any point, even when they are personally targeted by punishment and even when some players free‑ride strongly or behave erratically. This is consistent across all observed situations. They absorb anti‑social punishment and do not retaliate. They also do not employ punishment as a deterrent against low contributors. The complete absence of punishment—despite the presence of cheap and powerful punishment technology and multiple candidates who could be seen as unfair or uncooperative—suggests either a principled aversion to harming others’ payoffs or a belief that punishment is inefficient, normatively undesirable, or ineffective in this environment. Since they change their own contributions and do use rewards, the lack of punishment is unlikely to be simple inattention to the mechanism. Instead, it is best interpreted as a stable preference against negative sanctions, even when they are on the receiving end of them.

They never punish anyone, even when confronted with repeated low or zero contributions and when they themselves receive deductions. This indicates a strong reluctance to use costly negative sanctions. There is no sign of retaliatory behavior: being punished does not trigger any change in their sanctioning pattern. Nor do they appear to use punishment instrumentally to deter free-riding or enforce fairness. The absence of punishment, despite clear opportunities and strong antisocial or targeted sanctioning by others, suggests one of two broad motives: a principled opposition to harming others’ payoffs (even defectors) or a purely self-protective avoidance of spending on sanctions. Because they are willing to sacrifice profit by fully contributing, yet still avoid spending on punishment, the pattern aligns more with “non-punitive altruist” than with purely payoff-maximizing egoist. They accept free-riding and even personal losses without resorting to negative enforcement.

They never use the punishment mechanism, even when it is widely used by others against a low‑contributing and later antisocial player, and even though their contributions and payoffs give them ample capacity to do so. They refrain both when under‑contribution is obvious and norm‑violating and when antisocial punishment by that same player appears. This consistent non‑use suggests a strong reluctance to incur personal costs to punish, even in clear cases of norm violation. Their motive appears to be “cooperate without sanctioning” rather than “enforce cooperation through costly punishment.” Whether this stems from principled opposition to punishment, indifference, or strategic avoidance of potential retaliation is unknown. Their selectivity is extreme: threshold for punishing seems so high that it is never crossed.
