They are clearly willing to punish, and specifically engage in costly punishment of a conspicuously low-contributing player when group members call for sanctions. Their punishment is targeted at that known free rider, not spread broadly, indicating selectivity toward clear norm violators rather than aggressive or spiteful tendencies. They follow group signals (“we will punish the low contributor”) rather than initiating punishment agendas, suggesting a conformity-based or reinforcement-oriented motive more than personal vengeance. Their punishment intensity is moderate relative to others: they contribute nontrivially to sanctions but do not escalate to extreme levels. Later, when that same target continues problematic behavior and others pour very heavy punishment into them, this player reduces or ceases their own punitive spending, which is consistent with a pragmatic deterrence logic—once the norm-breaking is already heavily sanctioned, further personal spending seems unnecessary. They do not engage in antisocial punishment against cooperators, nor do they retaliate against anyone, even after general turbulence and arbitrary deductions appear in the group.

They are willing to punish, but in a targeted and norm-enforcing way rather than vindictively. They support the idea of punishing under-contributors from the outset and follow through in a measured way against a clear low contributor. They coordinate with others to sanction that player, and they explicitly label the punishment magnitude they intend, which communicates both deterrence and fairness. They later again announce an intention to deduct from a chronic problem player for a prior round’s misbehavior, then apologize to that player, framing the punishment as deserved but not personal. They do not punish anyone who consistently cooperates and do not engage in spiteful or random deductions, even when others start to show noise or mistakes. They also refrain from piling on during extreme group punishments initiated by others, indicating some restraint and cost–benefit sensitivity. Their punishment style is best described as “prosocial, norm-based targeting of clear defectors, with low inclination toward aggression beyond that.”

They mostly refrain from punishing throughout the game, even when others under‑contribute or when the group briefly breaks down. They encourage others to enforce norms but personally do not pay to punish during those earlier deviations. Only after they themselves receive an unjust deduction do they punish, targeting the specific individual responsible. The timing and content of their message make this a clear act of retaliation rather than forward‑looking deterrence. They also align with other group members who see reciprocating against norm violators as “only fair,” suggesting they accept reciprocal punishment as legitimate in principle. Overall, they are selective and patient: tolerant of efficiency‑damaging behavior in earlier stages, but willing to spend significantly on punishment when fairness to themselves is violated and the horizon is short.

They are willing to incur nontrivial costs to punish clear free riding and do so early and again later when a participant repeatedly contributes nothing. Their punishment is selective and targeted at the most obvious norm violator, consistent with a conditional cooperator who supports cooperation through deterrence. They openly announce their intention to punish in chat, indicating they see punishment partly as a public signal to enforce norms, not just a private act. They do not punish moderate deviations or ambiguous behavior, nor do they engage in antisocial punishment against cooperators. Once the worst offender appears to improve contributions (at least temporarily), they refrain from further sanctions, then reinstate punishment when the same player again contributes nothing. This pattern suggests their motive is primarily norm enforcement and deterrence rather than spite: they react to clear defection rather than to interpersonal slights. They never use punishment to respond to antisocial punishment by others, and they do not join retaliation chains, which reinforces the view that their punitive behavior is rule-based and cooperation-focused.

This player uses punishment rarely and selectively. They almost always refrain from punishing when others behave cooperatively, even when they themselves have been punished in prior periods. This suggests they do not engage in spiteful or retaliatory punishment against cooperators. They do punish clear free-riders and appear to target the lowest contributor when a deviation from full cooperation occurs. This is consistent with norm-enforcing punishment aimed at non-contributors rather than generalized aggression. When they themselves are fully contributing and another player contributes nothing, they are willing to incur a personal cost to sanction that deviation. This places them in the category of “mild altruistic punisher”: they will pay to uphold the cooperation norm, but only in obvious, one-sided violation cases. The intensity of punishment is modest rather than maximal. They do not escalate into repeated or broad punishment campaigns; instead, they intervene occasionally when a single player breaks the norm, then return to non-punishing behavior once contributions realign. This restraint indicates a focus on signaling and correction rather than maximizing the other’s loss. There is no clear evidence that they use punishment as personal revenge against those who have previously punished them, nor that they punish high contributors. When others cooperate, they withhold punishment entirely. Overall, their punishment pattern aligns more with deterrence and norm enforcement than with retaliation.

This player is generally reluctant to use costly punishment and only deploys it sparingly and in a targeted way. They abstain from punishing when all others contribute fully and do not engage in antisocial punishment against cooperators. When they do punish, it is only in response to clear free-riding by a specific partner, and the punishment intensity is low relative to what is technically possible, suggesting a minimal, possibly norm-signaling intervention rather than maximum deterrence. They do not systematically punish every free-ride they see; instead, their use of punishment is intermittent and selective, consistent with a player who dislikes spending on punishment but is willing to incur a small cost to express disapproval or to try to correct behavior when defection seems salient or repeated. There is no evidence of vindictive punishment directed at individuals who merely differ slightly in contribution, nor do they punish in situations of mutual full cooperation, implying that their punishment motive is closer to norm enforcement than retaliation for small payoff inequalities. However, because punishment is infrequent and low magnitude, there is limited evidence that they aim to strongly deter future free-riding; the behavior is more consistent with a “reluctant, light-touch enforcer” than a hard-line punisher.

They are a frequent and heavy punisher, regularly spending nontrivial amounts of their own payoff to impose deductions on others. Their sanctioning is most plausibly aimed at low contributors: they spend a lot on punishment in periods where there are clear and repeated free riders, and they spend nothing only in the one instance where they themselves fully match the cooperative norm and many others also contribute highly. They do not appear to target high contributors or random players; their pattern aligns with a norm-enforcing role directed at under-contributors. However, they do not follow group calls to punish medium-level partial contributors in a coordinated, all-out way; instead, they focus their own punishment decisions in a consistent, but somewhat individualized, pattern. The sanctions are often large relative to their own payoffs, indicating that they are willing to incur substantial personal cost to punish what they perceive as unfair behavior. This willingness persists even at the end of the game, suggesting that deterrence of future rounds is not their sole or primary motive. Retaliation motives are unclear: they are occasionally punished by others, but there is no direct evidence that they change targets to hit those who punished them. Overall, they fit a “strong but self-interest-hedging enforcer”: they punish defectors aggressively while not fully living up to the cooperative standard themselves.

This player rarely uses the punishment mechanism and does so at a very low level compared with others. When they do punish, it appears to be directed at prominent low contributors (those who give nothing) during a phase when the group is collectively sanctioning such behavior. Their punishment is not extreme and does not look like blind retaliation for being punished themselves; it is more plausibly aligned with group norm enforcement, but at a minimal intensity. They do not appear to lead punishment campaigns or invest heavily in deterrence. Their low, selective use suggests they understand the purpose of punishment and are willing to participate symbolically, but they prioritize conserving their own payoff over bearing significant costs to discipline others. There is no clear evidence of spiteful or revenge‑driven punishment; if anything, they behave as a reluctant or “cheap” norm enforcer.

They never spend on punishment in any round. This remains true even when others clearly free‑ride or when the group discusses coordinated punishment of low contributors. They do not participate in the sanctioning system at all. There is also no retaliatory punishment from them despite receiving substantial deductions at times, which indicates either low desire for revenge or a preference to conserve coins rather than spend on social control. Their earlier chat agreement with a “punish low contributors” plan is not backed by behavior, pointing to either strategic cheap talk, misunderstanding of the mechanism, or a lack of motivation to incur personal cost for enforcing norms. Their stance is effectively: benefit from others’ punishment efforts but never invest in punishment themselves.

They are willing to incur nontrivial personal costs to punish, but not immediately and not maximally; their style is targeted enforcement, not indiscriminate aggression. Early on, they refrain from punishment even when clear low contributors exist, possibly relying on others to enforce or hoping social norms and talk will suffice. As low contributions repeat and seem intentional, they start to punish, spending enough to be meaningful but staying far below the most extreme punishers in the group. Their targets are consistently those who contribute little or nothing relative to the emerging group norm, especially repeatedly low contributors; there is no evidence they punish high contributors or apparent cooperators, which makes their punishment pattern look norm-enforcing rather than spiteful. They also join in verbal support of coordinated group punishment of persistent low contributors, indicating they see punishment mainly as a deterrent and a tool to secure cooperation, not as a vehicle for personal revenge. At the same time, their own moderate contribution reductions in the mid-game, combined with their willingness to punish others, suggests a mild asymmetry: they accept punishing defectors while themselves occasionally under-contributing relative to the explicit “everyone max” norm. This is consistent with a “moralistic but somewhat self-serving enforcer” type rather than a perfectly strict altruistic punisher.

They are clearly willing to punish and spend multiple units on punishment in several rounds. Their punishment appears norm-enforcing rather than retaliatory: there is no evidence they are themselves targeted and then respond; instead, they punish others who contribute little or nothing relative to the agreed-upon maximum. They explicitly link punishment to contribution behavior, agreeing that people who give less than the maximum should face deductions and affirming their own participation in that scheme. Their punishment is selective—aimed at clear under-contributors, particularly those who persist in contributing nothing or well below the group’s target after repeated discussion. They also support the idea of collective, coordinated punishment (“everyone deduct from them”), indicating a deterrence motive: they want punishment to signal that deviation from the norm will reliably be met with losses. There is no evidence they use punishment out of spite unrelated to contribution behavior; all observed comments about sanctioning connect it to cooperation levels.

They demonstrate a willingness to punish, including an episode of extremely heavy, costly punishment directed at low contributors. For multiple earlier rounds they refrain from punishing at all, even when free-riding is present and others are already using punishment. This indicates that punishment is not their default: they initially tolerate some deviation or rely on others’ sanctions. Later, they engage in moderate-cost punishment and then in one round impose very large total punishment at high personal cost, targeting clear under-contributors who repeatedly give nothing or very little. This heavy punishment strongly harms their own payoff, suggesting a willingness to sacrifice substantial income to sanction perceived unfairness and repeated free-riding, more consistent with norm-enforcement motives than with pure payoff maximization. They never appear to punish high contributors or engage in “antisocial” punishment; their targets are consistently those contributing much less than the cooperative norm. After this intense sanctioning episode, they return to using no punishment even when non-contributors persist, which may indicate that they see their earlier punishment as a one-time corrective attempt, or that they learn the marginal effectiveness is low and choose to conserve payoff. Overall, they are a conditional, fairness-driven punisher who escalates sharply when patience runs out, focuses punishment on chronic low contributors, and does not use punishment for retaliation against those who punish them (they themselves are never deducted) or for opportunistic reasons.

This player is a frequent and relatively heavy user of punishment. They are clearly willing to spend nontrivial amounts of their own payoff to impose costs on others. Their punishment is not random; it is plausibly targeted toward perceived low contributors or norm violators, as indicated by the coexistence of low contributions by some peers and the player’s repeated choice to punish even when not themselves the lowest contributor. Because we cannot see the exact targets, inference about specific targeting is low confidence, but the repeated deployment of punishment in rounds with visible low contributions by others makes a norm-enforcement motive plausible. They often punish even when they are themselves contributing very little, which suggests that their punishment is not purely altruistic or tied to their own personal cooperative sacrifice. This raises two compatible interpretations: (1) they may hold a view that certain minimal levels of cooperation are required from everyone, regardless of their own exact contribution in that round (a “you must at least try” standard), or (2) they may use punishment in a more strategic or emotionally driven way, enforcing norms selectively while continuing to free-ride themselves. Their punishment is sometimes heavy relative to their round payoff, indicating they are willing to accept sizable immediate personal costs in order to deter or sanction others. This behavior is consistent with a player who values shaping the group’s behavior or expressing disapproval more than maximizing immediate earnings. The pattern of repeated punishment toward the end of the game, even when they themselves are also being punished, suggests that once conflicts are established they tend to escalate rather than de-escalate. They do not always punish when low contributions by others are present, indicating some selectivity. This could reflect budget management, shifting focus to those seen as persistent offenders, or simple experimentation with when punishment is most effective. Because reward is unavailable, their only direct instrument for influencing others is negative; they appear willing to use it regularly and sometimes aggressively. In summary, their punishment profile fits a “selectively harsh enforcer with self-interest” type: they enforce contribution norms, likely focusing on low contributors, but do so even when their own cooperative stance is ambiguous, and they are prepared to bear significant costs to punish.

This player is willing to spend heavily on punishment and is not generally restrained about the cost, indicating a strong norm-enforcement or retaliation orientation. They go through stretches with no punishment, then suddenly invest a substantial portion of their available payoff in sanctions, including multiple episodes of very costly punishment that greatly reduce their own round payoff. That pattern suggests they use punishment selectively rather than reflexively: they do not punish every deviation, but when they decide someone deserves it, they are prepared to escalate. The targets of their punishment cannot be seen from the transcript, but the timing suggests two likely triggers: persistently low contributors and players they perceive as unfair or hostile (especially those who may have punished them previously). Since punishment is identifiable to the punished and they themselves are punished in multiple cooperative periods, it is plausible that some of their heavy punishment bursts are retaliatory rather than purely prosocial. Their willingness to incur net losses from punishing even when group contributions are reasonably high points to a strong emotional or principled response to perceived injustice rather than a purely instrumental deterrence calculus. They do not systematically punish in every round of low cooperation, which implies some threshold or discretion: they may punish when low contributions or perceived slights accumulate beyond a tolerance level. There is no evidence they use graded, finely tuned punishment; the bursts of high expenditure are consistent with “strong signals” rather than marginal corrections. Overall, they behave as an intense, sometimes vengeful norm enforcer who is comfortable sacrificing efficiency in the short run to send a clear message.

This player uses punishment sparingly and selectively. Across the game, they almost always decline to spend on punishment, even when faced with clear and repeated low contributions by some peers. This indicates that they do not have a strong intrinsic taste for sanctioning, and they are clearly sensitive to the direct cost of punishing. When they do punish, it coincides with situations where they are themselves contributing at the very high end while some identifiable others are giving very little. Their limited use of sanctions appears targeted toward low contributors rather than toward high contributors, suggesting they are not engaging in antisocial or competitive punishment. The pattern is consistent with norm‑enforcing or deterrence‑oriented punishment: they are willing to incur a cost to signal that under‑contribution is unacceptable, but they require a clear discrepancy between their own high provision and others’ low provision to act. They do not escalate punishment across rounds. Even after being punished by others, they mostly refrain from punishing in response. This lack of systematic retaliation suggests that their rare punishment is not driven by revenge but by perceived fairness violations in the contribution stage. The absence of a broad punitive campaign against chronic low contributors shows a preference for maintaining earnings and avoiding costly conflict rather than repeatedly “teaching a lesson.” The player’s willingness to incur punishment costs at all, given a strong personal contribution profile, points to a moderate but not extreme commitment to enforcing cooperative norms. They are the type who can and will punish if pushed far enough by others’ low contributions, but they do not see punishment as a default tool and avoid using it unless the deviation from their fairness standard is salient. Overall, they resemble a “reluctant norm enforcer”: mostly non‑punitive, but capable of targeted, cost‑aware sanctioning of clear free‑riding.

This player is willing to use punishment, but not indiscriminately. They do not punish in every round, and there are long periods where they refrain from punishing despite the presence of clear low contributors. When they do punish, the total cost they incur is sometimes substantial, indicating that, in those moments, they accept personal losses to shape others’ behavior. The pattern suggests that punishment is primarily norm-enforcing and secondarily retaliatory. They are most willing to punish when there is a salient mismatch between their own moderate contribution and another player’s much lower contribution, particularly when the group as a whole is contributing relatively well. In such contexts, the focal player appears to target clear free riders or chronic under-contributors. The size of their punishment expenditures in those phases suggests a deterrence motive: they are prepared to pay nontrivial costs to discourage low contribution, not just to “get even.” However, they do not pursue a rigid or universal enforcement strategy. They tolerate some deviation by others without consistent punishment and sometimes withhold punishment even when they would have clear justification on a fairness basis. This indicates selectivity and a willingness to conserve resources, possibly weighting the expected impact of punishment (e.g., whether the target seems likely to respond) over simple retribution. There are also moments where punishment expenditure coincides with the player themselves contributing very little. In those cases, it is unclear whether they are targeting specific past punishers (retaliation) or low contributors (second-order norm enforcement despite their own low contribution). Given the data, both motives are plausible; confidence is low in distinguishing them. Overall, they are a conditional punisher: they engage when they feel norms are clearly violated or when they are frustrated, but they are not a consistent “moralist” who always punishes all defectors.

This player never spends on punishment across the entire observed game. This holds even when others contribute much less than they do, even when there are clear and persistent low contributors, and even when they themselves are punished despite contributing more than many peers. The absence of punishment in all these situations implies a consistently non‑punitive strategy rather than just a lack of opportunity. They do not engage in altruistic punishment to enforce contribution norms, nor do they use antisocial punishment to retaliate against those who punished them. They effectively ignore the punishment mechanism, even though they know it is available and see that others are using it on them. Motivationally, this looks less like indifference to norms (they do care about cooperation) and more like a preference to avoid costly conflict. They appear unwilling to sacrifice their own coins to reduce others’ payoffs, even for potential long‑term deterrence. This is consistent with either a principled aversion to harming others or a pragmatic focus on maximizing their own earnings without engaging in side conflicts. From behavior alone, which of these motives dominates is unknown. They also show no sign of strategic signaling through punishment (for example, never “teaching a lesson” to clear free‑riders). This indicates low willingness to take on a “norm enforcer” or leadership role; they prefer to lead by example in contributions, not by imposing sanctions.

They never use the punishment mechanism, despite clear opportunities to target repeated non-contributors and despite punishment being relatively effective in reducing others’ payoffs. This indicates a strong reluctance to impose costs on others, even on clear free riders and even when they themselves are bearing the cost of others’ low contributions. There is no evidence of norm-enforcing punishment, retaliatory punishment, or strategic deterrence. Their nonuse of punishment persists after experiencing a deduction themselves, suggesting they do not respond to being punished by “punishing back” or by trying to discipline others. Overall, they behave as a non-punisher with either moral objections to harming others, a strong focus on prosocial cooperation rather than enforcement, or indifference to the strategic benefits of sanctioning. The exact underlying motive is unknown, but their consistent inaction is clear.

They never use the punishment option. This includes situations where they are punished heavily and could plausibly retaliate, as well as situations where they could try to discipline defectors or non-helpers. The absence of any punitive action suggests either a strict payoff-maximizing stance that avoids spending on sanctions, or a deliberate non-engagement with norm enforcement. There is no evidence of targeted or selective punishment and no sign of using punishment as a deterrent, a means of retaliation, or as a tool to shape others’ future behavior. Their type does not invest in second-order enforcement; they free-ride not only on the public good but also on others’ willingness to bear the cost of punishment.

The player is willing to use costly punishment, but selectively and in a targeted, low-intensity manner. They punish a clear low contributor in a context where most others contribute fully, indicating sensitivity to norm violations and a willingness to incur personal cost to sanction free-riding. The punishment is minimal rather than maximal, suggesting a calibrated response aimed at signaling disapproval rather than inflicting severe harm. They do not escalate punishment in later opportunities, even though clear low contributors persist. This restraint implies they are not driven by spite, anger, or a desire for repeated retaliation. Their punishment use looks more like one-off norm enforcement than an ongoing deterrence strategy: they do not systematically punish all low contributors, nor do they continue punishing after being punished themselves. Overall, they resemble a mild, principled enforcer rather than a vengeful or highly strategic punisher. Their underlying motive appears to be expressing and upholding a cooperation norm, not optimizing payoff via deterrence.

This player is a pure non-punisher in this setting, even when they themselves deviate or are sanctioned. They never use costly punishment against either low or high contributors, despite clear opportunities and an efficient punishment technology. They do not retaliate when heavily punished for free-riding, indicating either a strong aversion to negative sanctions, a desire to avoid escalating conflict, or a belief that the punishment is at least partly legitimate given their own defection. This non-retaliation suggests low propensity for spiteful behavior and a relatively high tolerance for being sanctioned without responding in kind. They also do not engage in norm-enforcement punishment against clear low contributors in later periods, even after they themselves have become a full contributor. This points to a type that separates their own cooperative behavior from active policing of others. They may rely on others to enforce norms (“second-order free-rider” in punishment) or simply dislike inflicting losses on others, even when doing so is efficient and potentially group-beneficial. Motivationally, deterrence and norm enforcement via punishment do not seem to guide their behavior. Their shift to cooperation happens without them adopting punishing behavior themselves. Overall, they are punishment-averse and non-confrontational.

This player is an active, costly punisher with a clear focus on non-contributors. They repeatedly target those who give nothing, and they do so in a selective and limited way rather than broadly sanctioning everyone who contributes less than them. They never punish partial cooperators; their threshold appears to be complete free-riding. Their willingness to bear a relatively high personal cost to inflict large deductions suggests they value norm enforcement and deterrence more than short-term profit. They do not escalate into punishing cooperators, even when one of their targets is themselves punished later, which suggests their motive is predominantly norm-based rather than retaliatory. Their pattern indicates a “strong reciprocator” or “altruistic punisher” type: they contribute fully and sanction free-riders to sustain a cooperation norm, not to settle scores.

This player never uses costly punishment, even in the presence of clear free-riders and even after being punished themselves. This suggests a strong reluctance to engage in negative sanctioning and possibly an aversion to harming others, including those who behave selfishly. They do not display retaliatory behavior: when they become a target of punishment, they refrain from punishing the likely punisher or any group members. This is consistent with a non-retaliatory, non-aggressive style. Because they do not punish low contributors, they also do not appear focused on using punishment as a cooperative enforcement tool. Overall, they behave as a non-punisher type: they absorb costs from others’ behavior (including punishment directed at them) without responding in kind, and they appear unwilling to incur costs solely to deter or discipline others.

They are highly willing to use costly punishment and are willing to spend a substantial portion of their payoff to sanction others. The punishment pattern targets clear free-riders and near free-riders, not partial cooperators, suggesting a threshold-based norm: contributing nothing appears to violate their standard enough to justify sanctions. They distinguish between occasional and repeat low contributors: a player who consistently contributes nothing is punished more heavily than those who free-ride once or less frequently. They also later re-punish a persistent non-contributor, indicating that repeated norm violation is especially salient. Their punishment seems primarily norm-enforcing and deterrence-oriented rather than retaliatory. They do not punish cooperators or middling contributors, and there is no evidence that they punish in response to being punished themselves; when they are personally sanctioned, they do not lash out at likely culprits. They do not appear spiteful or broadly aggressive; punishment is selective and aligned with contribution levels. At the same time, they are willing to bear sizable personal cost for these sanctions, suggesting a strong internal enforcement motive and relatively low concern for short-term earnings when norms are breached.

This player is clearly willing to use costly punishment and targets it selectively at clear free riders. They direct sanctions only at those who systematically contribute nothing, and they never punish cooperators. Their pattern shows attention to contribution histories and discrimination between persistent and occasional non-cooperation. They are willing to spend substantial resources to impose strong costs on the most extreme defector and smaller, calibrated penalties on others who do not contribute, indicating an apparent concern with proportionality and deterrence rather than blind aggression. There is no evidence of retaliatory punishment, as they are never observed punishing someone who harmed them personally; all sanctions are oriented toward norm enforcement. The player does not appear to adjust punishment volume in response to changes in overall group contribution, suggesting a stable enforcement disposition rather than highly reactive emotional swings. Overall, they fit a “principled enforcer” type: cooperative, norm-focused, prepared to bear personal costs to discipline defectors.

The player is a very willing and extremely severe punisher, with a consistent pattern of targeting low or zero contributors. They do not use punishment sparingly; they invest substantial resources to impose large deductions on specific defectors. Their targeting is selective: they do not punish cooperators, only those who contribute nothing or markedly less than the majority. This indicates a clear contribution-based norm as the trigger. The scale of punishment escalates, especially against a repeat low contributor, suggesting an attempt at strong deterrence and norm enforcement rather than mere mild signaling. The magnitude of sanctions far exceeds any plausible material benefit to the punisher, given the high cost of harming others relative to their own payoff, implying a likely non-instrumental or morally driven motive (“punish defectors because it is right”) rather than purely strategic profit-seeking. When a previous defector later cooperates, the player still continues to punish them, which indicates low forgiveness and a tendency to treat past defection as deserving ongoing sanction. Their behavior fits a “harsh moralizer” style: they defend full-cooperation norms aggressively, accept large personal costs to discipline defectors, and are willing to escalate against repeat or especially salient free-riders.

Punishment is central to this player’s type. They punish frequently, spend large amounts on it, and repeatedly target the same chronic under-contributors. They are highly selective: sanctions focus overwhelmingly on the two players who contribute far below the rest across many opportunities, with occasional extension to a moderately low contributor once. High contributors are never punished by them, indicating the sanctions are norm-driven rather than competitive or spiteful toward strong cooperators. They are willing to incur substantial personal cost to reduce free-riders’ payoffs, well beyond any direct monetary return, indicating a strong norm-enforcement or inequality-aversion motive. The intensity escalates over time against persistent low contributors, suggesting frustration or a desire to make the free-riding strategy clearly unprofitable. They rarely use punishment in a fine-tuned, proportional manner; instead they often impose large, discrete sanction levels that push targets’ payoffs very low. This indicates a taste for “tough justice” rather than minimal deterrence. There is no evidence of retaliatory punishment toward those who punish them (because they are not punished by others), and no evidence of competitive punishment against cooperators. Their pattern aligns with a “moralistic enforcer” or “strong reciprocator” type: they value group cooperation enough to pay heavily to discipline defectors, even when others are already punishing the same targets.

They never use punishment at any point, despite persistent and obvious free‑riding and despite punishment being highly cost‑effective in this environment. They refrain from punishing both chronic low contributors and extreme deviants who are heavily punished by others. This indicates a strong reluctance (or principled refusal) to incur costs in order to reduce others’ payoffs. The absence of any “test” punishment suggests that they either reject punitive enforcement as a tool, consider it not worth the cost, or are indifferent to sanctioning as a means of shaping behavior. They also do not engage in antisocial punishment: they do not target high contributors or apparent norm enforcers. The motive that best fits the data is a non‑punitive, live-and-let-live stance: they maintain high cooperation personally but do not attempt to discipline non‑cooperators.

This player never uses the punishment mechanism, even though they are repeatedly punished themselves and can see that others employ punishment extensively. Their restraint persists under conditions where many group members punish defectors, plus some apparent “moralistic” or escalated punishment against certain others. The refusal to punish holds both when the player cooperates and when they defect. This suggests a strong reluctance to spend resources on sanctioning, regardless of potential deterrence value. Their non-use of punishment makes their motives unclear: they might judge punishment as too costly, as ineffective, or as something they do not want to participate in normatively. There is no evidence of retaliatory motives, since they never punish those who have punished them, despite punishers being identifiable. Nor do they use targeted punishment to steer others’ contributions or to reward cooperators by punishing defectors. This stable non-punishing stance points to a personality or strategy that is sanction-averse and unwilling to invest in enforcing norms, even when those norms work against them.

The player is a frequent and heavy user of punishment, especially toward persistent low contributors. Early on, they refrain entirely, even when others under-contribute, suggesting they first observe and confirm patterns before intervening. Once they start, they repeatedly target the same low-contributing players, focusing on chronic free-riders rather than one-off deviations, which indicates selective norm enforcement rather than indiscriminate aggression. They are willing to invest large amounts of their own payoff into sanctions, at times spending a substantial share of what they could have earned. The intensity and repetition of sanctions toward the same offenders point to a deterrence or moral retribution motive: they appear intent on making norm violators pay, not just nudging them. They do occasionally punish moderate contributors as well, likely because those players lag behind the cooperative core and are perceived as insufficiently committed to the group. There is no evidence that they use punishment strategically to build alliances or curry favor; instead, punishment is aligned with a high-cooperation norm and is applied even when it does not obviously improve the player’s own earnings. When they themselves are punished, they do not redirect their sanctions toward their own punishers, indicating their punishment decisions are driven more by contribution behavior than by personal vendettas.

They are an active, norm-focused punisher with a strong willingness to incur costs to sanction perceived free riders, even when punishment is already heavy. Their primary trigger is very low or zero contribution, especially when others are contributing high amounts. Two players who repeatedly contribute almost nothing become consistent targets; the player often adds to collective punishment against those individuals rather than abstaining because “enough” punishment is already applied. This indicates a strong deterrence/retribution motive and a preference for reinforcing a group norm of high contribution, not just personal retaliation. They also punish moderate under-contributors on occasion, including someone who earlier received their punishment and later reduces contributions again, suggesting they track contribution patterns over time. Their selectivity is contribution-based, not payoff-based: they do not punish simply because someone is earning more or less, but because they contribute substantially below the apparent informal standard. There is at least one instance of punishing a full or near-full contributor while others’ contributions are comparatively low; the reason for this is unclear. It could reflect a misclick, misperception of norms, or an attempt to discipline someone for their own punishing behavior, but this is speculative; motive here is unknown. Overall, they display high willingness to spend on punishment, especially in later rounds, and they do not appear deterred by the personal cost or by the substantial punishment others are already inflicting. Their pattern is consistent with a “strong reciprocator” or “moralist enforcer” type, albeit with some noisy or opaque decisions.

For most of the game, this player refrains completely from punishment, despite clear, persistent free-riding by specific others. Their threshold for punishing is very high: they tolerate many rounds of extremely low contributions from certain players without acting. When they finally do punish, they target only the worst, consistently low contributors, and they do so in a coordinated environment where those same targets are already being heavily punished by others. Their own punishment is modest in size compared to the extreme sanctions others impose, implying a measured rather than vengeful style. The timing and targets suggest norm-enforcement or moral expression, not retaliation (they themselves are never punished) and not forward-looking deterrence (it occurs at the last round). They are willing to pay a meaningful cost to punish, despite no future benefit, indicating that upholding or signaling fairness norms can, in itself, justify costly punishment for them. They are selective and do not use punishment in ambiguous or moderate deviation cases.

For most of the game this player abstains entirely from using punishment, even while some others contribute little and others spend heavily to punish defectors. This restraint suggests a preference for tolerance or a reluctance to accept efficiency losses from costly sanctions, at least initially. However, they track behavior: one group member repeatedly contributes almost nothing across many rounds. At the very end, the player delivers a very large, targeted punishment to that specific chronic low contributor. This punishment is severe relative to what they have ever done before (they have not punished anyone else), and it strongly reduces that target’s payoff. This pattern fits a “high threshold” altruistic punisher: they generally avoid punishing but are willing to impose heavy sanctions when (a) non-cooperation is consistent and extreme, and (b) there is no future to preserve cooperation, so the sanction likely serves a backward-looking norm-enforcement or moralistic motive rather than forward-looking deterrence. They do not punish moderate contributors or conditional cooperators, and they ignore other strategic targets even when those players also contribute zero in several rounds; their focus is uniquely on the most extreme, persistent under-contributor. This shows selectivity and a clear qualitative trigger: long-term, near-total freeriding.

They never punish anyone, across all observed rounds, despite: - Clear and repeated opportunities to punish obvious free-riders. - Strong social pressure and explicit calls from others to punish specific targets. - Very high punishment intensity used by others. This strongly suggests a “non-punisher” type. Several features stand out: - **High threshold / strong restraint**: Even when a single player persistently contributes far less than the group, and the group frames this as a “justice” issue, they abstain. They do not even purchase minimal punishment units. - **No evidence of conditional or retaliatory punishment**: They are never personally deducted, but they also do not punish to restore equality or to deter future free-riding on behalf of the group. - **Cost–benefit awareness is unclear**: They witness many others willingly sacrifice substantial payoffs to reduce the free-rider’s gains, and they see those punishers often end up with lower payoffs. They may infer that punitive spending is inefficient, but they never articulate this explicitly in chat. Their behavior is consistent with either an efficiency concern (“wasteful to burn coins”) or a moral reluctance to harm, even norm-violators. Given their unwavering maximum contributions, their refusal to punish is not due to selfishness. A more consistent interpretation is that they hold a prosocial norm that focuses on contributing and not on harming others, combined with a willingness to tolerate inequality rather than participate in costly sanctions.

They are willing to punish, but they do so selectively and with a distinctive profile: - Early on, they punish clear low contributors and non‑compliers, aligning with the group’s justice norm. This includes punishing two obvious deviators in the first round. - They also join group campaigns against a recurrent norm‑breaker: they punish the chronic zero‑contributor once, adding to the pile‑on. - Later, they punish an otherwise highly cooperative player who has just been randomly punished by the group’s “angry bird” narrative target, even though that player’s contributions have been consistently maximal. This choice is not driven by contribution levels or by direct harm from that player. Punishment is relatively infrequent compared to how often they use rewards, but when they do punish it is not purely deterrent: they sometimes side with group justice against norm violators, and other times they appear to “twist the knife” on a high contributor who has just been victimized, making that player worse off while the group is framing them as innocent. This indicates a willingness to exploit ambiguity in social narratives. They never use punishment to retaliate for punishment they themselves receive, despite being heavily and repeatedly sanctioned. They do not try to deter others from punishing them. That suggests they treat punishment more as an experimental tool and a lever on the group’s emotional dynamics than as self‑protective retaliation. In a new environment, expect them to punish occasionally, especially clear defectors or salient targets singled out by group talk, but not to be a consistent norm enforcer. They can also direct punishment in ways that seem arbitrary or strategically opaque from others’ perspective.

They are very willing to punish, including at substantial personal cost. Their early punishment targets the lowest contributor, consistent with enforcing a contribution norm. Once the “angry bird” (Parrot) emerges as a repeated low contributor and exploiter, they repeatedly punish it, including several high-intensity bursts that meaningfully reduce their own payoff. They explicitly express a motive of “justice,” talk about needing to “destroy” the deviant, and join coalition punishments when others propose coordinated hits. Deterrence and norm enforcement appear primary: they do not punish random players or high contributors, and they join group calls to sanction those who are seen as bots, trolls, or norm violators. However, their punishment of Owl at the very end is more suspicion- and frustration-driven than strictly payoff-based; Owl’s earlier behavior is mixed, and the trigger seems to be Owl suddenly doing very well combined with low contributions in a late round and other players calling them “sus.” That suggests they also punish to restore perceived social fairness, even with incomplete information. They do not appear to use punishment as retaliation for being punished themselves (they are almost never targeted) nor as strategic intimidation for future gains; instead they accept that punishing costs them “real money” and continue anyway, suggesting strong non-monetary preferences for norm enforcement and group justice. They occasionally talk about “democracy” and “justice” in the same breath, and they coordinate with others before punishing, but they are also willing to act independently when they feel a violator must be sanctioned.

This player is a willing but selective punisher. They never punish high contributors or norm-compliant players; all observed punishments are aimed at targets perceived as deviating from group norms or as “suspicious.” Their decisions to punish track two main triggers: 1. **Clear contribution deviations:** - Very low contributions from specific players (first an under-contributing owl, later repeatedly the parrot/bird and again owl near the end) reliably prompt them either to punish directly or to call for others to do so. - They sometimes verbally announce their intended target before or after punishing, which shows a norm-enforcing and communicative motivation: punishment is not secret retaliation but part of a coordinated enforcement effort. 2. **Perceived “meta” norm violations (bot/researcher behavior):** - Once the group frames the parrot as a bot or manipulative actor benefiting from mysterious extra payoffs and spending heavily on rewards, this player repeatedly supports and joins collective punishment against the parrot. - At times they punish even when they explicitly acknowledge boredom or non-instrumental motives (“bored, got the bird again”), consistent with a mix of entertainment, group bonding, and resentment at perceived unfairness rather than pure economic deterrence. They do not escalate to maximal self-sacrifice in punishment like some others: they use single punishment units at a time, spread across multiple rounds. This points to a controlled, “measured enforcer” style, willing to spend but not to self-immolate. They never use punishment vindictively in response to being punished themselves. After being heavily deducted in one early round, they do not retaliate at random; instead, they continue contributing fully and remain socially engaged. This indicates a norm-based rather than ego-based motive. Finally, their late-game targeting of owl—after noticing owl’s atypical payoffs and contribution pattern—shows they continue monitoring outcomes and adjusting punishment targets as new “suspicious” behavior appears. They are not stuck on one narrative; they update and extend enforcement as new anomalies arise.

They never use the punishment option, despite having many clear opportunities and strong group pressure to sanction a repeated low contributor and later to target other “suspect” players. This occurs even when punishment of low contributors becomes a central group activity and source of amusement, and even when punishment is relatively efficient (high impact per cost). Their abstention holds both early and late, suggesting a stable anti-punishment or at least strongly punishment-averse profile rather than simple inattention. They also do not threaten to punish or endorse punishment rhetorically; they neither join in the “justice” rhetoric nor echo calls to “kill” or bankrupt specific avatars. This pattern is consistent with a payoff-protecting, non-enforcer type: they prefer to conserve their own coins and let others bear the cost of norm enforcement, even when they themselves are committed cooperators.

They never purchase punishment, despite having many opportunities and strong social pressure to do so. This is robust: they refrain from punishing clear low contributors, do not join coordinated “justice” campaigns against the parrot, and do not retaliate when personally harmed by the parrot’s deductions. Even after being singled out and explicitly encouraged by others to “clap back” or “kill the bird,” they maintain zero punishment use. This indicates a strong reluctance to spend on negative sanctions, consistent with either a principled aversion to harming others, a payoff-maximizing preference against the high inefficiency of punishment, or simple adherence to their “keep it simple” heuristic (cooperate, don’t micromanage others). Their behavior is non-retaliatory: personal harm does not trigger sanctioning. They also never attempt “deterrence” punishment to enforce norms, even though the group’s main narrative is precisely about using punishment as justice. Their type is strongly non-punitive.

They never spend a single coin on punishment, despite seeing multiple rounds of heavy sanctioning behavior against low contributors and especially the anomalous bird. They also experience being punished at least once themselves, but this does not trigger any retaliatory behavior. This indicates very low willingness to engage in costly moral enforcement, even when fairness norms are clearly salient in the group. Their inaction is consistent across contexts: - They do not punish early low contributors when that behavior is first detected. - They do not join collective “mob punishment” episodes against the bird, even when nearly everyone else participates and the deterrence logic is explicit in chat. - They do not punish the players who hit them with deductions. This suggests they are neither a second-order enforcer nor a revenge-seeker. Their approach is more “let others handle justice while I focus on my own payoff and contributions.” They benefit indirectly from others’ punishments (which raise average contributions and discipline free-riders), but they never pay for that enforcement themselves. They also do not use punishment strategically to reshape outcomes near the end of the game, even when some players begin to slack on contributions or when suspicion falls on others (e.g., owl). They appear to treat punishment as never worth its cost, regardless of multiplier or social pressure.

They never use punishment, despite having many opportunities and multiple salient norms violations (low contributors, suspected bot behavior, and the parrot directly harming them). Across all rounds they spend zero on punishment. Given how salient punishment is in group chat—where others describe themselves as “police,” talk about “delivering justice,” and coordinate mass hits—this consistent non-use suggests a deliberate or strong disposition, not mere inattention. They see the same events (and even report who has been punished) but never join in. Their non-punishment cannot be explained by self-protection against retaliation, since punishers are anonymous. Nor is it driven by self-interest in all cases: some coordinated punishments would have increased their relative payoff versus the target. Ignoring both deterrence and expressive “justice” suggests either (a) a strong preference against burning coins even for norm-enforcement, or (b) a belief that the punishment mechanism is inefficient, noisy, or not worth engaging with, especially once they suspect non-human or “researcher” roles in the game. They also do not punish the parrot after being personally targeted, nor do they retaliate against any human co-players. This implies low propensity for revenge; they mostly absorb harm passively and continue cooperating. So their punishment “type” is: principled non-punisher / abstainer. They tolerate others’ heavy punishment use but choose not to participate, even when it would be emotionally or strategically natural.

They are willing to use punishment, but do so sparingly and in a targeted, norm-enforcing way. Punishment is almost exclusively directed at the persistently under-contributing “angry bird” player, and always when that player is clearly deviating from the group’s near-unanimous high-contribution norm. They participate in early collective punishment of that deviant, then briefly stop punishing when the group debates whether continued punishment is worthwhile or whether the deviant might be a bot. After being reminded (and after seeing the deviant’s payoff remain high), they return to punishing that same target. Their punishment is never used for personal retaliation (they do not punish anyone who punished them, and they do not punish randomly) and is not used against low-but-human-looking contributors once those contributors move toward the norm. They do not join the more extreme “empty my bank on justice” behavior; instead, they add a moderate, periodic contribution to the sanctioning, tolerating a pay cost but not sacrificing everything. They also never punish high contributors or cooperative players, even when those players receive suspiciously high payoffs, suggesting a clear internal boundary: punishment is for obvious free-riding or disruptive behavior, not for advantage per se. Their pattern indicates a conditional cooperator with modestly strong altruistic-punishment tendencies: they are willing to pay to enforce norms but keep the intensity bounded.

This player does not use the punishment mechanism at any point, despite punishment being relatively effective in terms of impact per unit cost. They consistently choose to spend nothing on punishment, even when clear deviations from cooperative behavior occur and when they themselves become a punishment target. This suggests a reluctance to incur costs to sanction others, consistent with a “non-punisher” or “forgiving by default” profile. They do not engage in norm-enforcing punishment, nor do they retaliate when they are punished. They appear to prioritize preserving their own earnings and/or avoiding escalation over actively shaping others’ behavior through costly sanctions. Their inaction in punishment, even when they could reasonably justify sanctioning low contributors or apparent aggressors, implies low inclination toward both deterrence and revenge. This might be due to skepticism about the effectiveness of anonymous punishment, discomfort with harming others even at low cost, or a general preference for letting the group dynamics play out without direct intervention. Based solely on behavior, their underlying motive for abstaining from punishment is unknown, but the pattern itself is clear and stable.

The player is willing to use punishment, but only sparingly and with apparent selectivity. They engage in costly punishment at least once, and the target choice is consistent with sanctioning behavior perceived as socially undesirable (low contribution or norm violation), rather than with arbitrary or spiteful use. Over the game, they do not retaliate in an obvious way despite themselves being repeatedly punished; their own punishment behavior does not escalate following being targeted. This pattern suggests a greater orientation toward norm enforcement or signaling disapproval than toward personal revenge. They also refrain from frequent or high-intensity punishment even when others’ contributions and punishment behavior would give excuses to do so, indicating a cautious or efficiency-minded stance: they do not appear eager to spend to harm others unless a threshold of unfairness is met. Their use of punishment seems more deterrent than expressive—trying to nudge behavior—though the short horizon and limited data make this inference low confidence.

This player is willing to pay nontrivial personal costs to punish and shows a relatively strong inclination toward using the punishment mechanism. Their punishment is clearly selective: they target players who contribute notably less than the group or appear to free ride, including very low or zero contributors. They do not appear to punish high contributors, and there is no sign of spiteful or anti-social punishment. This pattern is consistent with a norm-enforcing or “altruistic punisher” type, using costly sanctions to police fairness and discourage exploitation. They are willing to increase punishment intensity when a deviation from cooperative norms is large, indicating that magnitude of norm violation matters to them. There is no clear evidence that they punish in retaliation for being punished themselves; observed punishment choices are more straightforwardly explained by others’ contribution behavior rather than by payoffs deducted from them. Their use of punishment in the final round, when deterrence for future rounds is minimal, suggests that they may also be motivated by immediate fairness concerns or a desire for retributive justice rather than purely forward-looking deterrence. Overall, they function as a principled, contribution-based punisher rather than a vengeful or random one.

They do make use of costly punishment, but only intermittently and at low intensity. Across the game, they spend small amounts on punishment in a few rounds while abstaining in most rounds, even though chronic free‑riding is visible. This implies a willingness to sanction defectors but not a strong taste for frequent or escalating punishment. Given the timing—punishment episodes occur in periods where specific low contributors are salient in group chat, and where “all zeros” or named free‑riders are being discussed—it is likely they target clear, repeated defectors rather than punishing broadly; however, exact targets are unknown. Their punishment appears norm‑enforcing rather than retaliatory, since they are never personally deducted and yet still occasionally pay to punish. At the same time, they do not invest enough to fully deter persistent defectors, suggesting that they balance the norm motive against the personal cost of punishment and possible fatigue. They function as a light, supportive enforcer: willing to contribute to group sanctioning when there is a clear social signal to do so, but not inclined to lead a punitive campaign or to punish in the absence of group discussion.

This player never uses punishment despite repeated, clear opportunities and despite ongoing low contributions from certain peers. They also never complain about being punished, which is consistent with the fact that they are never targeted for deductions. Their refusal to punish holds even when other players explicitly call for sanctioning low contributors and discuss punishing them. They therefore appear to reject costly punishment as a tool, whether for retaliation or norm enforcement, even though doing so would be instrumentally justified in many standard PGG models. This suggests either (a) a strong preference for avoiding direct harm to others, (b) a belief that punishment is unnecessary or counterproductive to overall group cooperation, or (c) an expectation that others will handle enforcement if it happens at all. They rely entirely on communication and example‑setting to support norms, not on deterrence. Their type is thus “non‑punishing, principled cooperator”: they cooperate fully, accept the presence of free‑riders, and try to persuade rather than sanction, even in a setting where punishment is easy and socially endorsed by others.

This player never uses the punishment option at any point, despite repeated and visible free-riding by specific others and open calls in the chat to punish low contributors. They also never complain about being exploited, never argue for or against punishment, and never publicly endorse others’ punitive actions. From observed behavior, they function as a pure non-punishing cooperator. Their avoidance of punishment persists even when punishment is clearly being used by others as an enforcement tool and when the cost/impact structure might make targeted punishment instrumentally reasonable. This suggests one or more of the following (each with low to moderate confidence, as motives are not verbalized): a personal reluctance to harm others even at a gain to the group, a focus on their own “do the right thing” rule rather than on enforcing rules on others, or a perception that punishment is not worth the cost or hassle. They also never retaliate—no one visibly punishes them, and they do not use punishment to respond to any outcome, so there is no evidence for a retaliatory disposition. Their pattern is closer to “non-enforcing altruist”: they contribute fully but abstain from sanctioning, even when sanctioned enforcement by others is publicly discussed. Deterrence and norm-policing do not appear in their behavior. They rely entirely on their own unilateral cooperation and, behaviorally, they leave enforcement to others.

This player is a frequent and often heavy user of costly punishment, targeting low contributors and especially those perceived as deceptive. They spend punishment resources in a large majority of rounds where there are clear free‑riders in the contribution profile, and they continue doing so even when it significantly reduces their own round payoffs. Their willingness to incur substantial personal costs, without any guarantee of identification or reciprocation, indicates a strong preference for enforcing contribution norms rather than maximizing short‑term earnings. They coordinate implicitly with other punishers: when others suggest punishing all zero contributors, they immediately endorse the idea and follow through behaviorally. Punishment magnitude varies, suggesting some selectivity rather than random lashing out. The explicit chat reference linking a specific player’s lie to a specific total deduction implies they sometimes calibrate sanctions to the perceived severity of norm violation (lying vs merely contributing zero). There is no evidence they punish high contributors or random players; all punishable targets visible in the data are those contributing nothing or repeatedly deviating, with particular focus on a few chronic low contributors. Their punishment appears primarily deterrent and norm‑enforcing rather than retaliatory or spite‑based: they are never punished themselves, so they are not responding to harm directed at them. They keep punishing late into the game, even while others complain about boredom or consider quitting, implying that adherence to cooperative rules and honesty matters to them independent of how “serious” others now take the game. At a personality level, they fit the profile of a “moral enforcer”: a high contributor who is also willing to spend aggressively on sanctions to uphold fairness and punish deception, even at a personal cost and without deriving obvious expressive satisfaction in chat.

This player never spends any coins on punishment, despite persistent under‑contribution by some others and despite a norm of sanctioning zero contributors that several players adopt and discuss. Even when free‑riding increases and punishment of defectors is openly coordinated in the chat, they abstain from participating. This holds both when they are contributing fully (and would have standard altruistic‑punisher motives available) and when they themselves are low‑contributing (in which case strategic or retaliatory punishment might be expected). Their consistent non‑use suggests either a principled aversion to costly sanctions, a strong focus on maximizing their own payoff, or simple inertia and low engagement with the punishment stage. When they themselves are punished after free‑riding, they do not comment, complain, or signal any resentment. They neither retaliate nor attempt to negotiate or justify their behavior. Instead, they quietly adjust their contribution back toward the cooperative norm in subsequent play, but still without enforcing that norm on others. This behavior is more consistent with someone who accepts punishment as “part of the game” or not worth engaging over, rather than someone who is concerned with fairness in the sense of “wrongdoing must be sanctioned.” Functionally, their stance is: contribute a lot, rarely defect, but almost never invest in enforcement. They rely on others to bear the cost of punishing free‑riders and benefit from that enforcement when it increases group contributions, yet they do not reciprocate by punishing themselves. There is no evidence of deterrence‑oriented or norm‑expressive punishment on their part, and no observable retaliatory impulse. Their punishment type is best described as a non‑punishing, norm‑conforming opportunist: they enjoy cooperative gains, occasionally exploit the group, accept being sanctioned, and avoid the costs—material and social—of active enforcement.

They are generally reluctant to punish and do not treat punishment as a routine tool; for much of the game they spend nothing on deductions despite repeated free‑riding by others. When they do move toward punishment, it is framed as a group‑sanctioning strategy (“start punishing?”, “all zeros minused from here”), suggesting they see punishment mainly as a coordinated deterrent against persistent zero‑contributors rather than as personal revenge. Their actual spending on punishment is limited and concentrated late in the game, and it appears targeted at clear free‑riders rather than used indiscriminately, implying selective use based on blatant norm violation. They never retaliate for harm done to themselves (they are never punished) and show no interest in using punishment to settle interpersonal scores; the motive is enforcement of the contribution norm and protection of group welfare. Even as others grow cynical and some begin contributing zero, they keep their own contributions at maximum and only modestly increase punishment, indicating that while they do accept costly sanctioning in principle, they prioritize maintaining their own cooperative identity over escalating punitive behavior. Overall, they are a norm‑enforcing altruist: willing to punish defectors when free‑riding is obvious and repeated, but using punishment sparingly, in a measured and group‑oriented way rather than aggressively.

They are generally restrained in using punishment, but not categorically against it. For most of the game they impose no deductions at all, despite repeated opportunities and visible free‑riding. This indicates either a preference to avoid the cost of punishment, a hope that social pressure and group norms suffice, or mild ambiguity about the effectiveness of punishment. However, at one point they engage in a sizable punishment episode, spending a nontrivial amount of their own payoff to sanction others. This use of punishment coincides with ongoing, clearly observable non‑contribution by specific players, and follows extensive group discussion of which players are contributing zero. The timing and scale suggest a norm‑enforcement motive rather than retaliation: they have not been personally targeted with deductions, and there is no evidence of reprisal. Instead, they appear to punish in response to persistent, willful free‑riding and perceived refusal to “do something simple” like always contributing max. After this episode, they return to not punishing, even though non‑contribution continues to appear. This pattern suggests they treat punishment as an occasional, costly last resort rather than a routine tool. They do not openly coordinate punishment strategies in chat, but they express disapproval of defectors in words, which acts as a softer form of enforcement. Overall, they are a principled cooperator who is willing to bear substantial cost to punish clear norm violators once, but generally prefers to maintain cooperation and ride out the game rather than escalate.

This player never uses the punishment mechanism, even when another participant contributes less than the full amount. Their willingness to punish appears effectively zero in practice, both when all others fully cooperate and when one peer slightly under-contributes. Because they never impose any sanctions, it is unclear whether they endorse punishment in principle; what can be inferred is that modest deviations from full cooperation are not sufficient to trigger costly punishment for them. They also never engage in antisocial punishment; they do not punish cooperators, and they do not appear to use punishment strategically to reshape behavior or to retaliate. Given that they consistently contribute fully yet refrain from punishing lesser contributors, they likely prioritize maintaining efficiency and keeping their own payoff intact over spending resources to enforce norms. Alternatively, they may hold a non-punitive, tolerant norm where others’ choices are left to them as long as the group outcome remains high. The data do not distinguish clearly between “reluctant enforcer” and “principled non-punisher,” but the absence of punishment across all contexts suggests a stable, low-punishment type with no observable deterrence or revenge motives.

This player never uses the punishment option, even when another participant lowers their contribution below the prevailing group norm. They do not spend any resources to sanction under-contributors, and they are never the target of punishment themselves. Their non-use of punishment is consistent and unconditional, showing no sensitivity to small deviations in others’ behavior. This pattern suggests either (a) an intrinsic aversion to harming others or to engaging with punitive mechanisms, even when those mechanisms are cost-effective, or (b) a belief that punishment is unnecessary in a generally cooperative environment. They may implicitly adopt a “cooperate but don’t enforce” stance, relying on social norms and others’ intrinsic cooperation rather than active deterrence. There is no evidence of retaliatory motives, norm-enforcing behavior, or strategic use of punishment to stabilize cooperation. They do not appear to use punishment instrumentally to shape others’ behavior, nor do they test the mechanism even once, which points to a stable preference against punitive interventions rather than mere inattention. Their type is therefore best characterized as a non-punitive, principled cooperator: they contribute maximally but decline to enforce norms through costly sanctions, even when mild free-riding emerges.

This player is almost completely non-punitive. They use the punishment mechanism once at the very beginning, then never again. After that initial instance, they abstain from punishing even when a group member contributes somewhat less than the rest. Their pattern indicates that they do not adopt punishment as an ongoing tool to manage group behavior. Their single use of punishment occurs when one peer contributes clearly less than most others in an otherwise highly cooperative group. This suggests that their trigger, when it exists, is asymmetric under-contribution relative to a high-cooperation norm. However, they do not follow up when a later, milder deviation occurs, implying that either their threshold for acting is quite high, or that they quickly decide that punishment is unnecessary or not worth the cost in this environment. They are highly selective: they do not punish when everyone cooperates, and they do not punish small deviations once the group’s general cooperative pattern is established. This selectivity, along with the absence of any counter-punishment against them, means their punishment decisions are not driven by retaliation. There is no evidence they use punishment to compete, equalize payoffs, or express spite. Their motives appear, at most, weakly deterrent and very short-lived: they may initially be willing to signal that low contributions are unacceptable, but once the group stabilizes at full cooperation, they avoid incurring any further punishment costs. They behave as if punishment is a last-resort tool to correct stark norm violations, not a standing enforcement strategy. In summary, their punishment “type” is a minimally interventionist, almost entirely non-punishing cooperator who may deliver a rare, targeted sanction against salient free-riding but otherwise refrains from using costly punishment, relying instead on cooperative norms and others’ intrinsic willingness to contribute.

The player is generally non-punitive and uses punishment only once, in a selective and seemingly norm-enforcing way. For the vast majority of the game, including periods when they themselves are punished and lose payoff, they refrain from imposing any deductions on others. This indicates a high threshold for intervention: they do not treat punishment as a routine tool, and they do not respond in kind to personal sanctions. Their single use of punishment coincides with a clear, albeit small, deviation from an otherwise unanimous maximum-contribution norm by another player. This suggests that their trigger is norm violation rather than personal grievance. Because punishers are anonymous, retaliation against a specific prior punisher is difficult to target, and there is no observable pattern of them attempting to do so. The timing and the focus on a lone undercontributor are more consistent with deterrence and maintenance of group standards than with revenge. The level of punishment they do use is minimal compared to what is technologically possible, which indicates restraint. They appear to aim to send a corrective signal rather than to inflict maximal loss. Given that they then return to not punishing at all, and the group returns to unanimous maximum contributions, it is plausible (though not certain) that they see punishment as a last-resort measure to protect a productive cooperative equilibrium, not as a core part of their strategy. Overall, their punishment style is “reluctant norm enforcement”: they strongly prefer to sustain cooperation without sanctions, tolerate even substantial personal losses without retaliating, but are willing to spend a small amount to discipline a visible deviation from a well-established cooperative standard when they judge that group efficiency might otherwise be at risk.

This player never uses the punishment mechanism. Across the entire interaction, including when one group member contributes less than the rest, they impose no sanctions on anyone. They also receive no punishment themselves, which is consistent with their consistently cooperative behavior but does not by itself explain their non-use of punishment. Their non-punishing pattern suggests one or more of the following (with uncertainty where appropriate): they may be averse to harming others, even at low personal cost; they may see punishment as unnecessary in a highly cooperative group; or they may be uncertain about whether using punishment is normatively appropriate, especially when deviations are small. They do not engage in targeted discipline against a moderate under-contributor and do not escalate in response to that deviation, indicating that they are not motivated by norm enforcement when cooperation levels remain high overall. The player therefore fits a “non-punishing cooperator” profile. They prioritize contributing over enforcing, place little weight on retaliation or deterrence, and appear reluctant to trade even a small amount of their own payoff for the sake of reducing another’s payoff. Their focus is on positive contribution rather than on controlling others’ behavior through sanctions.

This player is a very restrained but norm‑enforcing punisher. They almost never spend on punishment and only do so when another player falls clearly below an otherwise unanimous high‑contribution pattern. When everyone cooperates fully, they impose no sanctions at all. When one peer deviates downward, they become willing to pay a personal cost to impose a substantial deduction, then immediately revert to non‑punishment once cooperation is restored. Their punishment appears targeted at deviation from a shared full‑contribution norm, not at personal grievances or payoff envy. They do not use punishment opportunistically when everyone cooperates, nor do they escalate after their single use of sanctions. This pattern suggests a primary motive of norm enforcement and deterrence: they are prepared to “send a signal” against free‑riding but have no interest in repeated or vindictive punishment. They are selective: mild or one‑off deviations are enough to trigger at least some sanction, but they do not over‑invest in it. They spend just enough to create a nontrivial penalty, indicating a willingness to bear some cost for group discipline while still conserving their own payoff once the norm is re‑established. There is no evidence of antisocial punishment: they never punish high contributors or use punishment when the group is fully cooperating. Their general style is cooperative‑first, with punishment as a backup tool to protect a high‑contribution equilibrium, not as a routine instrument of control.
